name: COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Socket and Spigot Joint
topics.content:
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Socket and Spigot Joint A **Socket and Spigot Joint** is a classical *detachable pipe joint* widely used in **cast iron (CI) pipelines**, plumbing networks, and underground water distribution systems. It consists of two mating components: - **Socket (Female End):** A flared, bell-shaped enlarged end cast integrally with one pipe section. It has an internal cavity that receives the spigot. - **Spigot (Male End):** A plain, cylindrical, straight end of the adjoining pipe section that is inserted into the socket. The sealing and rigidity of the joint are achieved by packing the annular gap between the socket and spigot with **lead (Pb)** or **cement mortar** (1:2 cement-sand), followed by caulking (mechanical compaction). In the context of **PCMUL507 – CAD & Analysis Lab**, this experiment demands mastery of **bottom-up assembly modelling**, application of **geometric mating constraints**, and generation of **production-ready engineering drawings** (orthographic part drawings + assembly drawings) as per **IS 696 (Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing)** and **ASME Y14.5** conventions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This experiment primarily satisfies **CO2 – Create parametric part models and assemblies using CAD tools**, and **CO3 – Generate engineering drawings with proper dimensioning, tolerancing, and bill of materials.** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine the joint between two **cylindrical thermocol rods**, where one end of the first rod has a hollow cavity scooped out (like the cup of an acorn). The second rod's straight end slides snugly into this cavity. To stop the joint from leaking water, you pour molten wax into the gap, let it solidify, and then press it tight with a blunt tool. That, in essence, is a **socket and spigot joint**. In CAD terms: - The **socket** = a parent part with a *revolve cut* or *extrude cut* feature forming an inner cylindrical recess. - The **spigot** = a child component whose outer diameter is *slightly smaller* than the socket's inner diameter (the clearance accommodates the packing material). - The **assembly** = bringing both parts together with **mate**, **coincident**, and **concentric** constraints inside a CAD environment such as **SolidWorks**, **CATIA V5**, **Creo Parametric**, or **Siemens NX**. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Labs:** > - It trains students in **multi-part assembly** (a step above single-part modelling). > - It introduces **interference fit, clearance fit, and packing allowances**. > - It teaches **sectional views** — essential because internal joints are hidden features. > - It builds the foundation for **GD&T** and **BOM (Bill of Materials)** generation. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value / Standard | |---|---| | Spigot outer diameter | **D** (nominal pipe bore) | | Socket inner diameter | **D + 2t**, where **t = 6 mm to 12 mm** packing space | | Socket depth (length of insertion) | **L = 75 mm to 100 mm** for typical DN 100–DN 300 CI pipes | | Working pressure | **0.7 MPa to 1.0 MPa** (gravity flow systems) | | Applicable code (India) | **IS 1536 / IS 1729** for CI pipes | | Applicable code (International) | **ASME B16.4**, **BS 437** | | CAD Modelling Standard | **IS 696**, **ASME Y14.5-2018** | | Drafting Sheet Size | **A2 (594 × 420 mm)** for assembly, **A3 (420 × 297 mm)** for part | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Decomposition of the Joint The socket and spigot joint serves three engineering functions simultaneously: 1. **Mechanical Locking** — prevents axial separation under internal pressure and ground movement. 2. **Fluid Sealing** — the packed annular space must be leak-proof up to rated pressure. 3. **Angular Deflection Tolerance** — modern flexible socket joints permit **3° to 5°** deflection without leakage (critical for soil settlement). ### 2.2 Component-by-Component Theoretical Breakdown #### 2.2.1 The SOCKET (Female Component) - Geometry: A *cylindrical shell* with an enlarged bell at one end. - Wall thickness: typically **8 mm to 15 mm** depending on diameter. - Manufacturing process: *Centrifugal casting* (for CI) or *sand casting*. - CAD feature tree: ``` Base Revolve (outer profile) └─ Revolve Cut (inner bore + bell cavity) └─ Fillet (outer corner of bell, R = 3–5 mm) └─ Chamfer (inner lip, 2 mm × 45°) ``` #### 2.2.2 The SPIGOT (Male Component) - Geometry: Plain cylindrical extension on a pipe section. - Length of insertion: **L = 75 mm to 100 mm**. - Surface finish: smooth-turned to ensure uniform packing thickness. - CAD feature: simple *Extrude Boss* with a *Chamfer* at the leading edge to ease insertion. #### 2.2.3 The PACKING (Sealing Element) - **Lead (Pb) packing:** poured molten, caulked, height ≈ **25 mm** above socket face. - **Cement mortar packing:** 1:2 mix, rammed in layers of **10 mm**. - **Rubber gasket (modern variant):** confined within the socket groove. ### 2.3 Assembly Logic — How the Joint Works The CAD assembly workflow mirrors the real-world jointing procedure: | Step | Real-World Action | CAD Equivalent | |---|---|---| | 1 | Clean the spigot and socket | Verify no interference in assembly | | 2 | Insert spigot into socket | Apply **Coincident** + **Concentric** mates | | 3 | Wrap yarn gasket around spigot | (Conceptual — represented by clearance gap) | | 4 | Pour lead/caulk | (Represented by cross-hatched section view) | | 5 | Verify alignment | Check **interference detection** tool | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Rule | Description | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D_{socket,inner} = D_{spigot,outer} + 2t_{packing}$ | Packing thickness allowance | mm | | 2 | $L_{spigot} = (0.75 \text{ to } 1.0) \cdot D_{nominal}$ | Spigot insertion length rule | mm | | 3 | $V_{packing} = \pi \cdot t_{packing} \cdot (D_{spigot} + t_{packing}) \cdot L_{socket}$ | Volume of lead/mortar needed | mm³ | | 4 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2t}$ | Hoop stress in socket wall | N/mm² | | 5 | $\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4t}$ | Axial stress in pipe wall | N/mm² | | 6 | $M_{caulking} = k \cdot \sigma_{y,lead} \cdot A_{packing}$ | Torque-equivalent for lead compaction | N·mm | | 7 | $T_{drafting} = \pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$ for $D \leq 100$ mm | General dimensional tolerance | mm | | 8 | $Ra \leq 3.2 \, \mu m$ | Spigot surface finish requirement | µm | | 9 | $\theta_{deflection,max} \leq 5°$ | Angular flexibility limit | degrees | | 10 | $\text{SF}_{joint} = \dfrac{\sigma_{burst}}{\sigma_{hoop}}$ | Safety factor against bursting | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The hoop stress equation $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ is the same equation used in pressure vessel design (Thin Cylinder Theory). The socket wall is essentially a short pressure vessel segment, so students who learn this joint also learn the foundations of pressure vessel code design. ### 2.5 Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T) Considerations For a KTU lab viva, students are expected to apply: - **Dimensional tolerances:** $\pm 0.2$ mm on spigot OD, $\pm 0.3$ mm on socket ID. - **Geometric tolerances:** Cylindricity, concentricity, perpendicularity on mating faces. - **Surface finish symbols:** $\nabla\nabla$ on spigot sealing surface. - **Datum reference frame:** Typically **A** = spigot axis, **B** = socket face. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Packing Volume Formula **Problem:** A DN 150 cast iron socket and spigot joint has a spigot OD = **168 mm**, socket ID = **180 mm**, and socket depth = **100 mm**. Determine the volume of lead required to pack the joint completely. #### Step 1: Identify the Geometry of the Packing Region The packing occupies an **annular cylindrical volume** between the spigot outer surface and the socket inner surface, of length equal to the socket depth. $$ \text{Annulus cross-section area} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{socket}^2 - D_{spigot}^2\right) $$ #### Step 2: Substitute the Dimensions $$ \begin{aligned} A_{annulus} &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(180^2 - 168^2\right) \, \text{mm}^2 \\ &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(32400 - 28224\right) \\ &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(4176\right) \\ &= 3280.46 \, \text{mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ The packing thickness on each side is: $$ t = \dfrac{180 - 168}{2} = 6 \, \text{mm} $$ #### Step 3: Multiply Annulus Area by Socket Depth $$ \begin{aligned} V_{packing} &= A_{annulus} \times L_{socket} \\ &= 3280.46 \times 100 \\ &= 328046 \, \text{mm}^3 \\ &\approx 328.05 \times 10^3 \, \text{mm}^3 \\ &= 328.05 \, \text{cm}^3 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4: Convert to Mass of Lead Density of lead $\rho_{Pb} = 11.34 \, \text{g/cm}^3$. $$ \begin{aligned} m_{Pb} &= V_{packing} \times \rho_{Pb} \\ &= 328.05 \times 11.34 \\ &= 3720.1 \, \text{g} \\ &\approx 3.72 \, \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Approximately **3.72 kg of lead** is required to seal one DN 150 socket and spigot joint. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Hoop Stress in the Socket Wall **Problem:** A DN 200 socket is subjected to internal water pressure $p = 0.7 \, \text{MPa}$. The socket wall thickness is $t = 12 \, \text{mm}$ and nominal bore $D = 200 \, \text{mm}$. Find the hoop stress and check the safety factor. #### Step 1: Verify the Thin-Wall Assumption The thin-wall approximation is valid when $D/t \geq 20$. $$ \frac{D}{t} = \frac{200}{12} = 16.67 $$ This is **borderline thin-wall**. For better accuracy, we use the *Lamé equation* for thick cylinders, but for KTU-level problems, the thin-wall approximation is acceptable. #### Step 2: Apply the Thin-Wall Hoop Stress Equation $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{hoop} &= \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2t} \\ &= \dfrac{0.7 \times 200}{2 \times 12} \\ &= \dfrac{140}{24} \\ &= 5.833 \, \text{N/mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3: Check the Safety Factor For cast iron, ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{u} \approx 200 \, \text{N/mm}^2$, yield strength $\sigma_{y} \approx 120 \, \text{N/mm}^2$. $$ \begin{aligned} SF &= \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{hoop}} \\ &= \dfrac{120}{5.833} \\ &= 20.57 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The socket has a safety factor of **20.57**, which is far above the code requirement of **2.5 to 4.0** for pressure piping. This justifies the use of CI sockets for low-pressure municipal water lines. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Joint Geometry Variations | Parameter | Small Diameter (DN 80) | Medium Diameter (DN 150) | Large Diameter (DN 300) | |---|---|---|---| | Spigot OD | 92 mm | 168 mm | 332 mm | | Socket ID | 100 mm | 180 mm | 350 mm | | Packing thickness $t$ | 4 mm | 6 mm | 9 mm | | Socket depth $L$ | 75 mm | 100 mm | 125 mm | | Lead required | ≈ 0.55 kg | ≈ 3.72 kg | ≈ 14.1 kg | | Test pressure | 1.5 MPa | 1.0 MPa | 0.7 MPa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** As diameter increases, packing volume grows **quadratically** while hoop stress grows **linearly**. This is why large-diameter joints use rubber gaskets instead of lead — labor economics. --- ### 3.4 Assembly Constraint Matrix The CAD assembly uses these primary geometric relations: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Constraint 1: Concentricity} \quad & \text{Axis}_{spigot} \equiv \text{Axis}_{socket} \\ \text{Constraint 2: Coincidence (face)} \quad & \text{Plane}_{spigot\_end} \equiv \text{Plane}_{socket\_bottom} \\ \text{Constraint 3: Distance offset} \quad & \text{Distance} = L_{insertion} - t_{clearance} \\ \text{Constraint 4: Angle (optional)} \quad & \theta_{deflection} = 0° \text{ to } 5° \end{aligned} $$ In SolidWorks, these correspond to: **Concentric**, **Coincident**, **Distance**, and **Angle** mates — all available under the *Mechanical Mates* toolbar. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — CAD Modelling Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Experiment 2] --> B[Open CAD Software
SolidWorks or CATIA] B --> C[Step 1: Create Part 1
Socket Component] C --> C1[Revolve Base
Outer Profile] C1 --> C2[Revolve Cut
Inner Bell Cavity] C2 --> C3[Apply Fillets
R equals 3 to 5 mm] C3 --> D[Step 2: Create Part 2
Spigot Pipe Section] D --> D1[Extrude Boss
Cylindrical Pipe] D1 --> D2[Add Chamfer
2 mm at leading edge] D2 --> E[Step 3: Create Part 3
Second Pipe Section] E --> F[Step 4: New Assembly File
ASM extension] F --> F1[Insert Socket Part
Fixed Component] F1 --> F2[Insert Spigot Pipe
Apply Concentric Mate] F2 --> F3[Apply Coincident Mate
Axial Insertion] F3 --> F4[Insert Second Pipe
Mate to Other End] F4 --> G[Step 5: Run Interference Check
Verify No Collision] G --> H[Step 6: Generate Drawing File
DRW extension] H --> H1[Standard 3 Orthographic Views
Front Top and Side] H1 --> H2[Sectional View
Showing Packing Region] H2 --> H3[Exploded View
For Assembly Clarity] H3 --> H4[Apply Dimensions
Per IS 696] H4 --> H5[Add GD&T and
Surface Finish Symbols] H5 --> H6[Generate BOM
Bill of Materials Table] H6 --> I[Step 7: Title Block
Name Roll No and Scale] I --> J[Plot to PDF
Submit for Valuation] J --> K[End of Experiment] ``` ### 4.2 Joint Cross-Sectional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR SP[Spigot Pipe
Outer Diameter D] -->|insertion length L| SO[Socket Bell
Inner Cavity] SO --> PA[Annular Packing
Region thickness t] PA --> YN[Yarn Gasket
Inner Layer] PA --> LD[Lead or Cement
Mortar Outer Layer] LD --> CC[Caulking Surface
Compacted] SP -.->|axial force F| SL[Self Weight and
Internal Pressure Load] SL --> GR[Ground Reaction
and Soil Friction] ``` ### 4.3 Drawing Sheet Layout Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD DR[Drawing Sheet A2] --> ZA[Zone A Part Drawing
3 Views plus Section] DR --> ZB[Zone B Assembly Drawing
Sectional Front View] DR --> ZC[Zone C Title Block
Bottom Right Corner] DR --> ZD[Zone D BOM Table
Above Title Block] DR --> ZE[Zone E Revision History
Bottom Left Corner] DR --> ZF[Zone F Notes and
Tolerances Block] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why these diagrams?** Socket and spigot joints are inherently *internal features* that cannot be visualized from outside. A CAD block diagram clarifies the *workflow* that students must follow in the lab — which is what examiners expect when they grade a student's submitted drawing. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCMUL507]` **Q: List the three primary geometric mates used in the CAD assembly of a socket and spigot joint. State one real-world engineering function each mate represents.** **Model Answer:** | # | CAD Mate | Real-World Function Represented | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Concentric** | Ensures the spigot and socket share a common central axis, replicating the symmetry of pipe alignment | | 2 | **Coincident** | Defines the axial insertion depth — equivalent to the worker pushing the spigot into the socket until it bottoms out | | 3 | **Distance / Offset** | Represents the packing thickness allowance between spigot OD and socket ID | These three mates collectively enforce the *concentricity*, *position*, and *fit clearance* of the joint — the three geometric prerequisites for any leak-proof pipe joint. **(3 marks)** --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMUL507]` **Q: Differentiate between a part drawing and an assembly drawing in the context of this experiment.** **Model Answer:** | Attribute | Part Drawing | Assembly Drawing | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Shows complete geometry of a single component | Shows how multiple parts fit together | | **Views shown** | All orthographic + necessary sections | Usually one sectional view + sometimes exploded view | | **Dimensions** | Every feature dimensioned | Only overall dimensions + key interface dimensions | | **Item/Balloon numbers** | Not used | Used to identify parts in BOM | | **BOM** | Not included | Included in title block area | | **Example in this experiment** | Socket part drawing OR Spigot part drawing | Combined socket + spigot + packing assembly | **Marking Tip:** The student must mention **sectional view** — it is essential because internal packing cannot be seen otherwise. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Question – Module 2 Internal Choice A]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a sectional front view, describe the constructional features of a socket and spigot joint used in a cast iron pipeline of DN 150. Label all critical dimensions and state the function of the lead packing. **(b) [7 Marks]** Outline the step-by-step procedure you would follow in **SolidWorks** to (i) model the socket and the spigot as two separate part files, and (ii) assemble them with appropriate mates. Mention any two drafting standards you would follow while generating the drawing. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Constructional Features (7 marks):** The DN 150 socket and spigot joint consists of: 1. **Socket (bell end)** — integrally cast with one pipe; outer profile tapers outward over a length of ~80 mm forming a flared bell. Inner cavity diameter = 168 mm to receive the spigot. **(1 mark)** 2. **Spigot (male end)** — plain cylindrical extension on the adjoining pipe, outer diameter = 150 mm (nominal), length of insertion = 100 mm, with a 2 mm chamfer at the leading edge. **(1 mark)** 3. **Annular packing region** — gap of 9 mm radial (socket ID − spigot OD = 168 − 150 = 18 mm, divided by 2 = 9 mm). **(1 mark)** 4. **Yarn/hemp gasket** — packed at the base of the socket to prevent lead from flowing into the pipe interior. **(1 mark)** 5. **Lead packing** — molten lead poured into the remaining annular space to a height of ~25 mm above the socket face. Function: provides a **compressible, corrosion-resistant, and self-sealing** seal under internal water pressure. **(1 mark)** 6. **Caulking surface** — lead is compacted with a caulking tool and finished flush with the socket face. **(1 mark)** 7. **Reference codes:** **IS 1536** for CI socket pipes, **IS 696** for drafting conventions. **(1 mark)** --- **Part (b) — CAD Modelling Procedure (7 marks):** **Step 1: Create the Spigot Part (2 marks)** - Open SolidWorks → New → Part. - Select *Front Plane* → Sketch a circle of diameter **150 mm** with its centre at the origin. - Apply *Extruded Boss* feature → depth = **600 mm** (typical pipe length). - Add a 2 mm × 45° chamfer at the spigot end using *Chamfer* feature. - Save as `Spigot_Pipe_DN150.SLDPRT`. **Step 2: Create the Socket Part (2 marks)** - New Part file. Sketch the **revolve profile** on Front Plane: - A horizontal line representing the pipe axis (length 600 mm). - A small outward step at the right end of height ~12 mm (socket wall thickness). - A vertical line up of ~80 mm (socket flared length). - A small inward step of ~6 mm (top of bell). - Apply *Revolved Boss* feature. - Create the inner cavity: sketch a profile on Front Plane showing the inner bore of 168 mm diameter and bell cavity depth of 100 mm. - Apply *Revolved Cut*. - Apply *Fillet* of R = 3 mm at the bell outer corner. - Save as `Socket_Pipe_DN150.SLDPRT`. **Step 3: Assemble (2 marks)** - New → Assembly. Insert both part files. - Fix the Socket (it becomes the *ground component*). - Select the spigot's cylindrical face and the socket's inner cylindrical face → apply **Concentric Mate**. - Select the spigot's end face and the socket's bottom face → apply **Coincident Mate** with an offset of (insertion length) = 100 mm. - Save assembly as `Socket_Spigot_Assembly.SLDASM`. **Step 4: Drafting (1 mark)** - Create Drawing from Assembly → select **A2 sheet**. - Place *Standard 3 View* of the assembly. - Insert a *Section View* (full section through pipe axis) showing the packing. - Apply dimensions as per **IS 696** and tolerances per **ASME Y14.5**. - Add Title Block with student details, BOM (2 line items), and revision history. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Question – Module 2 Internal Choice B]` **(a) [7 Marks]** A DN 200 cast iron socket and spigot joint has socket internal diameter = 222 mm, spigot outer diameter = 200 mm, and socket depth = 110 mm. Calculate: (i) the radial packing thickness, (ii) the volume of lead packing required, and (iii) the mass of lead in kg. Take density of lead = 11.34 g/cm³. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch how a sectional view differs from an outside view in communicating the construction of a socket and spigot joint. Why is a sectional view mandatory in the drawing submitted for this experiment? --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Numerical (7 marks):** (i) **Radial packing thickness** (1 mark): $$ t = \dfrac{D_{socket} - D_{spigot}}{2} = \dfrac{222 - 200}{2} = 11 \, \text{mm} $$ (ii) **Volume of lead packing** (4 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} A_{annulus} &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{socket}^2 - D_{spigot}^2\right) \\ &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(222^2 - 200^2\right) \\ &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(49284 - 40000\right) \\ &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}(9284) \\ &= 7291.1 \, \text{mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ V_{packing} = A_{annulus} \times L_{socket} = 7291.1 \times 110 = 802022 \, \text{mm}^3 = 802.02 \, \text{cm}^3 $$ (iii) **Mass of lead** (2 marks): $$ m_{Pb} = V \times \rho = 802.02 \times 11.34 = 9095.3 \, \text{g} \approx 9.10 \, \text{kg} $$ --- **Part (b) — Sketch and Explanation (7 marks):** An **outside view** (orthographic external view) shows only the outer contour of the assembled socket and spigot joint — i.e., the external profile of the socket bell and the spigot pipe. The packing region, being an *internal feature*, is completely **invisible**. The student would only see a smooth contour with no indication of how the seal is achieved. **(2 marks)** A **sectional view** is generated by passing a *cutting plane* through the assembly along the pipe axis, and showing the exposed cross-section. Hatching (section lining) is applied to the cut solid material — cast iron and lead in this case — at **45° to the axis with line spacing of 3 mm** (per IS 696). **(2 marks)** Key information revealed in the section: | Feature | Visible in Section? | Visible in Outside View? | |---|---|---| | Spigot OD | Yes | Yes (only end face) | | Socket inner cavity | **Yes** | No | | Yarn gasket position | **Yes** | No | | Lead packing thickness | **Yes** | No | | Caulking recess | **Yes** | No | | Inner bore of pipe | **Yes** | No | **(2 marks)** **Why sectional view is mandatory:** Because the functional performance of the joint depends entirely on internal packing dimensions, and an outside view cannot communicate these. The KTU valuation key explicitly awards 2 marks for a correctly drawn section. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Experiment:** > 1. **No Section View Drawn (–3 marks):** The single biggest error. A plain outside view without section is considered *incomplete* — the packing region is invisible. > 2. **Missing Mates in Assembly (–2 marks):** Students often insert parts without applying *Concentric* and *Coincident* mates. The assembly must be *fully constrained*. > 3. **No BOM in Title Block (–1 mark):** Even with two parts only, the BOM must list each item with quantity and material. > 4. **Wrong Hatching Pattern (–1 mark):** Cast iron and lead must be hatched with *different* patterns. Using one common hatch for all materials is a common error. > 5. **Dimensions Without Tolerances (–1 mark):** KTU expects at least one tolerance callout (e.g., spigot OD = $150 \pm 0.2$ mm). > 6. **Drawing Sheet Size Wrong (–1 mark):** Using A4 instead of A2 for assembly is a violation of IS 696. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] The **socket and spigot joint** is a *detachable*, *gravity-friendly*, *low-pressure* pipe joint used primarily in CI water pipelines. - [x] The **packing thickness** $t = \dfrac{D_{socket,ID} - D_{spigot,OD}}{2}$ — typically **6 mm to 12 mm**. - [x] The **socket depth** $L$ ≈ **75–125 mm** depending on nominal diameter. - [x] The **packing volume** is computed as $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_s^2 - D_p^2) \cdot L$. - [x] The **mass of lead** $m = V \times 11.34 \, \text{g/cm}^3$. - [x] CAD assembly requires **Concentric + Coincident mates** as a minimum. - [x] A **sectional front view** is *non-negotiable* in the submitted drawing. - [x] Drawing must follow **IS 696** conventions and **ASME Y14.5** GD&T rules. - [x] The **BOM** must include part name, part number, material, and quantity. - [x] The **exploded view** is *optional* but highly recommended for full marks. - [x] Hoop stress in socket wall: $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ — Thin Cylinder Theory applies. - [x] Safety factor for CI sockets typically exceeds **15** for low-pressure applications. - [x] **Hatching convention:** CI parts at 45°, lead packing at 135° (or vice-versa) to distinguish materials. - [x] **Title block** must include student name, roll number, branch, experiment number, date, and scale. - [x] The drawing sheet for assembly is **A2** (594 × 420 mm); part drawings may be on **A3**. ---

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Knuckle Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Knuckle Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Knuckle Joint** is a mechanical pin-type joint used to connect two rods that are subjected to tensile (pulling) loads, while permitting a limited amount of angular articulation (rotation) between them in the plane of the joint. From a **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** laboratory perspective (per the PCMUL507 syllabus), the experiment involves three sequential engineering operations:

1. **Part Modeling (3D Solid Modeling):** Creating individual solid models of the constituent parts — namely the *Single Eye (Rod End)*, the *Double Eye (Fork/Yoke)*, and the *Knuckle Pin* — using a parametric 3D CAD platform such as **SolidWorks / CATIA / Creo / NX / Fusion 360**.
2. **Assembly Modeling:** Importing (or creating within the assembly file) the individual parts and applying **mating constraints** (coincident, concentric, distance, angle) to assemble them into a fully constrained, kinematically correct knuckle joint assembly.
3. **Drafting (2D Drawing Generation):** Extracting standard orthographic 2D engineering drawings (with section views, dimensions, tolerances, and Bill of Materials) from the 3D assembly model using the drafting workbench of the CAD software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMUL507 – Module 2):**
> The lab experiment tests the student's ability to **independently model**, **constrain**, and **draft** a standard mechanical joint. The evaluation weightage (typical) is split as **Part Modeling (30%)**, **Assembly + Mating (35%)**, and **Drafting + Dimensioning (35%)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine holding two sticks with your hands such that they form a slight "V" shape, and you pin them together at the meeting point using a nail driven through both. Your two hands act as the **eye (single eye)** and the **fork (double eye)** — the rod ends. The nail is the **knuckle pin**. If you now gently swing one stick up and down, the joint allows angular motion, but if you try to pull the sticks apart along their length, the nail prevents it — this is exactly how a knuckle joint resists tensile load while permitting rotation.

In CAD terms:
- The **3D solid model** is the "digital clay" representation of each piece.
- The **assembly** is the act of snapping these clay pieces together using mating logic.
- The **drafting view** is the engineering blueprint a factory worker would read on the shop floor to actually manufacture the joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Knuckle joints are widely used in **automotive steering mechanisms (tie rods, drag links)**, **pump connecting rods**, **valve linkages**, **chain links**, and **jib cranes**. The pin-based articulation accommodates misalignment and minor motion without inducing bending stresses in the rods.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Conventions

- The standard reference proportioning follows **Shigley's Mechanical Engineering Design** (Khurmi & Gupta is also used in Indian B.Tech curricula).
- All dimensions are derived as **empirical multiples** of the parent rod diameter, denoted **d**.
- The conventional engineering units are **millimeters (mm)** for drafting and **Newtons (N)** / **MPa** for stress calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used Throughout This Module:**
> - $\mathbf{d}$ = Diameter of the rod being joined.
> - The **factor of safety (FoS)** for knuckle joint design typically lies in the range **3 to 5** for static tensile loads.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Knuckle Joint (Logical Breakdown)

A standard knuckle joint assembly consists of **three primary sub-components**:

| # | Component | Functional Role | Manufacturing Method (Typical) |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Single Eye (Rod End with one eye)** | Forged or machined at the end of one rod; has a single circular boss with a through-hole to receive the pin. | Forging + Machining |
| 2 | **Double Eye (Fork / Knuckle)** | Forged or fabricated at the end of the second rod; has two prongs forming a slot that receives the single eye between them. | Forging + Machining |
| 3 | **Knuckle Pin (with head & taper hole for cotter)** | A hardened cylindrical pin that passes through the aligned holes of both eyes, transferring the tensile load via shear. | Hardened Steel, Ground Finish |

Supporting hardware: A **taper pin (cotter pin)** inserted through a transverse hole in the knuckle pin to lock it axially, and a **collar or washer** under the pin head.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Mating Constraint (Operational Logic)

When assembling in a CAD environment, the engineer applies **constraints** that mimic the real-world physical behavior:

- **Coincident Constraint:** Forces two planar faces or axes to lie in the same plane (e.g., the inner faces of the fork touching the outer faces of the single eye).
- **Concentric Constraint:** Aligns circular edges/axes (e.g., the pin hole in the single eye aligned with the holes in both prongs of the fork).
- **Distance / Offset Constraint:** Sets a precise gap (e.g., the clearance gap of $\mathbf{0.5\text{ mm}}$ to $\mathbf{1.0\text{ mm}}$ between mating eyes).
- **Angle Constraint:** Sets the angular orientation between rods (e.g., a typical 180° straight configuration, or any inclined angle the design requires).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:** Proper mating in the CAD assembly directly validates that the physical parts will **assemble in the real world without interpenetration or under-constraint** — saving expensive prototype iteration.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The table below provides the **empirical proportional design formulas** for a knuckle joint in terms of the parent rod diameter **d**. These proportions are the high-yield, must-memorize values for KTU examinations.

| S.No | Parameter | Symbol | Empirical Formula (in terms of $d$) | Remarks |
|:----:|-----------|:------:|:----------------------------------:|---------|
| 1 | Diameter of rod | $d$ | — | Given / design input |
| 2 | Diameter of knuckle pin | $d_1$ | $d_1 = d$ | Pin is the most highly stressed member |
| 3 | Outside diameter of eye | $d_2$ | $d_2 = 2.2 \, d$ | Forms the eye boss |
| 4 | Diameter of eye boss head (knuckle head) | $d_3$ | $d_3 = 3.0 \, d$ | Increases bearing area of fork |
| 5 | Thickness of single eye | $t$ | $t = 1.1 \, d$ | Strengthens single eye against tension |
| 6 | Thickness of fork (each prong) | $t_1$ | $t_1 = 0.75 \, d$ | Two prongs = $1.5 \, d$ combined |
| 7 | Diameter of taper pin (cotter) | $d_4$ | $d_4 = \dfrac{d_1}{4} \text{ to } \dfrac{d_1}{3}$ | Locks the pin axially |
| 8 | Distance from center of pin to end of eye | $a$ | $a = 1.5 \, d \text{ to } 1.6 \, d$ | Resists tearing/shear failure |
| 9 | Width of fork (slot width $\approx$ single eye thickness) | $b$ | $b = 3.0 \, d \text{ to } 3.5 \, d$ | Provides overall rigidity |
| 10 | Length of pin head / taper portion | $h$ | $h = 1.5 \, d$ | Provides bearing seat |
| 11 | Length of knuckle pin (overall) | $l$ | $l = 2 t_1 + t + \text{clearance} + \text{head}$ | Geometric sum of components |

**Design Verification Formulas (Strength Checks):**

| Failure Mode | Governing Stress | Formula | Allowable Limit (Mild Steel) |
|:-------------|:-----------------|:--------|:-----------------------------|
| Tension in rod | $\sigma_t$ | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{4 P}{\pi \, d^2}$ | $\sigma_t \leq \dfrac{S_{ut}}{FoS}$ |
| Shear in pin (double shear) | $\tau$ | $\tau = \dfrac{2 P}{\pi \, d_1^2}$ | $\tau \leq \dfrac{S_{sy}}{FoS}$ |
| Crushing / Bearing on pin | $\sigma_c$ | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P}{d_1 \, t}$ | $\sigma_c \leq \dfrac{S_{uc}}{FoS}$ |
| Tension in single eye across slot | $\sigma_{te}$ | $\sigma_{te} = \dfrac{P}{(d_2 - d_1) \, t}$ | — |
| Shear of single eye end (tearing) | $\tau_e$ | $\tau_e = \dfrac{P}{2 \, a \, t}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Material Strength Values (Mild Steel, for quick exam reference):**
> - Ultimate Tensile Strength, $\mathbf{S_{ut} = 400 \text{ MPa}}$
> - Ultimate Shear Strength, $\mathbf{S_{su} = 320 \text{ MPa}}$
> - Ultimate Crushing Strength, $\mathbf{S_{uc} = 640 \text{ MPa}}$
> - Yield Strength, $\mathbf{S_{yt} = 250 \text{ MPa}}$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Knuckle joints are integral in applications requiring **pull-only load transfer** with **free articulation**. Their principal advantages are:
- Simple geometry → easy to forge and machine.
- Reliable pin locking mechanism (taper pin) → prevents accidental disengagement.
- Accommodates up to **±30° to ±45° angular motion** between rods without binding.

Used in: **Steering linkages of automobiles**, **intermediate shafts of marine engines**, **valve actuating rods**, **pendulum suspensions**, and **architectural tie-rod assemblies**.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Design Example (Standard KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> Design a knuckle joint to transmit a tensile load of **$P = 50 \text{ kN}$**. The parent rods are made of mild steel with $\mathbf{S_{ut} = 400 \text{ MPa}}$, $\mathbf{S_{su} = 320 \text{ MPa}}$, $\mathbf{S_{uc} = 640 \text{ MPa}}$. Adopt a **factor of safety (FoS) = 4**. Calculate all standard proportions of the joint.

### Step 1 — Diameter of the Rod ($d$)

The rod is subjected to direct tensile stress. For safe design:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{S_{ut}}{FoS}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{4P}{\pi \, d^2} = \frac{400 \times 10^6}{4}
$$

$$
\frac{4P}{\pi \, d^2} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
$$

Rearranging for $d^2$:

$$
d^2 = \frac{4P}{\pi \times 100 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
d^2 = \frac{4 \times 50 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 100 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
d^2 = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{3.14159 \times 100 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
d^2 = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{3.14159 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
d^2 = 6.366 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
d^2 = 636.6 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{636.6} = 25.23 \text{ mm}
$$

**Standardize: $d = 25 \text{ mm}$** (or, to be conservative, adopt $d = 30 \text{ mm}$; let us adopt $\mathbf{d = 30 \text{ mm}}$ for clean proportions).

### Step 2 — Recompute Design Load with Adopted Diameter

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{4 \times 50 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (30)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 900}
$$

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{2827.43}
$$

$$
\sigma_t = 70.74 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is well below the allowable $(100 \text{ MPa})$ — design is safe.

### Step 3 — Diameter of the Knuckle Pin ($d_1$)

Per empirical formula:

$$
d_1 = d = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Verification by Shear Check (Pin in Double Shear):**

The knuckle pin is sheared in **two planes** (since the fork straddles the single eye).

$$
\tau = \frac{2P}{\pi \, d_1^2}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (30)^2}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{2827.43} = 35.37 \text{ MPa}
$$

Allowable shear stress:

$$
\tau_{allow} = \frac{S_{su}}{FoS} = \frac{320}{4} = 80 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $35.37 \text{ MPa} < 80 \text{ MPa}$ ✅ **Pin is safe in shear.**

### Step 4 — Diameter of Eye ($d_2$) and Knuckle Head ($d_3$)

$$
d_2 = 2.2 \, d = 2.2 \times 30 = 66 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_3 = 3.0 \, d = 3.0 \times 30 = 90 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Thickness of Single Eye ($t$) and Fork ($t_1$)

$$
t = 1.1 \, d = 1.1 \times 30 = 33 \text{ mm} \; \approx \; \mathbf{33 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
t_1 = 0.75 \, d = 0.75 \times 30 = 22.5 \text{ mm} \; \approx \; \mathbf{23 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 6 — Diameter of Taper Pin ($d_4$)

$$
d_4 = \frac{d_1}{4} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5 \text{ mm} \; \approx \; \mathbf{8 \text{ mm}}
$$

### Step 7 — Edge Distance ($a$) and Fork Width ($b$)

$$
a = 1.5 \, d = 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
b = 3.0 \, d = 3.0 \times 30 = 90 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 8 — Bearing / Crushing Stress on Pin

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{P}{d_1 \times t}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{30 \times 33}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{990} = 50.51 \text{ MPa}
$$

Allowable crushing stress:

$$
\sigma_{c,allow} = \frac{S_{uc}}{FoS} = \frac{640}{4} = 160 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $50.51 \text{ MPa} < 160 \text{ MPa}$ ✅ **Bearing is safe.**

### Step 9 — Length of Knuckle Pin ($l$)

$$
l = 2 t_1 + t + \text{clearance} + \text{head length}
$$

$$
l = 2(23) + 33 + 2 + 25 \quad [\text{assuming } 2 \text{ mm clearance, head length } 25 \text{ mm}]
$$

$$
l = 46 + 33 + 2 + 25 = 106 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $\mathbf{l = 110 \text{ mm}}$ for standard sizing.

### Step 10 — Tearing Shear Check at Single Eye End

$$
\tau_e = \frac{P}{2 \, a \, t}
$$

$$
\tau_e = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{2 \times 45 \times 33} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{2970} = 16.84 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is far below $80 \text{ MPa}$ ✅ **No tearing failure.**

### Step 11 — Tensile Stress in Single Eye Body (Across the Slot)

$$
\sigma_{te} = \frac{P}{(d_2 - d_1) \, t}
$$

$$
\sigma_{te} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{(66 - 30) \times 33}
$$

$$
\sigma_{te} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{36 \times 33} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{1188} = 42.09 \text{ MPa}
$$

Below the allowable $100 \text{ MPa}$ ✅ **Safe.**

---

## 3.2 Final Analytical Matrix — Consolidated Design Output

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | Standardized Value (mm) |
|-----------|:------:|:--------------:|:-----------------------:|
| Rod diameter | $d$ | 25.23 mm | **30** |
| Pin diameter | $d_1$ | — | **30** |
| Eye outside diameter | $d_2$ | $2.2 \times 30$ | **66** |
| Knuckle head diameter | $d_3$ | $3.0 \times 30$ | **90** |
| Single eye thickness | $t$ | $1.1 \times 30$ | **33** |
| Fork prong thickness | $t_1$ | $0.75 \times 30$ | **23** |
| Taper pin diameter | $d_4$ | $d_1 / 4$ | **8** |
| Edge distance | $a$ | $1.5 \times 30$ | **45** |
| Fork width | $b$ | $3.0 \times 30$ | **90** |
| Length of knuckle pin | $l$ | — | **110** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:**
> Every dimensional quantity is a **deterministic multiple** of the master rod diameter $d$. Once $d$ is fixed (from the tensile strength equation), **all 10+ other dimensions are algebraically derived** — this is why knuckle joint design is considered one of the most structured questions in KTU Machine Design papers.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Assembly Workflow (CAD Procedure Flow)

The following block-level flowchart maps the **operational sequence** the student must follow in the CAD lab to convert a blank screen into a fully drafted knuckle joint drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Open CAD Software] --> A1[Create New Part File 1]
    A1 --> A2[Sketch Single Eye Profile on Front Plane]
    A2 --> A3[Extrude Boss to thickness t equals 1.1d]
    A3 --> A4[Cut Hole of diameter d1 using Extrude Cut]
    A4 --> A5[Save Part as SingleEye.SLDPRT]
    A5 --> B0[Create New Part File 2]
    B0 --> B1[Sketch Fork Profile Two Prongs]
    B1 --> B2[Extrude Fork to combined thickness 1.5d]
    B2 --> B3[Cut Two Holes diameter d1 aligned on axis]
    B3 --> B4[Save Part as DoubleEye.SLDPRT]
    B4 --> C0[Create New Part File 3]
    C0 --> C1[Sketch Circular Profile diameter d1]
    C1 --> C2[Extrude Cylinder to length l]
    C2 --> C3[Create Head on one end diameter h]
    C3 --> C4[Cut Transverse Hole diameter d4 near head]
    C4 --> C5[Save Part as KnucklePin.SLDPRT]
    C5 --> D0[Open New Assembly File]
    D0 --> D1[Insert Part SingleEye]
    D1 --> D2[Insert Part DoubleEye]
    D2 --> D3[Apply Concentric Mate between Single Eye Hole and Fork Holes]
    D3 --> D4[Apply Coincident Mate between Fork Inner Faces and Single Eye Outer Faces]
    D4 --> D5[Insert Part KnucklePin and Concentric Mate with both Eyes]
    D5 --> D6[Insert TaperPin and lock with Coincident Mate]
    D6 --> E0[Open Drawing File from Assembly]
    E0 --> E1[Generate Front View Top View Side View]
    E1 --> E2[Add Sectional View through pin axis]
    E2 --> E3[Apply Dimensions Tolerances and Surface Finish Symbols]
    E3 --> E4[Insert Bill of Materials BOM Table]
    E4 --> F0[End: Drafted Drawing Ready for Print]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Component Anatomy (Logical Block Architecture)

The schematic below maps the **physical hierarchy** of parts inside the assembled knuckle joint, showing how load flows from one rod to the other through the pin.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROD_A[Rod A Assembly]
        RA[Rod A Shaft] --> SEA[Single Eye]
    end

    subgraph ROD_B[Rod B Assembly]
        RB[Rod B Shaft] --> DEA[Double Eye Fork]
    end

    subgraph PIN_SYSTEM[Pin and Locking System]
        KP[Knuckle Pin Cylinder] --> KPH[Pin Head]
        TP[Taper Cotter Pin] -.locks.-> KP
    end

    SEA -- Concentric Mating --> KP
    DEA -- Concentric Mating --> KP
    SEA -- Coincident Faces --> DEA

    RA -- Tensile Load P --> KP
    KP -- Shear Transfer --> RB
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Failure Mode Mapping

This block diagram summarizes the **four principal failure modes** a knuckle joint must be checked against — a favorite KTU viva question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD[Applied Tensile Load P] --> F1[Failure 1: Rod Tensile Rupture]
    LOAD --> F2[Failure 2: Pin Double Shear]
    LOAD --> F3[Failure 3: Pin Crushing Bearing]
    LOAD --> F4[Failure 4: Single Eye Tearing Shear]

    F1 --> CHECK1[Check sigma_t vs Sut by FoS]
    F2 --> CHECK2[Check tau vs Ssu by FoS]
    F3 --> CHECK3[Check sigma_c vs Suc by FoS]
    F4 --> CHECK4[Check tau_e vs Ssu by FoS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> Each *Check N* node corresponds exactly to one row in the Strength Verification Formula table provided in **Section 2.3**. The student is expected to **derive all four checks** in a 14-mark KTU question.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any four empirical proportions used in the design of a knuckle joint, expressed in terms of the rod diameter $d$.**

**Model Answer:**
The four empirical proportions are:
1. Diameter of knuckle pin, $\mathbf{d_1 = d}$
2. Outside diameter of eye, $\mathbf{d_2 = 2.2 \, d}$
3. Thickness of single eye, $\mathbf{t = 1.1 \, d}$
4. Thickness of fork, $\mathbf{t_1 = 0.75 \, d}$

(Any four correct proportions from Section 2.3 may be awarded full 3 marks.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the knuckle pin designed in double shear rather than single shear? State the governing formula.**

**Model Answer:**
In a knuckle joint, the fork (double eye) straddles the single eye, so the applied load $P$ is resisted by the pin across **two parallel shear planes**. This reduces the shear stress on the pin by half compared to single shear, allowing use of a smaller-diameter pin.

Governing formula:

$$
\tau = \frac{2P}{\pi \, d_1^2}
$$

Compared to single shear $\tau = P / (\pi \, d_1^2 \, / \, 2)$ → the factor of **2 in the denominator of the shear force** confirms double-shear action.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Two full questions are asked; student answers **ONE**. Each question has sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Total marks for the module = 14.

---

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **knuckle joint**. State any **five empirical proportions** used in its design.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A knuckle joint connects two coaxial (or slightly inclined) rods that carry tensile loads. The end of one rod is forged into a **single eye** — a flat boss with a circular hole. The end of the second rod is forged into a **double eye (fork)** — a U-shaped yoke with two prongs, each having a hole. The single eye fits inside the slot of the fork; a cylindrical **knuckle pin** passes through the aligned holes, secured at one end by a **head** and at the other end by a **tapered cotter pin** driven through a transverse hole.

**Working:** When the rods are pulled, the tensile force $P$ is transmitted from rod → single eye → knuckle pin (in bearing/shear) → fork → second rod. The joint permits limited angular motion of one rod relative to the other about the pin axis.

**Five Empirical Proportions (in terms of $d$):**

| Parameter | Proportion |
|-----------|:----------:|
| Pin diameter | $d_1 = d$ |
| Eye outside diameter | $d_2 = 2.2 \, d$ |
| Knuckle head diameter | $d_3 = 3.0 \, d$ |
| Single eye thickness | $t = 1.1 \, d$ |
| Fork prong thickness | $t_1 = 0.75 \, d$ |

*(Neat sketch must show all three components with section view — 3 marks; description 2 marks; proportions 2 marks.)*

---

**A. (b) [7 Marks]** A knuckle joint is used to connect two mild steel rods of diameter $d = 40 \text{ mm}$ to transmit a tensile load of $P = 80 \text{ kN}$. Determine:
(i) The shear stress induced in the pin.
(ii) The crushing/bearing stress on the pin.
(iii) Whether the joint is safe, given $S_{su} = 320 \text{ MPa}$ and $S_{uc} = 640 \text{ MPa}$.

**Model Solution:**

Given: $d = 40 \text{ mm}$, $P = 80 \text{ kN} = 80 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$.

From empirical proportions:

$$
d_1 = d = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t = 1.1 \, d = 1.1 \times 40 = 44 \text{ mm}
$$

**(i) Shear Stress in Pin (Double Shear):**

$$
\tau = \frac{2P}{\pi \, d_1^2}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{2 \times 80 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (40)^2}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{160 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 1600}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{160 \times 10^3}{5026.55}
$$

$$
\tau = 31.83 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Crushing / Bearing Stress on Pin:**

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{P}{d_1 \, t}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{40 \times 44}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{1760} = 45.45 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Safety Check:**

Allowable shear stress: $\tau_{allow} = S_{su} / FoS$. Assuming $FoS = 4$:

$$
\tau_{allow} = \frac{320}{4} = 80 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{c,allow} = \frac{640}{4} = 160 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $\tau = 31.83 \text{ MPa} < 80 \text{ MPa}$ ✅ and $\sigma_c = 45.45 \text{ MPa} < 160 \text{ MPa}$ ✅,

**The joint is SAFE in both shear and crushing.** (3 marks for calculations + 2 marks for verification + 2 marks for conclusion.)

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for creating a 3D assembly model of a knuckle joint using **SolidWorks / Creo / CATIA**. Clearly state the mating constraints used at each step.

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Operation | CAD Action | Mating Constraint Applied |
|:----:|-----------|------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Open New Assembly File | File → New → Assembly | — |
| 2 | Insert Single Eye | Insert Component → SingleEye.SLDPRT | Fix in space (default) |
| 3 | Insert Double Eye (Fork) | Insert Component → DoubleEye.SLDPRT | — |
| 4 | Align fork slots with single eye | Select outer faces of single eye & inner faces of fork prongs | **Coincident** |
| 5 | Align the three holes | Select hole edges of single eye + 2 fork prongs | **Concentric** (3 instances) |
| 6 | Insert Knuckle Pin | Insert Component → KnucklePin.SLDPRT | — |
| 7 | Align pin axis with all three holes | Pin cylindrical face with hole edges | **Concentric** |
| 8 | Position pin head | Mate pin head face to outer fork face | **Coincident** |
| 9 | Insert Taper Pin | Insert Component → TaperPin.SLDPRT | — |
| 10 | Lock the taper pin | Taper pin cylindrical face with pin transverse hole | **Concentric** |
| 11 | Validate | Check DOF = 0; no interferences | Assembly Complete |

*(4 marks for procedure listing, 2 marks for naming constraints, 1 mark for DOF verification.)*

---

**B. (b) [7 Marks]** From the 3D knuckle joint assembly, generate the **orthographic drafting views**. List the **six standard orthographic views**, state which is the **front view**, and explain why a **sectional view** through the pin axis is mandatory for manufacturing.

**Model Solution:**

**Six Orthographic Views (Third-Angle Projection — KTU Standard):**

1. **Front View** — Shows the knuckle joint with the rod axis horizontal and the pin axis vertical; reveals the silhouette of single eye, fork, and pin head.
2. **Top View** — Shows the slot of the fork and the head of the pin from above.
3. **Bottom View** — Mirror of the top view (often omitted in KTU answers).
4. **Left Side View (LSV)** — Shows the depth of the fork and the position of the taper pin.
5. **Right Side View (RSV)** — Mirror of LSV.
6. **Rear / Back View** — Opposite of front view.

**Sectional View Justification:**
A sectional view through the pin axis is mandatory because:
- It **reveals the hidden engagement** between the knuckle pin, taper pin, and the two eyes.
- It **clarifies internal features** (e.g., the slot in the fork, the transverse cotter hole) that are otherwise shown only as hidden (dashed) lines.
- It enables the **shop-floor machinist to read hole depths, pin lengths, and assembly clearances** unambiguously.
- As per **BIS SP 46:2003 drafting standards** (KTU reference), sectional views are recommended when hidden line density exceeds 4 lines per view.

*(2 marks view listing + 3 marks sectional justification + 2 marks neat dimensioned diagram.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Using single shear instead of double shear** for the knuckle pin → results in a pin diameter 1.414× larger than required; examiner deducts **up to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the crushing/bearing check** on the pin → examiner deducts **1.5 marks** (this is a frequently skipped verification).
> 3. **Not standardizing $d$ to a nearest whole number** (e.g., leaving $d = 25.23 \text{ mm}$) → examiner deducts **0.5 mark** for non-standard dimension.
> 4. **In CAD drafting, omitting the Bill of Materials (BOM)** → examiner deducts **1 mark**; BOM is mandatory per KTU lab rubric.
> 5. **Forgetting the third orthographic projection projection angle** (e.g., writing only top + front + side, not specifying if 1st or 3rd angle) → **1 mark deduction**.
> 6. **In assembly, leaving Degrees-of-Freedom (DOF) > 0** (i.e., not fully constraining the parts) → **2 marks deduction** in lab evaluation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet Point Summary:**

- 🔹 **Knuckle Joint = Pin Joint** used for **tensile load transmission** with **angular articulation**.
- 🔹 **Three core parts:** Single Eye, Double Eye (Fork), Knuckle Pin (+ Cotter Pin).
- 🔹 **Master variable = rod diameter $d$.** All proportions are multiples of $d$.
- 🔹 **Key empirical values (must memorize):** $d_1 = d$, $d_2 = 2.2d$, $d_3 = 3d$, $t = 1.1d$, $t_1 = 0.75d$, $a = 1.5d$, $b = 3d$, $d_4 = d_1/4$.
- 🔹 **Pin always in DOUBLE SHEAR** — formula is $\tau = 2P / (\pi d_1^2)$.
- 🔹 **Four mandatory design checks:** Rod tension, Pin shear, Pin crushing, Eye tearing.
- 🔹 **Standard FoS = 3 to 5** (typically 4 in KTU problems).
- 🔹 **Standard material (Mild Steel):** $S_{ut} = 400$ MPa, $S_{su} = 320$ MPa, $S_{uc} = 640$ MPa.
- 🔹 **CAD Assembly Rule:** DOF must equal **zero** (fully constrained).
- 🔹 **CAD Mating Rules:** Concentric (for holes & pin axes), Coincident (for face contact), Distance (for clearances).
- 🔹 **Drafting Rule (BIS SP 46:2003):** Section view mandatory when hidden-line density is high (as in knuckle joint).
- 🔹 **Always include BOM (Bill of Materials)** in the 2D drafting sheet — it carries independent marks.
- 🔹 **Always standardize dimensions** to whole millimeters before finalizing the drawing.
- 🔹 **Standard 3rd Angle Projection** is followed in Indian KTU drawings (not 1st Angle, which is ISO European).
- 🔹 **Real-world use:** Automobile steering linkages, valve rods, marine intermediate shafts, crane tie rods.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Rigid Flange Couplings
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Rigid Flange Couplings

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Rigid Flange Coupling** is a permanent, non-flexible, sleeve-type shaft coupling that rigidly connects two coaxial rotating shafts to transmit torque without any relative angular or axial misalignment. It consists of two separate flanges (also called *flange halves* or *coupling halves*) that are keyed to the respective shafts and bolted together at their facing faces using a circular pattern of high-tensile bolts and nuts.

In the context of the KTU **PCMUL507 – Computer Aided Design and Analysis Lab (2024 Scheme)**, the experiment involves:
- **Part Modeling (3D CAD)**: Creating individual solid models of the flange, shaft, key, bolt, nut, and washer using parametric solid modelers (e.g., **SolidWorks**, **CATIA**, **Creo Parametric**, or **Fusion 360**).
- **Assembly Modeling**: Importing the part files into an assembly environment and applying **assembly mates/constraints** (concentric, coincident, distance, angle) to position the components exactly as in the physical rig.
- **Drafting (2D Detailing)**: Generating a manufacturing drawing of the full assembly with a parts list (BOM), section views, tolerances, and surface-finish symbols, conforming to **IS 696 / ISO 128** drafting standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> The experiment is mapped to **CO2 – *Create 3D parametric part, assembly models and 2D engineering drawings of standard machine components using CAD software.***

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Explanation

Imagine two train carriages that have to move together as one rigid unit. The railway coupler (the metal claw linking them) is exactly the same idea as a **flange coupling**: two separate pieces are bolted through matching circular plates so they spin in perfect lockstep, just like the carriages roll together on the track.

A simpler kitchen analogy: take two round plates (the *flanges*), put a stick through the center hole of each (the *shaft*), and bolt the two plates together with nuts and bolts around the rim. When you turn one stick, the other turnsexactly the same. That is a flange coupling!

The phrase **"rigid"** in the name means it is **stiff** — it does NOT allow the two shafts to be misaligned, tilted, or shifted. It is meant for perfectly aligned shaft lines only.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants and Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values (as per PSG Design Data Book and IS 2062 / IS 1367):**
> - **Hub Outer Diameter (Dₕ) ≈ 2 × Shaft Diameter (d)** — Standard empirical relation.
> - **Flange Outer Diameter (Dₒ) ≈ 3 × d** for cast-iron flanges.
> - **Bolt Circle Diameter (D_bc) ≈ 1.5 × d + d** (typically between **2d and 3d**).
> - **Number of Bolts (n):** usually 3, 4, 6, or 8 depending on torque.
> - **Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD)** of bolts is a standard design parameter.
> - **Standard Bolt Sizes:** M10, M12, M16, M20, M24, M30 (ISO metric coarse pitch).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Why a Flange Coupling is Used

A rigid flange coupling transmits torque from one shaft to the other through a **two-stage load path**:

1. **Shaft 1 → Key 1 → Flange A** (via shearing of key and friction fit).
2. **Flange A → Bolts → Flange B → Key 2 → Shaft 2** (via tension in the bolts and shear in the bolts).

Because the coupling is **rigid**, any misalignment between the two shafts (parallel, angular, or axial) is transmitted back to the shafts as bending stresses. Hence, rigid flange couplings are used only when the shafts are **perfectly aligned** by the supporting bearings.

## 2.2 Component-wise Operational Breakdown

- **Flange (Hub + Disc):** The hub fits on the shaft (usually with an interference fit or a key); the disc carries the bolt holes and is the flat mating face.
- **Shaft:** The rotating member. The KTU standard problem typically uses shaft diameter **d = 30 mm to 50 mm**.
- **Key (Sunk or Taper):** Prevents relative rotation between shaft and flange hub. Standard size = **w = d/4, h = d/7** (Square key) or as per **IS 2292**.
- **Bolts, Nuts, and Washers:** High-tensile bolts (property class 8.8, 10.9, or 12.9) carry the torque as shear. Countersunk bolts or **turned bolts (fitted bolts)** are preferred for accurate alignment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bolts (and not rivets) are preferred in modern flanges:** Bolts can be removed for maintenance, allow controlled preload, and provide reliable clamp load. Fitted bolts (grade 10.9 / 12.9) are shear-resistant.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet for Design Calculations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **expected data** for the KTU 2024 lab viva and ESE. Most "design" questions in Part A of the question paper will reference these formulas.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft diameter | d | $d = 16.7 \sqrt[3]{P / N}$ | mm | Khurmi's empirical equation |
| 2 | Torque transmitted | T | $T = (60 \cdot P) / (2\pi N) = 9550 \cdot P / N$ | N·m | $P$ in kW, $N$ in rpm |
| 3 | Hub outer diameter | $D_h$ | $D_h \approx 2 \cdot d$ | mm | Empirical |
| 4 | Hub length | $l_h$ | $l_h \approx 1.5 \cdot d$ | mm | Empirical |
| 5 | Flange outer diameter | $D_o$ | $D_o \approx 3 \cdot d$ | mm | Empirical (CI flange) |
| 6 | Flange thickness | $t_f$ | $t_f \approx 0.5 \cdot d$ | mm | Empirical |
| 7 | Bolt circle dia. | $D_{bc}$ | $D_{bc} \approx 2.5 \cdot d$ | mm | For 4–6 bolts |
| 8 | Bolt core diameter | $d_c$ | $d_c = \sqrt{ (4 \cdot T) / (n \cdot \pi \cdot \tau_b \cdot R)}$ | mm | $R = D_{bc}/2$, $\tau_b \approx 60$ MPa |
| 9 | Number of bolts | n | $n = (T) / (R \cdot F_b)$ | — | $F_b = \sigma_t \cdot A_c$ |
| 10 | Key width | w | $w = d / 4$ | mm | Square key |
| 11 | Key height | h | $h = d / 7$ | mm | Square key |
| 12 | Key length | l | $l \approx 1.5 \cdot d$ | mm | Sunk key |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU model answer sheet, the **diameter symbol convention is critical** — write $d$ for shaft, $D$ for flange, $D_{bc}$ for bolt circle. Examiner deducts marks for ambiguous notation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rigid flange couplings are the **workhorse of industry** for:
- **Pumps and compressors** (where shafts are short and bearing alignment is excellent).
- **Steam and gas turbines** (high-speed, high-torque applications).
- **Marine propulsion shafting** (large forged-steel flanges).
- **Rolling mills and steel plants** (rigid drive shafts between gearboxes and rolls).
- **Machine-tool spindle drives** (rigid, zero-backlash requirement).

In all of these, the **CAD model** built in the lab directly translates to the actual machined part.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard KTU 2024 Sample Problem Statement

> *"A rigid flange coupling is to be designed to transmit 15 kW at 250 rpm. The shafts are of mild steel and the keys and bolts are of steel. Design the coupling and draw the 3D assembly using a CAD package. Also generate the 2D drafting drawing with a part list."*

### 3.2 Step 1 — Calculate the Torque

The torque transmitted by the shaft is given by the standard shaft-power equation.

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N} \quad \text{(N·m, with P in W and N in rpm)}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$T = \frac{60 \times 15000}{2 \pi \times 250}$$

$$T = \frac{900000}{1570.796}$$

$$T \approx 572.96 \; \text{N·m}$$

### 3.3 Step 2 — Calculate the Shaft Diameter

Using Khurmi's empirical formula (which is the KTU-board-accepted equation):

$$d = 16.7 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\frac{P}{N}}$$

$$d = 16.7 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\frac{15}{250}}$$

$$d = 16.7 \cdot \sqrt[3]{0.06}$$

$$d = 16.7 \times 0.3915$$

$$d \approx 6.54 \; \text{mm}$$

This is the **minimum** diameter based on torque alone. Standard practice is to add a keyway and select the next higher standard size:

- **Standardized shaft diameter: d = 30 mm** (the KTU textbook / lab manual uses this as the standard for this experiment).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU **always** uses a pre-defined standard size from the lab manual (e.g., 30, 35, 40, 45, 50 mm) so that the CAD model has clean integer dimensions. The calculation is a viva question, not a dimension input.

### 3.4 Step 3 — Derive the Flange and Hub Geometry

Using the empirical design rules:

$$D_h = 2 \cdot d = 2 \times 30 = 60 \; \text{mm}$$

$$l_h = 1.5 \cdot d = 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \; \text{mm}$$

$$D_o = 3 \cdot d = 3 \times 30 = 90 \; \text{mm}$$

$$t_f = 0.5 \cdot d = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \; \text{mm}$$

### 3.5 Step 4 — Derive the Bolt Circle and Bolt Specification

For a typical 4-bolt design:

$$D_{bc} = 2.5 \cdot d = 2.5 \times 30 = 75 \; \text{mm}$$

$$R = \frac{D_{bc}}{2} = 37.5 \; \text{mm}$$

**Torsional shear stress on bolts** (assuming bolts are the only torque-transmitting element between flanges):

$$F_b = \frac{T}{n \cdot R} = \frac{572.96 \times 10^3}{4 \times 37.5}$$

$$F_b = \frac{572960}{150}$$

$$F_b = 3819.7 \; \text{N}$$

**Bolt core area required** (using $\sigma_t = 60$ MPa for shear design):

$$A_c = \frac{F_b}{\sigma_t} = \frac{3819.7}{60} \approx 63.66 \; \text{mm}^2$$

**Core diameter required:**

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot A_c}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 63.66}{\pi}} = \sqrt{81.04}$$

$$d_c \approx 9.00 \; \text{mm}$$

**Standard bolt selected: M10** (core diameter ≈ 8.16 mm, but M10 is the nearest standard metric size). The next safer size is **M12** (core ≈ 10.36 mm).

> [!TIP]
> In CAD, use a **standard ISO 4014 / ISO 4017 bolt** from the Toolbox/Prospector. Manually modeling a bolt is not expected at the KTU level.

### 3.6 Step 5 — Key Dimensions (Square Key as per IS 2292)

$$w = \frac{d}{4} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5 \; \text{mm} \quad (\text{standard size} = 8 \; \text{mm})$$

$$h = \frac{d}{7} = \frac{30}{7} \approx 4.3 \; \text{mm} \quad (\text{standard size} = 5 \; \text{mm})$$

$$l = 1.5 \cdot d = 45 \; \text{mm}$$

These dimensions are used to cut the **keyway slot** in the shaft and the **keyway slot** in the flange hub bore.

## 3.7 CAD Procedural Workflow (Analytical Matrix)

| Step | CAD Action | Tool / Command | Parameter / Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open new part file | File → New → Part | Name: `flange_30mm` |
| 2 | Sketch hub circle | Sketch on Front Plane → Circle | $\phi 60$ mm, boss-extrude 45 mm |
| 3 | Sketch flange disc | Sketch on hub face → Circle | $\phi 90$ mm, extrude 15 mm |
| 4 | Cut shaft bore | Extruded Cut | $\phi 30$ mm through hub |
| 5 | Cut keyway | Extruded Cut (rectangle on bore) | $8 \times 5$ mm |
| 6 | Bolt holes | Circle pattern on PCD $\phi 75$ mm | 4 holes $\phi 11$ mm (clearance for M10) |
| 7 | Fillet edges | Fillet | R3 to R5 mm |
| 8 | Save | File → Save As | `flange.SLDPRT` |
| 9 | Repeat for shaft | Cylinder | $\phi 30$, length 200 mm (with keyway) |
| 10 | Open Assembly | File → New → Assembly | `coupling_assy` |
| 11 | Insert parts | Insert Components | Flange_A, Flange_B, Shaft_A, Shaft_B, Key×2, Bolt×4, Nut×4 |
| 12 | Apply mates | Concentric, Coincident | Align shaft–hub, flange faces meet |
| 13 | Bolt-fasten mates | Concentric bolt↔hole, coincident head↔face | Repeat for all 4 |
| 14 | Save Assembly | File → Save | `coupling_assy.SLDASM` |
| 15 | Open Drafting | File → New → Drawing | Select ISO A2 / A3 sheet |
| 16 | Add views | Standard 3-View + Sectional Front View | Use Section tool, offset A–A |
| 17 | Add dimensions | Smart Dimension | $\phi$, linear, angular |
| 18 | Add BOM | Tables → Bill of Materials | Auto-populate from assembly |
| 19 | Add tolerances | Dimension property | ISO 8015, hole ±0.05 |
| 20 | Save Drawing | File → Save | `coupling_assy.SLDDRW` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with `n`). No reserved keywords used. Labels contain raw uppercase text, no markdown formatting inside quoted strings.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rigid Flange Coupling CAD Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User Starts New Part File] --> A2[Sketch Hub Circle D = 60 mm]
    A2 --> A3[Extrude Boss 45 mm]
    A3 --> A4[Sketch Flange Disc D = 90 mm]
    A4 --> A5[Extrude Disc 15 mm]
    A5 --> A6[Cut Shaft Bore D = 30 mm]
    A6 --> A7[Cut Keyway 8 x 5 mm]
    A7 --> A8[Pattern Bolt Holes PCD 75 mm]
    A8 --> A9[Apply Edge Fillets]
    A9 --> A10[Save Part flange half]

    A10 --> B1[Open New Assembly]
    B1 --> B2[Insert Flange A]
    B1 --> B3[Insert Flange B]
    B1 --> B4[Insert Shaft A]
    B1 --> B5[Insert Shaft B]
    B1 --> B6[Insert Keys 8 x 5 x 45]
    B1 --> B7[Insert Bolts M10 4 nos]
    B1 --> B8[Insert Nuts M10 4 nos]

    B2 --> C1[Apply Mate Concentric Shaft A to Hub A]
    C1 --> C2[Apply Mate Coincident Flange A Face to Flange B Face]
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C3[Apply Mate Concentric Shaft B to Hub B]
    C2 --> C4[Apply Mate Coincident for Bolts through Holes]
    B6 --> C5[Apply Mate Concentric and Coincident for Keys]
    B7 --> C4
    B8 --> C6[Apply Mate Concentric Bolts to Nuts and Tighten]
    C3 --> C7[Verify All Mates and Degrees of Freedom = 0]
    C5 --> C7
    C4 --> C7
    C6 --> C7

    C7 --> D1[Open New Drawing ISO A3]
    D1 --> D2[Insert Standard Three View]
    D2 --> D3[Generate Sectional Front View A A]
    D3 --> D4[Add Dimensions Tolerances GD and T]
    D4 --> D5[Insert Bill of Materials]
    D5 --> D6[Add Title Block KTU Format]
    D6 --> D7[Save and Export to PDF]

    nstyle1[Style]:::processStyle
    A1:::inputStyle
    D7:::outputStyle

    classDef inputStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    classDef processStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Assembly Constraint Logic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nShaftA[Shaft A] -->|Concentric| nFlangeA[Flange A Hub]
    nFlangeA -->|Coincident Face to Face| nFlangeB[Flange B Hub]
    nFlangeB -->|Concentric| nShaftB[Shaft B]

    nKeyA[Key A] -->|Coincident Bottom| nKeywayShaftA[Keyway on Shaft A]
    nKeyA -->|Coincident Sides| nKeywayHubA[Keyway on Hub A]

    nBolt1[Bolt 1] -->|Concentric| nHole1[Hole on PCD]
    nBolt1 -->|Coincident Head| nFaceA[Outer Face Flange A]
    nNut1[Nut 1] -->|Concentric| nBolt1
    nNut1 -->|Coincident| nFaceB[Outer Face Flange B]

    nBolt2[Bolt 2] -->|Concentric| nHole2
    nBolt3[Bolt 3] -->|Concentric| nHole3
    nBolt4[Bolt 4] -->|Concentric| nHole4
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Full Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nP1[1. Part Design] --> nP2[2. Assembly Modeling]
    nP2 --> nP3[3. Drafting and Detailing]
    nP3 --> nP4[4. Validation and Print]

    nP1A[Flange Half A] --> nP1
    nP1B[Flange Half B] --> nP1
    nP1C[Shaft 1 and Shaft 2] --> nP1
    nP1D[Key Square] --> nP1
    nP1E[Bolt Nut Washer] --> nP1

    nP2A[Apply Mates] --> nP2
    nP2B[Verify Zero DOF] --> nP2
    nP2C[Interference Check] --> nP2

    nP3A[Sectional View] --> nP3
    nP3B[Bill of Materials] --> nP3
    nP3C[Dimensions] --> nP3
    nP3D[Tolerances and GD] --> nP3

    nP4A[Mass Properties] --> nP4
    nP4B[Print to PDF] --> nP4
    nP4C[Viva Defense] --> nP4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a rigid flange coupling. List any four applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A rigid flange coupling is a permanent, non-flexible type of sleeve coupling used to rigidly connect two perfectly aligned coaxial rotating shafts to transmit torque. It consists of two separate flanges, each keyed to its shaft, bolted together at their facing faces.

*Applications:*
1. Connecting motor to gearbox in industrial drives.
2. Pump and compressor shaft couplings.
3. Steam and gas turbine drive shafts.
4. Rolling-mill drive shafts and machine-tool spindles.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the empirical design formulas for (a) hub outer diameter, (b) flange outer diameter, and (c) bolt circle diameter of a rigid flange coupling in terms of shaft diameter d.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Hub outer diameter: $D_h = 2d$
2. Flange outer diameter: $D_o = 3d$
3. Bolt circle diameter: $D_{bc} = 2.5d$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - When answering a **Part B** design question, you **must** show all units, substitute numerical values in **every step**, and present intermediate formulas. Skipping the torque calculation and going directly to the flange diameter will cost 2–3 marks.
> - In the **CAD** section of the answer, **mention the specific mate/constraint used** (concentric, coincident, etc.) and the **order of applying mates** — generic statements like "we assembled the parts" fetch 0 marks.
> - The drafting view **must** include section lines (hatching) and a **BOM table** — missing these is the most common cause of -2 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Design a rigid flange coupling to transmit 22 kW at 240 rpm. The shafts are of mild steel and the bolts and keys are of steel. Sketch the assembly and list the standard parts.**

#### (a) Design Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Torque transmitted:**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 22000}{2 \pi \times 240} = 875.35 \; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft diameter (Khurmi):**

$$d = 16.7 \times \sqrt[3]{\frac{P}{N}} = 16.7 \times \sqrt[3]{\frac{22}{240}} = 16.7 \times 0.45 = 7.55 \; \text{mm}$$

Use standard size **d = 35 mm** (next higher preferred size, with keyway).

**Step 3 — Flange dimensions:**

- $D_h = 2d = 70 \; \text{mm}$
- $l_h = 1.5d = 52.5 \;\text{→}\; 55 \; \text{mm}$
- $D_o = 3d = 105 \; \text{mm}$
- $t_f = 0.5d = 17.5 \;\text{→}\; 18 \; \text{mm}$

**Step 4 — Bolts (4 nos, M12):**

- $D_{bc} = 2.5d = 87.5 \;\text{→}\; 88 \; \text{mm}$
- $R = 44 \; \text{mm}$
- $F_b = T / (nR) = 875.35 \times 10^3 / (4 \times 44) = 4973.6 \; \text{N}$
- $A_c = F_b / \sigma_t = 4973.6 / 60 = 82.9 \; \text{mm}^2$
- $d_c = \sqrt{4 A_c / \pi} = 10.27 \; \text{mm}$
- **Select M12 bolt** (core dia. = 10.36 mm).

**Step 5 — Key (Square, IS 2292):**

- $w = d/4 = 8.75 \;\text{→}\; 10 \; \text{mm}$
- $h = d/7 = 5 \; \text{mm}$
- $l = 1.5d = 55 \; \text{mm}$

#### (b) CAD Assembly Procedure (7 Marks)

The CAD assembly of the designed coupling is built in the following sequence using **SolidWorks** (or equivalent):

1. **Part files created** (`.SLDPRT`): `flange_half.SLDPRT`, `shaft.SLDPRT`, `key_10x5x55.SLDPRT`, `bolt_M12x60.SLDPRT`, `nut_M12.SLDPRT`, `washer_M12.SLDPRT`.
2. **Assembly file** (`coupling_assy.SLDASM`) is created.
3. **First mate**: Insert `flange_half` and `shaft`. Apply **Concentric** mate between shaft outer surface and hub bore. Apply **Coincident** mate between shaft end face and hub end face.
4. **Key insertion**: Apply **Coincident** mate between key bottom and shaft keyway, and between key sides and flange hub keyway.
5. **Second flange**: Insert the other `flange_half`. Apply **Concentric** between the two flange bores and **Coincident** between the two mating flange faces.
6. **Bolts and nuts**: For each of 4 bolts, apply **Concentric** between bolt shank and bolt hole, **Coincident** between bolt head and outer face of flange, then insert nut and apply **Concentric** + **Coincident** to the opposite face.
7. **Verify**: All degrees of freedom should show **0**. Run **Interference Detection** to confirm no overlap.
8. **Save and proceed to drafting**.

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure to create the 3D assembly of a rigid flange coupling in any standard CAD software. List the standard parts in a Bill of Materials (BOM).**

#### (a) CAD Procedural Description with Mating Strategy (7 Marks)

The 3D assembly of a rigid flange coupling is built in three structured phases:

**Phase 1 — Part Creation (one file per part):**
Each component is sketched in 2D and extruded/revolved into a 3D solid. The hub is created by extruding a circle (D = 2d) and the disc is created by extruding a larger circle (D = 3d) on the hub face. The shaft bore is subtracted, the keyway is cut, and the bolt holes are patterned on the **Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD)**.

**Phase 2 — Assembly and Mating:**
The assembly is built using a **bottom-up** approach (inserting pre-made parts). The order of mates is critical:

| # | Component Pair | Mate Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft → Hub bore | Concentric | Aligns shaft to flange |
| 2 | Shaft end → Hub end | Coincident | Locks axial position |
| 3 | Key → Shaft keyway | Coincident | Prevents relative rotation |
| 4 | Flange A → Flange B | Concentric + Coincident | Aligns mating faces |
| 5 | Bolts → Bolt holes (×4) | Concentric | Aligns bolt axes |
| 6 | Bolt head → Flange face | Coincident | Sits bolt on face |
| 7 | Nut → Bolt threads | Concentric + Coincident | Tightens assembly |

**Phase 3 — Verification:**
The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** counter should read zero. The **Interference Detection** tool is run to confirm no component overlaps.

#### (b) Drafting View and BOM (7 Marks)

A 2D drafting sheet of size **ISO A3 (420 × 297 mm)** is created with the following content:

1. **Standard Three-View layout**: Top view, front view, and left side view of the assembly.
2. **Sectional Front View** (Section plane A–A passing through the bolt axes and shaft axis) to reveal the shaft, key, hub, bolt, and nut.
3. **Dimensions**: Shaft diameter ($\phi 35$), flange outer diameter ($\phi 105$), bolt circle diameter ($\phi 88$), flange thickness ($18$ mm), hub length ($55$ mm), and key dimensions ($10 \times 5 \times 55$).
4. **Tolerances and Surface Finish** symbols per **ISO 8015** and **ISO 1302**.
5. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** table:

| Item No. | Description | Material | Qty |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flange Half | Cast Iron FG 200 | 2 |
| 2 | Shaft | Mild Steel EN8 | 2 |
| 3 | Key (Square) | Steel 45C8 | 2 |
| 4 | Hex Bolt M12 × 60 | Steel 8.8 Grade | 4 |
| 5 | Hex Nut M12 | Steel 8.8 Grade | 4 |
| 6 | Plain Washer M12 | Steel | 4 |

6. **Title Block** (KTU standard): College name, student name, roll no., scale, sheet size, drawing number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Print This Section!**

- **Rigid Flange Coupling Definition:** A non-flexible permanent coupling that rigidly joins two aligned shafts using two flanges, keys, and bolts.
- **Standard Empirical Relations (memorize these for viva):**
  - Hub dia = $2d$
  - Flange dia = $3d$
  - Flange thickness = $0.5d$
  - Hub length = $1.5d$
  - Bolt circle dia = $2.5d$ (for 4 bolts)
- **Two torque paths:** (1) Shaft → Key → Flange, (2) Flange → Bolts → Flange → Key → Shaft.
- **Standard parts list for BOM:** Flange × 2, Shaft × 2, Key × 2, Bolt × n, Nut × n, Washer × n.
- **CAD Mates Order:** Concentric (shaft-hub) → Coincident (shaft end-hub end) → Concentric (flange bores) → Coincident (flange faces) → Concentric (bolts to holes) → Coincident (nut to bolt).
- **DOF must be zero** at the end of assembly. Interference check must pass.
- **Drafting requirements:** Section view through bolt axes, BOM table, tolerances per ISO 8015, hatching for cut sections.
- **Material choices:** Flange = Cast Iron (FG 200), Shaft = Mild Steel (EN8 / AISI 1040), Bolts = 8.8 / 10.9 grade steel.
- **Key standard:** IS 2292 (square key), dimensions $w = d/4$, $h = d/7$, $l = 1.5d$.
- **Khurmi's shaft design equation:** $d = 16.7 \sqrt[3]{P/N}$ — used for viva and design steps.
- **Torque equation:** $T = 9550 \times P / N$ (N·m, with P in kW, N in rpm).
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Using $d$ for both shaft and flange diameters in the answer — examiners deduct 1 mark per confusion.
- **Viva favorite question:** *"What is the difference between rigid and flexible coupling?"* — Rigid = no misalignment tolerated; Flexible = accommodates misalignment.
- **CAD software preferred (KTU lab):** SolidWorks (most common in Kerala colleges), followed by CATIA, Creo, and Fusion 360.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Flexible Coupling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Flexible Coupling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Assembly Model?

In Computer Aided Design (CAD), an **Assembly Model** is a 3D digital representation that combines multiple individual part models (components) into a single, functional mechanism by defining their spatial and mechanical relationships. These relationships are known as **mates** (in SolidWorks), **constraints** (in CATIA), or **joints** (in Inventor).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Assembly Modeling):** "A collection of two or more parts positioned and constrained together in a manner that simulates the mechanical behavior and kinematic functionality of a real-world product."

## 1.2 What is a Flexible Coupling?
