A **Flexible Coupling** is a mechanical device used to connect two rotating shafts (typically an input shaft from a motor and an output shaft of a driven machine) to transmit torque while accommodating **misalignment** (parallel, angular, or axial) and dampening vibrations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Components of a Standard Flexible Coupling (Spider Type / Jaw-Coupling):**
> 1. **Flange / Hub (Part 1):** Mounted on the driving shaft, has jaws (teeth/curved slots).
> 2. **Flange / Hub (Part 2):** Mounted on the driven shaft, identical jaws.
> 3. **Spider / Elastomer (Part 3):** A flexible rubber/polyurethane star-shaped insert with prongs that fit between the jaws of both flanges.
> 4. **Fasteners:** Bolts, nuts, and washers to lock hubs onto shafts.
> 5. **Keys:** Parallel keys for shaft-hub power transmission.

## 1.3 What is Drafting?

**Drafting** (or **Engineering Drawing**) is the process of creating 2D orthographic projections, sectional views, dimensioned drawings, and Bill of Materials (BOM) derived directly from the 3D assembly or part model. It is the manufacturing-ready document.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a Flexible Coupling as a **gear-box of a kid's toy car**. Two hard plastic gears cannot mesh if the shafts are not perfectly aligned. The **spider (rubber piece)** is like a soft sponge in between — it lets the two gears "wobble" a little while still pushing each other, so even if the shafts are slightly off, the toy still works. In CAD, **Assembly** is the digital toy car, **Mates** are the rules that tell the gears how to touch, and **Drafting** is the instruction manual (blueprint) given to a factory to build the actual toy.

## 1.5 Standard Engineering Specifications (Typical KTU Problem Values)

| Parameter | Standard Metric Value |
|---|---|
| Shaft Diameter | **Φ30 mm** to **Φ50 mm** |
| Number of Jaw Teeth (typically) | **3 prongs per side, 6 total** |
| Spider Material | **Polyurethane / Natural Rubber (Shore 80A)** |
| Flange Material | **Cast Iron / EN8 Steel** |
| Standard Mating Tolerance | **±0.05 mm** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is a Flexible Coupling Used? (Engineering Justification)

A rigid coupling cannot tolerate:
- **Parallel Misalignment:** Shafts parallel but offset.
- **Angular Misalignment:** Shafts meeting at an angle (typically up to 1°).
- **Axial Misalignment (End-Play):** Shafts moving towards/away during operation.
- **Shock / Vibration Loading:** Motor startup transients.

The flexible spider absorbs these deviations, increasing **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**.

## 2.2 Component Design Logic (Why each part exists)

1. **Hub / Flange:** Provides the rigid body for shaft mounting via **interference fit (H7/s6)** or **clearance fit with key (H7/k6)**. The jaws are designed with curved involute-like profiles to provide uniform contact stress on the spider prongs.
2. **Spider (Elastomer):** Acts as the **mechanical fuse**. In case of overload, the spider shears first, protecting expensive shafts and bearings. Cheaper to replace.
3. **Fasteners (Bolts/Nuts):** Provide **clamping force** to prevent rotational slip between hub and shaft.
4. **Key (Parallel Key):** Transmits tangential torque: $T = F \times r$, where F is the tangential force at the shaft surface and r is the shaft radius.

## 2.3 Key Dimensions Calculation Formula Sheet

### Torque Transmission Capacity

$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Torque in **N·m**
- $P$ = Power transmitted in **Watts (W)**
- $N$ = Rotational speed in **RPM**
- **60** is the seconds-to-minutes conversion factor

### Shaft Diameter from Torque (Solid Shaft)

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{max}$ = Maximum allowable shear stress in **N/mm²**
- $T$ = Torque in **N·mm**
- $d$ = Shaft diameter in **mm**

### Hub Outer Diameter Rule of Thumb

$$D_{hub} \approx 2 \times d_{shaft}$$

### Flange Outer Diameter (Bolt Circle)

$$D_{BC} = D_{hub} + 4 \times d_{bolt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet Table**

| Formula | Purpose | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T = \frac{60 \, P}{2 \pi N}$ | Torque from Power & Speed | N·m |
| $\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | Shaft shear stress | N/mm² |
| $P_{shaft} = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} \tau_{allow}$ | Power capacity of shaft | W |
| $D_{hub} = 2 d_{shaft}$ | Hub diameter design | mm |
| $D_{BC} = D_{hub} + 4 d_{bolt}$ | Bolt Circle Diameter | mm |
| $A_{key} = \frac{w \times h}{2}$ | Shear area of key | mm² |
| $F_{bolt} = \frac{2T}{n \times D_{BC}}$ | Bolt clamping force | N |

### Assumed Allowable Stress Values (For KTU Problems)

- Cast Iron: $\sigma_{allow} = 100$ to $150$ **MPa**
- Mild Steel: $\sigma_{allow} = 140$ to $200$ **MPa**
- Shaft (MS): $\tau_{allow} = 40$ to $60$ **MPa**
- Key (MS): $\tau_{allow} = 90$ **MPa**, $\sigma_c = 180$ **MPa**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pumps & Compressors:** Connect electric motor to pump shafts.
- **CNC Machines:** Stepper/servo motor to lead screw.
- **Conveyor Systems:** Motor to roller shaft.
- **Automotive:** Driveshaft to differential.

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU 2024 Lab Exams**, you are typically given a dimensioned orthographic sketch of a flexible coupling (or its individual parts) and asked to model each part, assemble it, and generate the engineering drawing with a parts list.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure (Assembly & Drafting Workflow)

## 3.1 Pre-Requisite: KTU Exam Problem Setup (Typical)

**Given (assumed values from KTU problem statement):**
- Shaft diameter: $d = 40$ mm
- Power to transmit: $P = 7.5$ kW at $N = 1440$ RPM
- Hub length: $L = 80$ mm
- Number of jaws: $n_j = 3$
- Spider prong radius: $r = 12$ mm

## 3.2 Derivation of Design Dimensions (Sample)

**Step 1: Calculate Torque**

$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N}$$

$$T = \frac{7500 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 1440}$$

$$T = \frac{450000}{9047.79}$$

$$T = 49.74 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Convert to N·mm**

$$T = 49.74 \times 10^3 = 49740 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Check Shear Stress in Shaft**

Using $\tau_{allow} = 45$ MPa for MS shaft:

$$\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 49740}{\pi \times 40^3} = \frac{795840}{201061.9} = 3.96 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Verification**

Since $3.96 \text{ MPa} \ll 45 \text{ MPa}$, the **Φ40 mm shaft is safe** (Factor of Safety $\approx 11.4$). The KTU problem is conventionally dimensioned for fit and assembly, not strength optimization.

**Step 5: Hub Dimensions**

$$D_{hub} = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

Hub length $L = 1.5 \times d = 60$ mm (or as given). Assume $L = 80$ mm.

**Step 6: Flange Disc Diameter**

The flange disc extends beyond the hub. Standard ratio:

$$D_{flange} = 1.8 \times D_{hub} = 1.8 \times 80 = 144 \text{ mm} \approx 150 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Spider Outer Diameter**

$$D_{spider} = D_{flange} - 2 \times \text{material thickness} \approx 130 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8: Jaws/Prongs Geometry**

For 3 jaws, angular pitch:

$$\theta = \frac{360°}{3} = 120°$$

Jaw width $w_j = 25$ mm, Jaw depth $h_j = 15$ mm (radial).

## 3.3 CAD Modeling Procedure (SolidWorks-Style Workflow)

### Part Modeling Sequence

**Part 1: Driving Flange (Hub + Disc + Jaws)**

1. Start a new part file. Select **Front Plane**, sketch a circle of $\Phi 40$ mm for shaft bore.
2. Extrude boss: $L = 80$ mm (hub body).
3. On the end face, sketch a circle of $\Phi 150$ mm (flange disc).
4. Extrude disc: $t_{disc} = 20$ mm.
5. Sketch 3 jaws arranged at $120°$ angular spacing on the disc end face.
6. Use **Extruded Cut** to remove jaw pockets: depth = $15$ mm, leaving 3 prongs.

**Part 2: Driven Flange** — Mirror copy of Part 1.

**Part 3: Spider (Elastomer)**

1. New part file. Sketch a circle $\Phi 130$ mm on Front Plane.
2. Extrude: $L_{spider} = 30$ mm.
3. On both end faces, sketch 3 prongs at $120°$ pitch.
4. Extrude boss: prong length $= 15$ mm, prong width $= 25$ mm (interference fit with jaw pockets).

**Part 4: Shaft** — Sketch circle $\Phi 40$ mm, extrude $L_{shaft} = 200$ mm (with keyway slot).

**Part 5: Key** — Sketch rectangle $12 \times 8$ mm, extrude $L = 50$ mm.

**Part 6: Bolt & Nut** — Standard M10 × 40 hex bolt and nut (use Toolbox or model).

## 3.4 Assembly Procedure

| Step | Action | Mate Type Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Insert Part 1 (Driving Flange) | — (First component is fixed by default) |
| 2 | Insert Part 4 (Input Shaft) | **Concentric Mate** (shaft bore to shaft OD) |
| 3 | Insert Part 5 (Key) into shaft keyway | **Coincident** faces + **Concentric** |
| 4 | Mate Part 1 hub to shaft shoulder | **Coincident** (face-to-face) |
| 5 | Insert Part 3 (Spider) | **Concentric** with shaft axis |
| 6 | Align spider prong to flange 1 jaw | **Coincident** faces |
| 7 | Insert Part 2 (Driven Flange) | **Concentric** with output shaft |
| 8 | Align flange 2 jaws to remaining spider prongs | **Coincident** faces |
| 9 | Insert Bolts (4×) | **Concentric** + **Coincident** for seating |
| 10 | Save as `.SLDASM` | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Step:** The **axis of both flanges must be collinear** (same Concentric mate applied with both shafts as the central reference). This is the most common cause of failed assembly submissions.

## 3.5 Drafting Procedure (Engineering Drawing Generation)

1. Open a new 2D Drawing file (.SLDDRW).
2. Select the Assembly file as the model reference.
3. Insert standard views:
   - **Front View** (showing the shaft cross-section)
   - **Top View** (showing flange face with bolt holes)
   - **Sectional View:** A **Half Section** (Front-Right quadrant cut) — this is mandatory in KTU 2024 scheme for assembly drawings.
4. Add **Center Lines** through all circular features.
5. Add **Dimensions:**
   - Overall length $L_{total}$
   - Flange diameter $D_{flange}$
   - Shaft diameter $d$
   - Bolt circle diameter $D_{BC}$
   - Jaw count and angular pitch
6. Insert **Bill of Materials (BOM)** table.
7. Add **Title Block** (with Part Name, Material, Scale 1:2, Drawing No., Student Name, Date).
8. Add **Balloon annotations** for each BOM item.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Requirement:** The Sectional View must be **Half Section**, with hatching (section lines) at **45°**, spaced at **2 mm** gap. Standard hatching pattern: ANSI31 for steel/rubber.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the Assembly Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: KTU Lab Session] --> B[Read Problem Statement]
    B --> C[Identify Components Required]
    C --> D[Calculate Design Dimensions]
    D --> E[Torque = 60P / 2piN]
    D --> F[Shaft Dia d]
    D --> G[Hub Dia = 2d]
    D --> H[Flange Dia = 1.8 x Hub]
    E --> I[Begin CAD Modeling]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Part 1: Driving Flange]
    I --> K[Part 2: Driven Flange]
    I --> L[Part 3: Spider]
    I --> M[Part 4: Shaft x2]
    I --> N[Part 5: Key x2]
    I --> O[Part 6: Bolts and Nuts]
    J --> P[Open Assembly File]
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Apply Concentric Mates]
    Q --> R[Apply Coincident Mates]
    R --> S[Verify Degrees of Freedom = 0]
    S --> T[Create Drawing File]
    T --> U[Insert Standard Views]
    U --> V[Create Half Section View]
    V --> W[Add Dimensions and BOM]
    W --> X[Add Title Block and Save]
    X --> Y[Submit KTU Lab Record]
```

## 4.2 Mate Constraint Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driving Shaft] -->|Concentric| B[Driving Flange Hub]
    B -->|Coincident Face| C[Spider Prong Set 1]
    C -->|Coincident Face| D[Driven Flange Jaws]
    D -->|Concentric| E[Driven Shaft]
    F[Bolt 1] -->|Concentric + Coincident| B
    F -->|Concentric + Coincident| D
    G[Bolt 2] -->|Concentric + Coincident| B
    G -->|Concentric + Coincident| D
    H[Key] -->|Coincident + Concentric| A
    H -->|Coincident + Concentric| B
```

## 4.3 Component Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ASM[Flexible Coupling Assembly]
    ASM --> P1[Part 1: Driving Flange]
    ASM --> P2[Part 2: Driven Flange]
    ASM --> P3[Part 3: Spider]
    ASM --> P4[Part 4: Input Shaft]
    ASM --> P5[Part 5: Output Shaft]
    ASM --> P6[Part 6: Key x 2]
    ASM --> P7[Part 7: Bolts and Nuts x 4]
    P1 --> P1A[Hub Body]
    P1 --> P1B[Flange Disc]
    P1 --> P1C[3 Jaws with Pockets]
    P3 --> P3A[Center Disc]
    P3 --> P3B[3 Prongs Side A]
    P3 --> P3C[3 Prongs Side B]
```

## 4.4 Drafting View Layout Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRW[Engineering Drawing Sheet A3]
    DRW --> V1[Front View - Unsectioned Half]
    DRW --> V2[Front View - Sectional Half]
    DRW --> V3[Top View showing Bolt Circle]
    DRW --> V4[Detail View of Spider]
    DRW --> TB[Title Block - Bottom Right]
    DRW --> BOM[Bill of Materials Table]
    DRW --> BL[Balloon Annotations]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Assembly Modeling" in the context of CAD. List any two mate types used in SolidWorks.**

**Model Answer:**
Assembly Modeling is the process of combining multiple individual 3D part files into a single functional model by defining their spatial and mechanical relationships using constraints called "mates."

Two common mate types in SolidWorks:
1. **Concentric Mate** — Aligns cylindrical faces to share a common axis.
2. **Coincident Mate** — Aligns two faces to be coplanar (touching).

*(Acceptable alternatives: Distance Mate, Parallel Mate, Tangent Mate, Fixed Mate)*

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition: 2 marks
- Two mate types: 0.5 + 0.5 = 1 mark

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the function of a "Spider" in a flexible coupling? What is the typical material used?**

**Model Answer:**
The spider is a flexible elastomeric insert placed between the two flanges of the coupling. Its function is to transmit torque between the two flanges while simultaneously accommodating **misalignment** (parallel, angular, and axial) and absorbing **shock loads and vibrations**. It acts as a mechanical fuse, protecting the shafts from overload damage.

**Typical material:** Polyurethane or Natural Rubber with Shore Hardness **80A to 95A**.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Function: 2 marks
- Material: 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, students answer ONE of the two internal choice questions below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** A flexible coupling of the jaw type is required to transmit **7.5 kW at 1440 RPM** between a motor shaft and a pump shaft. The shaft diameter is **40 mm**. Calculate the **torque** and the **maximum shear stress** in the shaft. Assume allowable shear stress is **45 MPa** and verify the design is safe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step procedure** to model and assemble a flexible coupling in a CAD software. List the mates applied at each step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution A(a):

**Step 1: Given Data**

$$P = 7.5 \text{ kW} = 7500 \text{ W}, \quad N = 1440 \text{ RPM}, \quad d = 40 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Torque Calculation**

$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N} = \frac{7500 \times 60}{2 \times \pi \times 1440}$$

$$T = \frac{450000}{9047.79}$$

$$T = 49.74 \text{ N·m} = 49740 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Shear Stress in Shaft**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 49740}{\pi \times 40^3}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{795840}{201061.93}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 3.96 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Verification**

$$\tau_{max} = 3.96 \text{ MPa} < \tau_{allow} = 45 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Factor of Safety:**

$$FOS = \frac{45}{3.96} = 11.36$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft design is **safe** with a high factor of safety, indicating that the design is driven by **standardization and assembly** rather than strength.

#### Model Solution A(b):

**Step-by-step CAD Procedure:**

1. **Part Modeling** — Create separate part files for: (i) Driving Flange with hub + disc + 3 jaws, (ii) Driven Flange (mirror copy), (iii) Spider with 3+3 prongs, (iv) Two shafts, (v) Two keys, (vi) 4 sets of M10 bolts and nuts.

2. **Create Assembly File** — New assembly document, insert Driving Flange as the base (fixed) component.

3. **Mating Sequence:**
   - **Step 1:** Concentric mate between Input Shaft OD and Driving Flange hub bore.
   - **Step 2:** Coincident mate between shaft shoulder and hub end face.
   - **Step 3:** Concentric + Coincident for Key into shaft keyway and hub keyway.
   - **Step 4:** Concentric mate between Spider center bore and shaft axis.
   - **Step 5:** Coincident mate between Spider prong 1 face and Driving Flange jaw face.
   - **Step 6:** Concentric mate for Output Shaft into Driven Flange.
   - **Step 7:** Coincident mate between Spider prong 2 face and Driven Flange jaw face.
   - **Step 8:** For each bolt — Concentric mate (bolt shank to hole) + Coincident (bolt head to flange face).

4. **Verify** — Check that "Degrees of Freedom" indicator shows **0** in the lower status bar.

5. **Save** — `.SLDASM` file.

6. **Drafting** — Generate drawing, insert Front View, Half Sectional View, Top View. Add dimensions, BOM, Title Block.

**Marks Distribution A(b):**
- Part modeling (3 parts minimum): 2 marks
- Mating sequence with mate types: 3 marks
- Drafting step: 2 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** Explain the **three types of misalignment** that a flexible coupling accommodates. How does the spider element help in each case? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the **components of a flexible coupling assembly** and explain the function of each. Draw a labeled block diagram of the **KTU 2024 Scheme assembly workflow** for flexible coupling drafting. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B(a):

**The three misalignments are:**

1. **Parallel (Offset) Misalignment:**
   - The two shafts are parallel but their axes are laterally offset (typically up to **0.2 mm to 0.5 mm**).
   - **Spider action:** The flexible rubber prongs bend sideways, allowing the flanges to rotate at slightly different radial positions without binding.

2. **Angular Misalignment:**
   - The two shaft axes intersect at a small angle (typically up to **1° to 2°**).
   - **Spider action:** The prongs flex angularly, allowing the flanges to tilt relative to each other while still transmitting torque through the compressed/tensioned sides of the prongs.

3. **Axial Misalignment (End-Float):**
   - The shafts move towards or away from each other along the axis (typically ±1 mm).
   - **Spider action:** The elastomer compresses axially, accommodating the shift without losing contact with the jaws.

**Conclusion:** The spider's **Poisson's ratio and Shore hardness** govern its flexibility. Lower hardness = higher flexibility but lower torque capacity.

**Marks Distribution B(a):**
- 3 misalignments named and explained: 4.5 marks (1.5 each)
- Spider action in each case: 2.5 marks

---

#### Model Solution B(b):

**Components and Functions:**

| No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Driving Flange (Hub) | Mounts on motor shaft; transfers torque to spider |
| 2 | Driven Flange (Hub) | Mounts on driven shaft; receives torque from spider |
| 3 | Spider (Elastomer) | Transmits torque + absorbs misalignment + dampens vibration |
| 4 | Shaft (Input & Output) | Carries rotational power |
| 5 | Key | Provides positive drive between shaft and hub (anti-rotation) |
| 6 | Bolts and Nuts | Clamp flange to shaft (axial locking) |
| 7 | Washers | Distribute bolt clamping load |

**Assembly Workflow Block Diagram (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ STEP 1: PROBLEM ANALYSIS                                   │
│   - Read KTU question                                      │
│   - Identify shaft dia, power, RPM                         │
│   - Calculate torque T = 60P/(2πN)                         │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 2: PART MODELING (6 Part Files)                       │
│   - Flange 1, Flange 2, Spider, Shaft 1, Shaft 2, Key     │
│   - Bolts/Nuts from Toolbox                                │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 3: ASSEMBLY (.SLDASM)                                 │
│   - Base = Flange 1 (Fixed)                                │
│   - Mates: Concentric → Coincident → Concentric → ...      │
│   - Verify DOF = 0                                         │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 4: DRAFTING (.SLDDRW)                                 │
│   - Front View (Half Section)                              │
│   - Top View (Bolt Circle)                                 │
│   - Detail View of Spider                                  │
│   - Dimensions, BOM, Title Block, Balloons                 │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 5: VALIDATION & SUBMISSION                            │
│   - Check section hatching, center lines, scale            │
│   - Export PDF and submit                                  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Marks Distribution B(b):**
- Component list with functions: 3.5 marks
- Workflow block diagram: 2 marks
- Drafting output requirements: 1.5 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Center Lines:** Every circular feature (shaft, hub, bolt holes) MUST have a center line passing through it. Missing center lines = **−2 marks** in drafting questions.
> 2. **Sectional View Errors:** Hatching must be at **45°** angle, **2 mm spacing**, and ONLY on solid cut material (not on bolts, nuts, keys, or shafts in standard KTU convention). Wrong hatching = **−1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Concentric vs. Coincident Confusion:** Many students apply Coincident on cylindrical faces. This is geometrically wrong. Coincident is for planar faces only. Concentric is for cylindrical. Mixing these = **mate error** and assembly will fail to resolve.
> 4. **Missing BOM Columns:** The Bill of Materials must contain: **Item No., Part Name, Material, Quantity**. Forgetting "Material" column = **−1 mark**.
> 5. **No Title Block:** Title block must include: Name, Roll No., Sheet No., Scale, Date. Omission = **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Wrong Scale:** Standard KTU assembly drawing scale is **1:2** on A3 sheet. Using 1:1 makes the drawing exceed the sheet boundary.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam**

### Must-Know Concepts
- [x] **Assembly Modeling:** Combining parts with mates; first part is fixed by default.
- [x] **Mate Types (Minimum 4 to remember):** Concentric, Coincident, Parallel, Tangent, Distance.
- [x] **Flexible Coupling Components:** 2 Flanges + 1 Spider + 2 Shafts + Keys + Bolts.
- [x] **Spider Function:** Torque transmission + misalignment accommodation + vibration damping.
- [x] **Three Misalignments:** Parallel, Angular, Axial.

### Must-Know Formulas
- [x] $T = \frac{60P}{2\pi N}$ — Convert power-speed to torque.
- [x] $\tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ — Solid shaft shear stress.
- [x] $D_{hub} = 2d$ — Hub-to-shaft diameter ratio.
- [x] $D_{BC} = D_{hub} + 4d_{bolt}$ — Bolt circle diameter.

### Must-Know Drafting Rules
- [x] **Section View Type:** Half Section is the KTU standard for assembly.
- [x] **Hatching Angle:** 45°, spacing 2 mm, ANSI31 pattern.
- [x] **Standard Scale:** 1:2 for assembly on A3 sheet.
- [x] **BOM Columns:** Item No., Part Name, Material, Qty.
- [x] **Title Block:** Name, Roll No., Sheet, Scale, Date.

### Common KTU Exam Traps
- [x] Always verify Degrees of Freedom = 0 before saving assembly.
- [x] Never hatch bolts, nuts, keys, washers, or shafts in section view.
- [x] Always include a Detail View of the Spider in the drawing.
- [x] Use **Coincident** for faces, **Concentric** for cylinders — never interchange.

### Expected Practical Output (What to Submit)
1. Six individual part files (`.SLDPRT`).
2. One assembly file (`.SLDASM`).
3. One engineering drawing file (`.SLDDRW`) with Front, Top, Half Section, and Detail views.
4. PDF export of the drawing.
5. Lab Record with manual dimensioned sketches of all parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Plummer Block
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Plummer Block

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Assembly Modeling** in Computer-Aided Design (CAD) is the process of combining multiple individual 3D part models into a single, functional, and constrained product model that simulates the real-world mating and kinematic behavior of mechanical components. In the KTU 2024 Scheme CAD laboratory framework, the **Plummer Block (Pedestal Bearing Housing)** serves as a canonical mechanical assembly model used to train students in the systematic application of *mating constraints*, *parametric relations*, and *drafting standards (ASME Y14.5 / BIS 696)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plummer Block** — A standardized **pedestal-type bearing housing** assembly, typically manufactured as a **cast iron** or **cast steel** two-piece split body, used to support a rotating shaft through a journal/ball/bearing element. It is one of the most frequently tested lab assemblies in KTU end-semester practical evaluations.

**Drafting** refers to the generation of standardized **2D engineering drawings** (orthographic projections, sectional views, and exploded views) directly from the 3D assembly model, complete with a **Bill of Materials (BOM)**, **title block**, **GD&T tolerances**, and **engineering dimensions** conforming to **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) SP-46:2003** or **ASME Y14.5-2018**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like building a LEGO structure...**

Imagine you have individual LEGO bricks (the **parts**). When you press two bricks together in a specific way—one stud fitting into a tube—that deliberate connection is analogous to a **mate constraint** in CAD. The full LEGO construction with all bricks snapped together is your **assembly model**. Now, if you take that LEGO structure and photograph it from the front, top, and side, then draw precise measurements on those photos, you have just performed **drafting**.

In the Plummer Block:
- The **Base** is like the ground plate of a machine bed.
- The **Cap** sits on top, just like a "cap" of a pen closes the ink reservoir.
- The **Bearing** (journal/sleeve) is the cradle that holds the rotating shaft—think of it as a cushioned "armrest" for a spinning rod.
- The **Bolts + Nuts + Washers** act as the "clamp" that holds the cap onto the base, just like a sandwich clip.

## 1.3 Components of a Standard Plummer Block (Part List)

| Sl. No. | Component | Material (Typical) | Quantity |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Base (Pedestal Bottom) | Cast Iron (FG 200) | **1** |
| 2 | Cap (Pedestal Top) | Cast Iron (FG 200) | **1** |
| 3 | Bearing Bush (Journal) | Phosphor Bronze / Babbitt | **1** |
| 4 | Hexagon Socket Head Cap Screw (Bolt) | High-Tensile Steel (Grade 8.8) | **2** or **4** |
| 5 | Hexagon Nut | Mild Steel | **2** or **4** |
| 6 | Plain Washer | Mild Steel | **2** or **4** |
| 7 | Dowel Pin | Hardened Steel | **2** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment typically expects the student to model **at least 4 to 6 individual parts**, assemble them using *standard mating constraints*, and then generate a **fully dimensioned orthographic + sectional drawing** of the assembly along with the **Bill of Materials (BOM)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Assembly Modeling

Assembly modeling is governed by three fundamental pillars in any parametric CAD platform (SolidWorks, CATIA V5, Creo Parametric, Autodesk Inventor, Fusion 360, or Solid Edge):

1. **Bottom-Up Assembly** (Most common in KTU labs)
2. **Top-Down Assembly**
3. **In-Place (Hybrid) Assembly**

### 2.1.1 Bottom-Up Assembly Strategy

In the **bottom-up approach**, all individual 3D part files are modeled *first*, saved as separate `.SLDPRT` (SolidWorks) or `.CATPart` (CATIA) files, and then *inserted* into an *Assembly file* (`.SLDASM` or `.CATProduct`) where they are positioned and constrained against one another.

- **Why?** — This is the most intuitive, exam-friendly, and debug-friendly method, especially for a Plummer Block where the part geometries are simple and distinct.
- **How?** — The student creates `Part1_Base`, `Part2_Cap`, `Part3_Bearing`, `Part4_Bolt`, `Part5_Nut`, `Part6_Washer`, `Part7_DowelPin` as separate files, and assembles them in `Plummer_Block_Assembly`.

### 2.1.2 Top-Down Assembly Strategy

In the **top-down approach**, the assembly file is created first with a *skeleton/layout model* that defines critical interfaces. New parts are then created *in context* of the assembly, sharing global parameters and references.

### 2.1.3 Standard Mating Constraints (KTU High-Yield)

The following table consolidates the **standard mate types** that the KTU examiner expects you to name, define, and apply:

| Mate Type | Geometric Meaning | Plummer Block Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Coincident** | Two faces/edges occupy the same plane | Bolt head face coincident with Cap top face |
| **Concentric** | Two cylindrical/circular features share a common axis | Bolt shank concentric with Base bolt hole |
| **Tangent** | Two curved surfaces touch at one tangent line | (Not typical for Plummer Block) |
| **Parallel** | Two planar faces remain parallel | Cap top face parallel to Base bottom face |
| **Perpendicular** | Two planar faces are at 90° | Washer faces perpendicular to bolt axis |
| **Distance** | Specifies a gap between two faces | Bearing protrusion distance from cap inner wall |
| **Angle** | Specifies angular offset | — |
| **Lock / Fix** | Fully immobilizes a component | Base of Plummer Block is *fixed* to origin |
| **Symmetric** | Two faces/edges are mirror images about a plane | Dowel pins symmetric about assembly center plane |

## 2.2 Drafting Standards (KTU-Relevant)

The 2D drawing derived from the assembly model must comply with:

- **Projection Method:** First-angle projection (**BIS/SP-46 — used in India**), NOT third-angle.
- **Views to Generate:**
  1. **Front View** (with vertical center line and partial section)
  2. **Top View** (plan)
  3. **Side / Left View** (with half/full section)
  4. **Sectional View** through the bolt axis (preferred: **Full Section AA**)
- **Scale:** Usually **1:1** for small assemblies; **1:2** for large.
- **Section Hatching:** Standard angle = **45°**, spacing **3 mm to 5 mm**, with a **3 mm pitch break line** indicating where the section terminates.
- **BOM Table:** Must list every part with **Item Number**, **Part Name**, **Quantity**, **Material**, and optionally **Part Number**.
- **Title Block:** Standard format with **Drawing Title**, **Drawn By**, **Checked By**, **Approved By**, **Date**, **Scale**, **Sheet Size (A3)**, **Projection Symbol**, and **Tolerance Grade**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Rule | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Pitch of bolt | $p$ | $p = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d}{\tan(\alpha)}$ where $\alpha = 30°$ (for std. ISO) | mm |
| 2 | Bolt root / minor diameter | $d_{core}$ | $d_{core} \approx 0.85 \cdot d$ (empirical) | mm |
| 3 | Tapping drill diameter | $d_{tap}$ | $d_{tap} = d - p$ | mm |
| 4 | Standard bolt size (Plummer Block) | $d$ | $M8, M10, M12$ (typical) | mm |
| 5 | Standard washer inner diameter | $d_{in}$ | $d_{in} \approx 1.10 \cdot d$ | mm |
| 6 | Standard washer outer diameter | $d_{out}$ | $d_{out} \approx 2.20 \cdot d$ | mm |
| 7 | Dowel pin diameter | $d_{pin}$ | $d_{pin} \approx (0.25 \text{ to } 0.40) \cdot d_{shaft}$ | mm |
| 8 | Bearing inner diameter | $D_{in}$ | $D_{in} = d_{shaft} + (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \text{ mm}$ clearance | mm |
| 9 | Shaft rotational speed | $N$ | $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot d_{shaft}}$ | rpm |
| 10 | Tangential surface velocity | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_{shaft} \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always use **first-angle projection** (the circular symbol with the inner and outer divided into two — counter-clockwise, used in **India, UK, and Europe**). Using third-angle projection is a common mark-losing mistake.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Plummer Block is the *backbone* of low-to-medium speed shaft supports in:

- **Textile spinning machines** (carding rollers)
- **Conveyor systems** in bulk-material handling plants
- **Agricultural machinery** (threshers, elevators)
- **Rolling mills** (entry/exit stands)
- **Power transmission drives** for industrial fans and blowers

The skills gained—constraint-based assembly, parametric part design, and standards-compliant drafting—directly map to industry-grade digital product development pipelines using **PLM (Product Lifecycle Management)** systems like **Teamcenter**, **Enovia**, and **Windchill**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **exhaustive step-by-step lab procedure** that the KTU examiner expects to see in your **Lab Record / Observation Book**. We will use **SolidWorks-style nomenclature** (the most commonly taught CAD platform in KTU-affiliated colleges), but the steps are generalizable to **CATIA V5 / Creo / Fusion 360 / Autodesk Inventor**.

---

## 3.1 Pre-Requisite Setup

| Step | Action | Why? |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Open **SolidWorks 2021/2022/2023** (or equivalent) | Establishes a parametric modeling environment |
| 2 | Set the **default document template** to **Part** (`Part.SLDPRT`) and **Assembly** (`Assembly.SLDASM`) | Avoids unit-mismatch errors |
| 3 | Verify **unit system** → `Tools > Document Properties > Units > IPS (Inch-Pound-Second)` or **MMGS (Millimeter-Gram-Second)** | KTU convention uses **MMGS** |
| 4 | Set **decimal places** to **2** for length, **1** for angle | Drawing readability |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Part Modeling (Bottom-Up Approach)

We will create **seven individual part files** first. For brevity and KTU exam relevance, the **Base** and **Cap** are the most critical.

### 3.2.1 Part 1: Base of the Plummer Block

**Step 1 — Front Plane → Sketch**

Select the **Front Plane** and click **Sketch**. Use the **Center Rectangle** tool to draw a rectangle centered at the origin. Apply **Smart Dimension** with:
- Width = **$W_{base} = 120$ mm**
- Height = **$H_{base} = 40$ mm**

**Step 2 — Extrude (Boss-Extrude)**

Exit the sketch. Click **Features > Extruded Boss/Base**.

$$\text{Depth of Base} = D_{base} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Sketch the Base Profile (Pedestal Cut-Out)**

Select the **Top Face** of the extruded block. Sketch a **rounded rectangle (slot)** in the center, sized to accommodate the bearing. Use the **Equal Length** relation to make both fillet radii identical.

$$\text{Inner Slot Length} = L_{slot} = 70 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Inner Slot Width} = B_{slot} = 35 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Cut-Extrude (Through All)**

Use **Features > Extruded Cut > Through All** to carve out the slot. This creates the **U-shaped bearing cradle**.

**Step 5 — Add Bolt Holes to Base**

Select the top face of the base. Sketch **two circles** at the ends, equidistant from the centerline.

$$\text{Bolt Hole Diameter} = d_{bolt} = 11 \text{ mm (clearance for M10 bolt)}$$

$$\text{Bolt Center Distance} = L_{bolt} = 90 \text{ mm}$$

Apply **Cut-Extrude (Through All)**.

**Step 6 — Add Dowel Pin Hole**

Sketch a **single hole** of diameter $d_{pin} = 6$ mm in the base, near one corner. Cut through half the depth.

**Step 7 — Add Fillet to All Sharp Edges**

Use **Fillet** with radius **$R = 5$ mm** on all outer edges. This adds the **draft angle approximation** for a real cast part.

**Step 8 — Save**

Save as `Plummer_Base.SLDPRT`.

---

### 3.2.2 Part 2: Cap of the Plummer Block

The cap is the *top half* of the bearing housing. Repeat the rectangular profile with these dimensions:

$$W_{cap} = 120 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{cap} = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad D_{cap} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

Sketch a **semi-circular bearing recess** (radius matching the bush) on the bottom face.

$$R_{bush} = \dfrac{d_{shaft} + 2 \cdot t_{bush}}{2} = \dfrac{40 + 2 \cdot 8}{2} = 28 \text{ mm}$$

Cut two **bolt clearance holes** of $11$ mm diameter through the cap. Save as `Plummer_Cap.SLDPRT`.

---

### 3.2.3 Part 3: Bearing Bush (Journal Sleeve)

A hollow cylinder. Extrude an annular ring:

$$D_{outer} = 56 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(matches } R_{bush} \text{ in cap)}$$

$$D_{inner} = 40.05 \text{ mm (with } 0.05 \text{ mm clearance for shaft)}$$

$$\text{Length} = L_{bush} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Add an oil hole** (Ø 6 mm) on top with an internal **oil groove** (a circumferential sketch + sweep cut). Save as `Plummer_Bush.SLDPRT`.

---

### 3.2.4 Parts 4, 5, 6, 7: Standard Hardware

Use **SolidWorks Toolbox** (or **Design Library > ANSI Metric > Bolts and Screws**) to insert:

- **M10 × 60 Hexagon Socket Head Cap Screw** (×2)
- **M10 Hex Nut** (×2)
- **M10 Plain Washer** (Ø 10.5 inner × Ø 22 outer × 2 mm thick) (×2)
- **Ø 6 × 30 Dowel Pin** (×2)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** Using the **Toolbox** is the fastest, exam-safe way to insert standard hardware. Always verify the part is inserted at the correct standard (ISO 4762 for socket head cap screws, ISO 4032 for hex nuts, ISO 7089 for plain washers).

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Assembly Procedure

### Step 1 — Create New Assembly File

`File > New > Assembly`. Save as `Plummer_Block_Assembly.SLDASM`.

### Step 2 — Insert the Base

`Insert > Component > Part`. Browse and select `Plummer_Base.SLDPRT`. The base appears in the graphics area. Click **✓** to place it. The base is automatically **Fixed** at the origin (this is the convention).

### Step 3 — Insert the Bearing Bush

Insert `Plummer_Bush.SLDPRT`. We will apply two mates:

- **Mate 1 (Concentric):** Concentric the bush's outer cylinder with the **semi-circular recess** in the base.
- **Mate 2 (Coincident):** Coincident the left end face of the bush with the left vertical face of the base slot.

This positions the bush *floating* (3 DOF remaining).

### Step 4 — Insert the Cap

Insert `Plummer_Cap.SLDPRT`. Apply three mates:

- **Mate 3 (Concentric — Bush/Cap):** Concentric the cap's bearing recess with the bush.
- **Mate 4 (Coincident — Cap to Base):** Coincident the cap's bottom face with the base's top face.
- **Mate 5 (Coincident — Cap to Base Front Face):** Coincident the cap's front face with the base's front face (aligns them in width).

The cap is now **fully constrained** (0 DOF).

### Step 5 — Insert the Dowel Pins (×2)

Insert `Dowel_Pin.SLDPRT` (or a simple extruded cylinder).

- **Mate 6 (Concentric):** Concentric the pin with the dowel hole in the base.
- **Mate 7 (Coincident):** Coincident the pin's lower face with the base's dowel hole bottom face.

Repeat for the second dowel pin.

### Step 6 — Insert the Washers + Bolts + Nuts

For each bolt station (left and right):

1. **Insert Washer → Mate 8 (Concentric):** Concentric washer inner hole with bolt hole in cap. **Mate 9 (Coincident):** Coincident washer bottom with cap top.
2. **Insert Bolt → Mate 10 (Concentric):** Concentric bolt shank with washer inner hole. **Mate 11 (Distance/Offset):** Offset the bolt tip by **$D_{base} = 60$ mm** below the cap.
3. **Insert Nut → Mate 12 (Concentric):** Concentric nut with bolt shank. **Mate 13 (Coincident):** Coincident nut's top face with base's bottom face.

### Step 7 — Verify Assembly Kinematics

Click **Move Component** (or `Ctrl+Shift+M`). Drag the bush/cap gently. There should be **no unintended motion** if all mates are correct. The **base is fixed**, the bush floats only on the rotational axis (1 DOF), and all hardware is rigidly locked.

### Step 8 — Save and Document

Save the assembly. Note the **exploded view** step for the next sub-section.

---

## 3.4 Drafting the Assembly

### Step 1 — Open New Drawing

`File > New > Drawing`. Select the **Assembly** (`.SLDASM`) and choose the **A3 sheet size**.

### Step 2 — Insert Standard Views

SolidWorks/CATIA will auto-populate four standard views:

$$\text{Front View} \rightarrow \text{Top View} \rightarrow \text{Left (Side) View} \rightarrow \text{Isometric / 3D View}$$

### Step 3 — Generate Section View (AA)

- Click **Section View** on the toolbar.
- Draw the **section line** vertically through the **center of the bolt axis** on the Front View.
- SolidWorks auto-generates the **Full Section AA**, hatching the cut faces at **45°**.
- Place the section view to the right of the side view.

### Step 4 — Create Exploded View

- Switch to the **Assembly window** (not the drawing).
- Click **Exploded View** (`Insert > Explode View`).
- Manually drag each component outward in the *assembly's three principal axes* (or use **Auto-Explode**).
- For the Plummer Block, explode along: **+Y** (cap up), **+Z** (bush right), **+Y** (bolts + washers + nuts upward).
- The final exploded view shows the **bolt path** and the **bush insertion path**.

### Step 5 — Insert Bill of Materials (BOM)

- In the drawing, click **Bill of Materials > Auto-Balloon**.
- The BOM appears as a tabular block with:
  - **Item No.**
  - **Part Number**
  - **Description**
  - **Qty**
  - **Material**

| Item No. | Part Number | Description | Qty | Material |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | PCM507-001 | Plummer Block Base | 1 | Cast Iron FG 200 |
| 2 | PCM507-002 | Plummer Block Cap | 1 | Cast Iron FG 200 |
| 3 | PCM507-003 | Bearing Bush | 1 | Phosphor Bronze |
| 4 | ISO 4762 M10×60 | SHCS Bolt | 2 | High-Tensile 8.8 |
| 5 | ISO 4032 M10 | Hex Nut | 2 | Mild Steel |
| 6 | ISO 7089 10 | Plain Washer | 2 | Mild Steel |
| 7 | ISO 8734 6×30 | Dowel Pin | 2 | Hardened Steel |

### Step 6 — Add Dimensions and GD&T

- Use **Auto Dimension** in the section view.
- Add the **shaft bore diameter** $\varnothing \, 40^{+0.03}_{+0.00}$ mm with GD&T callout for **cylindricity** or **circularity**.
- Add a **surface finish** symbol (Ra 1.6 for the bearing bore).
- Add a **general note** referencing the standard:

> "ALL DIMENSIONS IN mm. TOLERANCES PER ISO 2768-mK. BREAK ALL SHARP EDGES 0.5 × 45°."

### Step 7 — Fill the Title Block

Complete the title block fields:
- **Title:** `Plummer Block Assembly`
- **Drawn By:** `Student Name`
- **Scale:** `1:1`
- **Sheet:** `1 of 1`
- **Projection:** `⊕` (First-angle symbol)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides **functional architecture flow diagrams** and **assembly-exploded hierarchical maps** that the student can sketch directly in the lab record.

## 4.1 Top-Down Functional Architecture of the Plummer Block Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Plummer Block Assembly]
    nodeB[Support System]
    nodeC[Rotational System]
    nodeD[Fastening System]
    nodeE[Base FG 200]
    nodeF[Cap FG 200]
    nodeG[SHCS Bolts M10 x 2]
    nodeH[Hex Nuts M10 x 2]
    nodeI[Plain Washers x 2]
    nodeJ[Dowel Pins x 2]
    nodeK[Bearing Bush Phosphor Bronze]
    nodeL[Oil Groove Channel]
    nodeM[Oil Inlet Hole]
    nodeN[Static Cradle]
    nodeO[Split Clamp]
    nodeP[Journal Sleeve]
    nodeQ[Circumferential Groove]
    nodeR[Top Entry 6 mm]
    nodeS[Clamp Force Generation]
    nodeT[Preload Compression]
    nodeU[Locational Repeatability]
    nodeV[Position Fixation]
    nodeW[Shaft Support]
    nodeX[Load Distribution]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeE --> nodeN
    nodeF --> nodeO
    nodeK --> nodeP
    nodeL --> nodeQ
    nodeM --> nodeR
    nodeG --> nodeS
    nodeH --> nodeT
    nodeI --> nodeT
    nodeJ --> nodeU
    nodeJ --> nodeV
    nodeK --> nodeW
    nodeN --> nodeX
    nodeO --> nodeX
```

## 4.2 Assembly Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Start CAD Session]
    stepB[Set Units to MMGS]
    stepC[Open New Part File]
    stepD[Sketch Base Profile on Front Plane]
    stepE[Extrude Base to 60 mm Depth]
    stepF[Cut Bearing Slot Through All]
    stepG[Cut Two Bolt Holes 11 mm Dia]
    stepH[Cut One Dowel Pin Hole 6 mm Dia]
    stepI[Apply 5 mm Fillet to Outer Edges]
    stepJ[Save as Plummer Base SLDPRT]
    stepK[Create Cap as Mirror of Base]
    stepL[Add Bearing Bush as Hollow Cylinder]
    stepM[Insert Toolbox Bolts Nuts Washers]
    stepN[Open Assembly File]
    stepO[Fix Base at Origin]
    stepP[Concentric Bush to Base Slot]
    stepQ[Coincident Cap to Base Top Face]
    stepR[Insert Dowel Pins with Coincident Mates]
    stepS[Insert Washer Bolt Nut Triplet on Each Side]
    stepT[Verify Zero DOF on All Parts]
    stepU[Generate Drawing from Assembly]
    stepV[Insert Front Top Side ISO Views]
    stepW[Add Full Section View AA]
    stepX[Create Exploded View of Assembly]
    stepY[Insert Bill of Materials BOM]
    stepZ[Add Dimensions and Title Block]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepJ --> stepL
    stepJ --> stepM
    stepK --> stepN
    stepL --> stepN
    stepM --> stepN
    stepN --> stepO --> stepP --> stepQ --> stepR --> stepS --> stepT --> stepU --> stepV --> stepW --> stepX --> stepY --> stepZ
```

## 4.3 Constraint-Mate Application Matrix (Mermaid Heatmap)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    matA[Coincident Mate]
    matB[Concentric Mate]
    matC[Distance Mate]
    matD[Fix Mate]
    matE[Coincident Bush to Base Slot]
    matF[Concentric Cap Recess to Bush]
    matG[Coincident Cap to Base Front Face]
    matH[Concentric Bolt to Washer]
    matI[Concentric Nut to Bolt]
    matJ[Concentric Pin to Base Hole]
    matK[Coincident Nut Bottom to Base Bottom]
    matL[Concentric Washer to Cap Hole]

    matA --> matE
    matA --> matG
    matA --> matK
    matB --> matF
    matB --> matH
    matB --> matI
    matB --> matJ
    matB --> matL
    matD --> matO[Base is Fixed at Origin]
    matC --> matN[Optional: Bolt Protrusion Distance from Cap]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the term "Assembly Modeling" in a parametric CAD environment. List any **four** standard mate types used in SolidWorks/CATIA assembly modeling.*

**Model Answer:**

**Assembly Modeling** is the process of combining multiple independently modeled 3D part files into a single integrated product model, where each part is precisely positioned and oriented using geometric and dimensional **mate constraints** that simulate real-world physical contact and kinematic behavior.

The four standard mate types are:
1. **Coincident Mate** — Aligns two faces, edges, or points to occupy the same plane or location.
2. **Concentric Mate** — Aligns two cylindrical or circular features (e.g., holes and shafts) to share a common central axis.
3. **Distance Mate** — Specifies a precise gap or offset between two selected faces or features.
4. **Fix Mate** — Fully immobilizes a component (typically the first inserted part) relative to the assembly origin, removing all six degrees of freedom.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the **Bureau of Indian Standard (BIS) code** applicable for engineering drawing practice in India. Differentiate between **first-angle** and **third-angle** projection methods.*

**Model Answer:**

The applicable standard is **BIS SP-46:2003** ("Engineering Drawing Practice for Schools and Colleges"), which mandates the use of **First-Angle Projection** in India.

| Aspect | First-Angle Projection | Third-Angle Projection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Symbol** | Trapezoidal frustum (front view inside cone) | Truncated cone (front view outside) |
| **View Placement** | Top view placed **below** front view; Right view placed on the **left** | Top view placed **above** front view; Right view placed on the **right** |
| **Country Use** | India, UK, Europe, Asia | USA, Canada, Japan |
| **Imaginary Box** | Object imagined inside the box; views projected *outward* onto the box | Object imagined *outside* the box; views projected *inward* onto the box |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model Question Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **functional components** of a **Plummer Block (Pedestal Bearing)** and explain the role of each part. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** to create the 3D assembly model of a Plummer Block in any parametric CAD software using the **bottom-up assembly approach**. List the **mate constraints** applied at each joining interface. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Components of a Plummer Block:**

| Component | Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Base (Pedestal)** | Lower half that is bolted to the machine bed/foundation; provides the **static cradle** and contains the bolt holes and locating dowel pin holes. |
| **Cap (Top Cover)** | Upper half that closes the bearing assembly; contains a **semi-cylindrical bearing recess** and matching bolt clearance holes. |
| **Bearing Bush (Journal Sleeve)** | Sleeve bearing that supports the rotating shaft; made of phosphor bronze or babbitt for **low-friction contact**; has internal oil grooves for **boundary lubrication**. |
| **Bolt + Nut + Washer** | Fastener triplet that clamps the cap to the base, generating the **preload compression** required to hold the bush firmly around the shaft. |
| **Dowel Pin** | Provides **locational repeatability** during disassembly/reassembly; ensures the cap is always repositioned in the **exact same orientation** relative to the base. |
| **Oil Hole + Oil Groove** | Allows **lubricant entry** from the top; the circumferential groove distributes oil evenly along the shaft-bearing contact arc. |

**(b) Step-by-Step Assembly Procedure in SolidWorks (Bottom-Up):**

| Step | Action | Mate(s) Applied |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Open a new **Assembly** file; insert `Plummer_Base.SLDPRT` | **Fix** the base to the assembly origin |
| 2 | Insert `Plummer_Bush.SLDPRT` | **Concentric** bush outer dia with base slot recess; **Coincident** bush end face with slot end face |
| 3 | Insert `Plummer_Cap.SLDPRT` | **Concentric** cap recess with bush outer dia; **Coincident** cap bottom with base top; **Coincident** cap front with base front |
| 4 | Insert two `Dowel_Pin.SLDPRT` parts | **Concentric** pin with base dowel hole; **Coincident** pin end face with hole bottom |
| 5 | Insert `Washer`, `Bolt`, `Nut` for the left station | **Concentric** washer–bolt–nut triad with bolt hole; **Coincident** washer top with cap top; **Coincident** nut top with base bottom |
| 6 | Repeat Step 5 for the right station | Same three mates |
| 7 | **Verify** by manually dragging each part — only the bush should rotate; all else should be rigidly locked | — |
| 8 | Save as `Plummer_Block_Assembly.SLDASM` | — |

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Question Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** Define **drafting** in the context of CAD. With reference to **BIS SP-46**, explain the **components of a standard engineering drawing layout** for an assembled product. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the procedure to generate a **fully dimensioned orthographic + sectional drawing** of the Plummer Block assembly. Include the steps to add the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and **title block** as per KTU standard practice. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Drafting Definition and Drawing Layout Components:**

**Drafting** is the process of creating standardized **2D engineering drawings** (orthographic, sectional, auxiliary, and isometric) directly from a 3D model, following national/international drawing standards. The drawing serves as the **definitive communication document** between design, manufacturing, and quality assurance teams.

The standard layout of a BIS SP-46-compliant engineering drawing consists of the following components:

1. **Drawing Sheet (Border Frame):** An **A3 (420 × 297 mm)** or **A2** sheet with a 10 mm inner border frame.
2. **Title Block:** Located at the **bottom-right** of the sheet. Contains: drawing title, drawing number, scale, sheet number, drawn by, checked by, approved by, date, and projection symbol.
3. **Views:**
   - **Front View (Elevation)** — primary view
   - **Top View (Plan)** — placed *below* the front view in first-angle projection
   - **Side View (End Elevation)** — placed on the *left* of the front view
   - **Sectional View** — wherever internal features need to be revealed
4. **Bill of Materials (BOM):** Tabular block listing every component of the assembly with item number, quantity, part number, description, and material.
5. **Dimensioning & GD&T:** All linear, angular, radial, and diametric dimensions with applicable tolerances per **ISO 2768** or **ASME Y14.5**.
6. **Notes and Specifications:** Surface finish (Ra values), general tolerances, and references to standards.

**(b) Procedure to Generate the Drawing with BOM and Title Block:**

| Step | Action in SolidWorks (or equivalent) |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | `File > New > Drawing` → select the assembly file `Plummer_Block_Assembly.SLDASM` → choose A3 sheet |
| 2 | Drop **Front**, **Top**, **Left**, and **Isometric** standard views onto the sheet |
| 3 | Use the **Section View** tool: draw a vertical section line through the bolt axis on the Front View; auto-generate **Section AA** with 45° hatching |
| 4 | Switch to the assembly file → use the **Exploded View** tool to create a logical explosion (cap up, bolts up, bush to side) |
| 5 | Insert the exploded view into the drawing (typically placed in the top-right corner) |
| 6 | Click **Bill of Materials > Auto-Balloon** → balloons with item numbers are auto-placed near each component |
| 7 | Edit the BOM table to include **Item No., Part No., Description, Qty, Material** columns |
| 8 | Add **dimensions** in the Section View using **Auto Dimension** for: shaft bore diameter, bolt hole spacing, base width, cap height, bush length, dowel pin diameter |
| 9 | Add a **surface finish symbol** (Ra 1.6) on the bush inner bore, and a **cylindricity GD&T callout** if required |
| 10 | Complete the **Title Block** fields: Title = "Plummer Block Assembly", Drawn By = student name, Scale = 1:1, Projection = First-Angle symbol |
| 11 | Save as `Plummer_Block_Drawing.SLDDRW` and export to PDF |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **❌ Common Mistake 1:** Using **third-angle projection** instead of first-angle. **India strictly uses first-angle.** Lose 1–2 marks immediately.
> - **❌ Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **fix the base** to the assembly origin. The assembly will float and the entire model becomes unstable.
> - **❌ Common Mistake 3:** Applying mates in the **wrong order**. Always apply the most constraining mate first (e.g., concentric before coincident).
> - **❌ Common Mistake 4:** Omitting the **section view hatching angle** (must be exactly **45°** on the cut faces).
> - **❌ Common Mistake 5:** Using **third-angle BOM layout** instead of the standard KTU format.
> - **❌ Common Mistake 6:** Failing to **explode the assembly** before drafting. Many KTU questions ask for an exploded view; without it, full marks cannot be awarded.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Plummer Block CAD Lab**

- [ ] **Plummer Block** is a **pedestal-style bearing housing** with a **split body** (base + cap) for easy assembly around a shaft.
- [ ] **Bottom-Up Assembly** is the KTU-recommended approach: model all parts first, then assemble.
- [ ] The **first component inserted** in the assembly is always **fixed** to the origin.
- [ ] Standard mates you MUST know: **Coincident, Concentric, Distance, Fix, Parallel, Perpendicular, Tangent, Angle, Symmetric**.
- [ ] The **bolt triplet** (Washer + Bolt + Nut) requires **three mates** per station: concentric, coincident (top), coincident (bottom).
- [ ] The Plummer Block has **two dowel pins** for **locational repeatability**, not load-bearing.
- [ ] The **bearing bush** is the only sacrificial, replaceable wear component.
- [ ] Use **BIS SP-46:2003** for drawing standards; **first-angle projection** is mandatory.
- [ ] Always generate a **Full Section AA** through the **bolt axis** in the front view.
- [ ] Section hatching angle is **45°** with a **3–5 mm pitch**.
- [ ] The **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a **mandatory KTU requirement** for every assembly drawing.
- [ ] Standard fastener standards to quote: **ISO 4762 (SHCS)**, **ISO 4032 (Hex Nut)**, **ISO 7089 (Plain Washer)**, **ISO 8734 (Dowel Pin)**.
- [ ] Standard Plummer Block dimensions: **$W = 120$ mm**, **$H = 80$ mm** (base+cap), **$D = 60$ mm**, **shaft = 40 mm**, **bolt = M10**.
- [ ] The **exploded view** must be **orthogonal** (along principal axes) — not arbitrary.
- [ ] Always **save the part, assembly, and drawing** as separate files with **descriptive filenames**.
- [ ] In the title block, the **projection symbol** must be drawn — the trapezoidal first-angle symbol is **NOT** interchangeable with the truncated third-angle cone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Single Plate Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Single Plate Clutch**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assembly Modeling** is the process of combining multiple individual part models into a single integrated CAD file by defining precise geometric relationships (mating conditions such as coincident, concentric, distance, and angle constraints) between them. The resulting assembly accurately simulates the functional behavior, relative motion, and manufacturing fit of the actual mechanical product.

In the context of KTU's PCMUL507 lab syllabus, **Module Experiment 2** focuses on the *bottom-up assembly* approach, where pre-modelled parts are inserted into an assembly file and constrained using the *Mate* tool (the terminology used in **SOLIDWORKS**; equivalent to *Constraints* in CATIA, *Component Placement* in Creo/NX, and *Assemble* in Autodesk Inventor).

> [!NOTE]
> A **Single Plate Clutch** is a friction clutch that transmits power between a driving shaft and a driven shaft through the frictional contact of a single friction disc (clutch plate) pressed axially against a flywheel by a spring-loaded pressure plate. It operates on the principle of **kinetic friction** and is governed by two classical pressure distribution theories: **Uniform Pressure Theory** and **Uniform Wear Theory** (proposed by Professor John Daniel, 1907).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition Building)

Imagine you are riding a bicycle that has a small friction pad pressing against the rear wheel rim. When you squeeze the brake lever:
- The pad **presses** against the wheel (normal force, $W$).
- The microscopic roughness on the pad **resists** the wheel's rotation (friction, $\mu$).
- The bicycle **slows down** because the rotational motion is converted to heat.

A **Single Plate Clutch** works on exactly this principle, but in *reverse* — instead of stopping motion, it **transmits** rotational motion from the engine (driving member) to the gearbox (driven member). The driver presses the clutch pedal, the spring force is temporarily released, the friction disc disengages, and gear-shifting becomes possible. When the pedal is released, the spring force pushes the pressure plate back, locking the disc against the flywheel — and power flows again.

## 1.3 Components of a Single Plate Clutch (Inventoried Parts for Assembly)

| Sl. No. | Component | Functional Role in Assembly |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Flywheel** (Driving Member) | Rotates with the engine crankshaft; provides the friction surface. |
| 2 | **Clutch Plate / Friction Disc** | The driven friction element, splined to the driven shaft. |
| 3 | **Pressure Plate** | Applies axial clamping force on the friction disc. |
| 4 | **Clutch Springs** (typically 4–6 helical springs) | Provide the axial engaging force $W$. |
| 5 | **Release Levers / Thrust Fingers** | Multiply pedal force to disengage the clutch. |
| 6 | **Driven Shaft with Splines** | Receives torque via the splined hub of the friction disc. |
| 7 | **Cover / Housing** | Encloses and aligns the entire assembly. |
| 8 | **Bolts, Pins, Retainers** | Fasten cover to flywheel; locate springs and levers. |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 lab exam, students are expected to **import part files → apply mates in correct sequence → verify degrees-of-freedom (DOF) = 0 → generate an exploded view drawing → prepare a 2D orthographic draft with Bill of Materials (BOM)**. Mastery of the mate order is the single most important evaluator's checkpoint.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — How a Single Plate Clutch Engages

The torque transmission capacity of a friction clutch depends on:
1. The **axial clamping force** $W$ (provided by the springs).
2. The **coefficient of friction** $\mu$ between the contacting surfaces.
3. The **effective radius range** $[r_1, r_2]$ of the friction lining.
4. The **pressure distribution** $p(r)$ across the contact area.

Two classical theories predict how $p(r)$ varies with the radial distance $r$:

### 2.1.1 Uniform Pressure Theory (New Assumed Clutch)
- **Assumption:** The pressure $p$ is **uniform** across the entire friction surface.
- **Physical Justification:** Holds true for a *new*, rigid, freshly ground clutch lining.
- **Key Result:** Torque capacity is *lower* than the uniform-wear case at the same axial load.

### 2.1.2 Uniform Wear Theory (Worn Clutch, Steady-State)
- **Assumption:** The product $p \cdot r = \text{constant}$, because wear rate $\propto p \cdot v \propto p \cdot r$ is uniform.
- **Physical Justification:** Holds true for a *worn-in* clutch after thousands of engagement cycles.
- **Key Result:** Pressure is **maximum at the inner radius** $r_1$ and decreases hyperbolically outward.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Uniform Pressure vs. Uniform Wear)

| Quantity | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Assumed pressure distribution** | $p(r) = p = \text{const}$ | $p(r) \cdot r = C \implies p(r) = \dfrac{C}{r}$ |
| **Axial load relation** | $W = \pi p (r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2})$ | $W = 2\pi C (r_2 - r_1) \implies C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi(r_2 - r_1)}$ |
| **Maximum pressure $P_{max}$** | $P_{max} = \dfrac{2W}{\pi(r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2})}$ | $P_{max} = \dfrac{2W}{\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1)}$ *(at $r = r_1$)* |
| **Torque capacity $T$** | $T = \dfrac{2}{3} \mu W \cdot \dfrac{r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3}}{r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}}$ | $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu W (r_1 + r_2)$ |
| **Friction lining area $A$** | $A = \pi (r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2})$ | $A = 2\pi (r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}) \Big/ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ *(effective)* |
| **Recommended design basis** | Conservative design of new clutch | Realistic, long-life design |
| **Standard $\mu$ (cork & leather)** | $0.25$ to $0.30$ | $0.25$ to $0.30$ |
| **Standard $\mu$ (sintered bronze)** | $0.30$ to $0.40$ | $0.30$ to $0.40$ |
| **Typical unit pressure $p$** | $0.25$ to $0.50 \,\text{N/mm}^2$ | $0.25$ to $0.50 \,\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| **Aspect ratio recommendation** | $r_2 \leq 2\,r_1$ (for uniform-wear validity) | $r_2 \leq 2\,r_1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** The factor $\dfrac{r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3}}{r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}}$ is mathematically equivalent to the **mean frictional radius** $R_m$ in uniform pressure theory. Examiners frequently test whether students recognize this simplification: $R_m = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3}}{r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}}$.

## 2.3 Logical Reasoning — Why the Mating Sequence Matters in CAD

In the assembly, parts must be placed in a sequence that progressively reduces the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of every component. A rigid body in 3D space has 6 DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations). Each successful mate removes 1–3 DOF.

**Recommended Mating Order (KTU Standard):**

1. **Step A — Fix the Flywheel.** Apply a *Fixed* constraint to the flywheel. This anchors the assembly ground frame (1 DOF = 0).
2. **Step B — Concentric Mate:** Insert the driven shaft and mate its outer cylindrical surface **concentric** to the flywheel's center bore (removes 4 DOF).
3. **Step C — Coincident Mate:** Place the friction disc, then mate its **back face coincident** to the flywheel's front face (removes 1 DOF).
4. **Step D — Concentric Mate (Splines):** Mate the friction disc's hub **concentric** to the driven shaft (removes 4 DOF).
5. **Step E — Distance/Offset Mate:** Position the pressure plate at an axial offset equal to the disc thickness (removes 1 DOF).
6. **Step F — Concentric Mate for Springs:** Place 4–6 helical springs between cover and pressure plate.
7. **Step G — Final Cover Mate:** Mate the cover's bolt-hole pattern to the flywheel's bolt pattern using *Coincident* + *Concentric* mates.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Single plate clutches are extensively used in:
- **Passenger automobiles** (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai) — capacity up to ~350 Nm.
- **Light commercial vehicles** and **motorcycles**.
- **Machine tool spindle drives** requiring smooth engagement.
- **Industrial pumps and compressors** for overload protection.

CAD assembly modeling of the clutch enables:
- **Interference detection** (verify no part collides during axial disengagement).
- **Motion simulation** (animate the release lever and observe plate lift-off).
- **Mass property calculation** (inertia tensor $I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz}$ for dynamic balancing).
- **Automatic generation of BOM and 2D shop-floor drawings.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque under Uniform Pressure Theory

### Given Data
- Inner radius of friction lining: $r_1$
- Outer radius of friction lining: $r_2$
- Uniform pressure: $p$
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu$
- Axial clamping force: $W$

### Step 1 — Consider an elementary annular ring

Take a thin ring of radius $r$, radial thickness $dr$, on the friction surface. Its area is:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

The normal force on this ring (since $p$ is uniform):

$$dN = p \cdot dA = 2\pi p \, r \, dr$$

The elemental frictional (tangential) force on this ring:

$$dF_f = \mu \, dN = 2\pi \mu p \, r \, dr$$

### Step 2 — Elemental torque about the shaft axis

Torque is tangential force multiplied by the moment arm $r$:

$$dT = r \cdot dF_f = 2\pi \mu p \, r^{2} \, dr$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from $r_1$ to $r_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{r_1}^{r_2} 2\pi \mu p \, r^{2} \, dr \\[4pt]
  &= 2\pi \mu p \left[ \dfrac{r^{3}}{3} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{2\pi \mu p}{3} \left( r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Express $p$ in terms of $W$ using the axial force balance

The total axial force on the disc equals the integral of pressure over the area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{r_1}^{r_2} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi p \left[ \dfrac{r^{2}}{2} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} \\[4pt]
  &= \pi p \left( r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2} \right) \\[4pt]
  \therefore \quad p &= \dfrac{W}{\pi \left( r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2} \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $p$ back into the torque expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{2\pi \mu}{3} \left( r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3} \right) \cdot \dfrac{W}{\pi \left( r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2} \right)} \\[6pt]
  &= \boxed{\,\dfrac{2}{3} \, \mu \, W \cdot \dfrac{r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3}}{r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}}\,} \quad \text{(Uniform Pressure Torque)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Maximum pressure in uniform pressure theory

By assumption, pressure is uniform, so $P_{max} = p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= \dfrac{W}{\pi (r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2})}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks write $P_{max} = \dfrac{2W}{\pi(r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2})}$ because they include the **factor-of-2** for two friction surfaces (front and back of the disc). Always clarify with your examiner whether the design is **single-faced** or **double-faced**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque under Uniform Wear Theory

### Step 1 — Wear rate assumption

Wear rate per unit area, $w \propto p \cdot v$, where $v$ is the sliding velocity. For uniform wear: $w = \text{const}$, and since $v = \omega r$:

$$p(r) \cdot r = C \quad \text{(constant)}$$

### Step 2 — Axial force balance to find $C$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{r_1}^{r_2} p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{C}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \\[4pt]
  &= 2\pi C \int_{r_1}^{r_2} dr = 2\pi C (r_2 - r_1) \\[4pt]
  \therefore \quad C &= \dfrac{W}{2\pi (r_2 - r_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Elemental torque

Friction force on ring: $dF_f = \mu p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \mu C \cdot 2\pi \, dr$ *(note: the $r$ cancels!)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
dT &= r \cdot dF_f = 2\pi \mu C \, r \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Integrate to obtain total torque

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{r_1}^{r_2} 2\pi \mu C \, r \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \left[ \dfrac{r^{2}}{2} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} \\[4pt]
  &= \pi \mu C \left( r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2} \right) \\[4pt]
  &= \pi \mu \cdot \dfrac{W}{2\pi (r_2 - r_1)} \cdot (r_2 - r_1)(r_2 + r_1) \\[4pt]
  &= \boxed{\,\dfrac{1}{2} \, \mu \, W \, (r_1 + r_2)\,} \quad \text{(Uniform Wear Torque)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Maximum pressure in uniform wear theory

Pressure is maximum at the smallest radius, $r = r_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} = p(r_1) &= \dfrac{C}{r_1} = \dfrac{W}{2\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Design Problem (Sample for Exam Practice)

**Problem Statement:** A single plate clutch (uniform wear theory) is required to transmit $7.5 \,\text{kW}$ at $900 \,\text{rpm}$. The coefficient of friction is $0.3$, the inner radius is $50 \,\text{mm}$, and the outer radius is $100 \,\text{mm}$. Find the axial force required and the maximum pressure.

### Step 1 — Compute the required torque from power and speed

Power $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ where $N$ is in rpm, $T$ in N·m, $P$ in watts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{60 \, P}{2\pi N} = \dfrac{60 \times 7500}{2\pi \times 900} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{450000}{5654.87} \approx 79.58 \,\text{N·m} = 79577 \,\text{N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply uniform wear torque formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{1}{2} \mu W (r_1 + r_2) \\[4pt]
79577 &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3 \times W \times (50 + 100) \\[4pt]
79577 &= 0.15 \times W \times 150 = 22.5 \, W \\[4pt]
W &= \dfrac{79577}{22.5} \approx 3537 \,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the maximum pressure

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= \dfrac{W}{2\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1)} = \dfrac{3537}{2\pi \times 50 \times 50} \\[4pt]
        &= \dfrac{3537}{15708} \approx 0.225 \,\text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Validation against standard limits

The result $P_{max} = 0.225 \,\text{N/mm}^2$ lies within the **recommended range of 0.10 to 0.35 N/mm²** for asbestos-based friction linings. ✅ **Design is safe.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Assembly Hierarchy Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following block diagram depicts the **mating hierarchy** and the dependency tree of the single plate clutch assembly. Top-level sub-assemblies and leaf-level parts are clearly distinguished.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[ASSEMBLY: Single Plate Clutch] --> A1[Sub-Assembly: Driving Group]
    A0 --> A2[Sub-Assembly: Driven Group]
    A0 --> A3[Sub-Assembly: Actuation Group]
    A0 --> A4[Sub-Assembly: Cover Group]

    A1 --> P1[Part: Flywheel]
    A1 --> P2[Part: Flywheel Bolts x6]

    A2 --> P3[Part: Driven Shaft with Splines]
    A2 --> P4[Part: Friction Disc with Hub]
    A2 --> P5[Part: Rivets x8]

    A3 --> P6[Part: Pressure Plate]
    A3 --> P7[Part: Helical Springs x6]
    A3 --> P8[Part: Release Lever x3]
    A3 --> P9[Part: Thrust Bearing]

    A4 --> P10[Part: Clutch Cover]
    A4 --> P11[Part: Retainer Pins x3]
    A4 --> P12[Part: Pivot Bolts x3]

    P1 -. Concentric Mate .-> P3
    P3 -. Concentric Mate .-> P4
    P1 -. Coincident Face .-> P4
    P4 -. Distance Offset .-> P6
    P6 -. Concentric .-> P7
    P10 -. Coincident Pattern .-> P2
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the Clutch Actuation Mechanism

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Driver Pedal Input] --> B[Mechanical Linkage]
    B --> C[Release Fork / Lever]
    C --> D[Thrust Bearing]
    D --> E[Pressure Plate Retraction]
    E --> F[Spring Compression / Release]
    F --> G{Engaged or Disengaged?}
    G -- Engaged --> H[Power Flow: Engine to Gearbox]
    G -- Disengaged --> I[No Torque Transfer]
    H --> J[Gearbox Input Shaft]
    I --> K[Free Wheel for Gear Shift]
```

## 4.3 Mating Relationship Schematic (Component-Pair View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[Flywheel] -- Concentric + Coincident --> FD1[Friction Disc]
    FD1 -- Splined Hub --> S1[Driven Shaft]
    S1 -- Concentric --> PP1[Pressure Plate]
    PP1 -- Distance Offset equal to Disc Thickness --> FD1
    SP1[Helical Springs] -- Concentric Location --> PP1
    CV1[Clutch Cover] -- Bolt Pattern Coincident --> F1
    RL1[Release Lever] -- Pivot Coincident --> CV1
    TB1[Thrust Bearing] -- Concentric on Shaft --> RL1
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Functional Layout (Mermaid Block-Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Block1[Engine Side - Driving] --> Block2[Flywheel Disc]
    Block2 --> Block3[Friction Lining - Front Face]
    Block3 --> Block4[Clutch Plate Core]
    Block4 --> Block5[Friction Lining - Rear Face]
    Block5 --> Block6[Pressure Plate]
    Block6 --> Block7[Spring Bank]
    Block7 --> Block8[Clutch Cover]
    Block8 --> Block9[Gearbox Side - Driven]
    Block9 --> Block10[Driven Shaft with Splines]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four Mermaid diagrams above are deliberately framed as **block-level functional architectures** rather than literal cross-section drawings, in accordance with the protocol for lab-style assembly topics where physical cross-sections are difficult to render textually. The mating labels (Concentric, Coincident, Distance Offset) reflect the exact constraint vocabulary used in SOLIDWORKS / CATIA / Creo, which is the expected KTU lab skill.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Uniform Pressure Theory and Uniform Wear Theory used in the design of friction clutches.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Assumption | Pressure $p$ is constant across contact area. | $p \cdot r = \text{constant}$; wear rate is uniform. |
| Applicable condition | New, rigid clutch. | Worn-in, long-service clutch. |
| Torque formula | $T = \tfrac{2}{3} \mu W \tfrac{r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3}}{r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}}$ | $T = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu W (r_1 + r_2)$ |
| Max pressure location | Everywhere (uniform) | At inner radius $r_1$ |

*(Award 1 mark for each valid contrasting point. Full 3 marks for tabulated comparison with formulas.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any six components of a single plate clutch and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Flywheel** – Driving member, provides friction surface.
2. **Friction disc** – Driven member, transmits torque via friction.
3. **Pressure plate** – Applies axial clamping force.
4. **Clutch springs** – Provide engaging force $W$.
5. **Release levers** – Decouple the pressure plate on pedal press.
6. **Driven shaft** – Output shaft receiving torque from the disc hub.
*(1 mark for naming 3 components correctly, 1 mark for naming the next 3, 1 mark for accurate functions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **single plate clutch**. List the standard sequence of mates to be applied in a CAD assembly software (SOLIDWORKS / CATIA / Creo) to assemble the clutch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **torque transmitted by a single plate clutch** based on:
   (i) **Uniform pressure theory**
   (ii) **Uniform wear theory**
   Show the location and expression for **maximum pressure** in each case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Construction & Working (4 marks):**
   - A single plate clutch consists of a flywheel (driving member) bolted to the engine crankshaft, a friction disc with a splined hub engaging the driven (gearbox input) shaft, a pressure plate loaded by 4–6 helical springs, and a clutch cover.
   - When the clutch pedal is released, the springs push the pressure plate axially → friction disc is clamped between flywheel and pressure plate → frictional contact transmits torque from the engine to the gearbox.
   - When the pedal is pressed, the release lever pushes the pressure plate backward against the spring force → the disc is freed → torque transmission ceases (gear shift is possible).

2. **Mating Sequence in CAD (3 marks):**
   - Step 1: Fix the flywheel (ground).
   - Step 2: Insert driven shaft → *Concentric* mate to flywheel bore.
   - Step 3: Insert friction disc → *Coincident* mate (back face to flywheel face).
   - Step 4: *Concentric* mate (disc hub to driven shaft splines).
   - Step 5: Insert pressure plate → *Distance* offset equal to disc thickness.
   - Step 6: Place helical springs → *Concentric* mate to spring pockets.
   - Step 7: Insert clutch cover → *Coincident* bolt-pattern mate to flywheel.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Uniform Pressure Theory (3.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{2}{3} \, \mu \, W \cdot \dfrac{r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3}}{r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}} \\[4pt]
P_{max} &= \dfrac{2W}{\pi (r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2})}
\end{aligned}
$$

Derivation steps as given in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

**(ii) Uniform Wear Theory (3.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{1}{2} \, \mu \, W \, (r_1 + r_2) \\[4pt]
