P_{max} &= \dfrac{2W}{\pi \, r_1 \, (r_2 - r_1)} \quad \text{(at } r = r_1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Derivation steps as given in **Section 3.2** of these notes.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**(a)** A single plate clutch with a single friction surface is to be designed using the **uniform wear theory** to transmit $15 \,\text{kW}$ at $1500 \,\text{rpm}$. The coefficient of friction is $0.3$. The inner radius of the friction lining is $40 \,\text{mm}$ and the outer radius is $80 \,\text{mm}$. Calculate:
   (i) The required **axial spring force** $W$.
   (ii) The **maximum pressure** on the lining.
   (iii) The **number of springs** required if each spring provides $500 \,\text{N}$ of force. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain the **step-by-step procedure to create an assembly model of a single plate clutch** in any CAD package, and list the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** entries typically generated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Required torque from power equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{60 \times 15000}{2\pi \times 1500} = \dfrac{900000}{9424.78} \approx 95.49 \,\text{N·m} = 95493 \,\text{N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply uniform wear torque formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
95493 &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3 \times W \times (40 + 80) \\[4pt]
95493 &= 0.15 \times W \times 120 = 18 \, W \\[4pt]
W &= \dfrac{95493}{18} \approx 5305 \,\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= \dfrac{W}{2\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1)} = \dfrac{5305}{2\pi \times 40 \times 40} \\[4pt]
        &= \dfrac{5305}{10053} \approx 0.528 \,\text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of springs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{W}{\text{Force per spring}} = \dfrac{5305}{500} \approx 10.6 \implies \boxed{n = 12 \text{ springs (round up)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Flow Diagram of Assembly Procedure (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Create individual parts in Part Mode] --> S2[Flywheel, Friction Disc, Pressure Plate, Shaft, Cover, Levers, Springs]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Open New Assembly File]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Insert Flywheel and Fix it - Ground]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4: Insert Driven Shaft with Concentric Mate]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Insert Friction Disc with Concentric + Coincident Mates]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Insert Pressure Plate with Distance Offset Mate]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Insert Helical Springs with Concentric Mate]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Insert Release Levers with Pivot Coincident Mate]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Insert Clutch Cover with Coincident Bolt Pattern]
    S10 --> S11[Step 10: Verify DOF equals 0]
    S11 --> S12[Step 11: Create Exploded View]
    S12 --> S13[Step 12: Generate BOM and Orthographic Drawing]
```

**Sample Bill of Materials (BOM):**

| Item No. | Part Name | Qty | Material |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Flywheel | 1 | Cast Iron GG-25 |
| 2 | Friction Disc | 1 | Steel Core + Asbestos Lining |
| 3 | Pressure Plate | 1 | Cast Iron |
| 4 | Helical Spring | 6 | Spring Steel 55Si7 |
| 5 | Release Lever | 3 | Forged Steel |
| 6 | Driven Shaft | 1 | EN8 Alloy Steel |
| 7 | Clutch Cover | 1 | Sheet Steel |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CAD & Analysis Lab Exams:**
> 1. **Mate Order Error (–2 marks):** Students often apply the *Concentric* mate before the *Coincident* mate on the friction disc, which locks the disc at the wrong axial position. Always do **Concentric first, then Coincident** (or Distance).
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 (–2 marks):** In a *double-faced* clutch (friction on both sides of the disc), the torque formula must be **doubled**: $T_{double} = 2 \times T_{single}$. Forgetting this gives half the correct answer.
> 3. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Mixing $\text{N/mm}^2$ with $\text{MPa}$ (they are equal, but examiners want explicit unit declaration) and mixing N·m with N·mm is the #1 silent error.
> 4. **No DOF Verification (–1 mark):** A clutch assembly with non-zero DOF is considered *invalid* by examiners — always run a *Move Component* check.
> 5. **Uniform Pressure assumed wrongly (–1 mark):** Unless the question explicitly says "new clutch", the safer default is **Uniform Wear Theory** for KTU problems.
> 6. **Missing scale or title block on the drafted 2D drawing (–2 marks):** The 2D draft must include a **title block, scale, third-angle projection symbol, BOM, and tolerances** as per KTU drafting standards.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet Snapshot:**

- ✅ **Assembly Modeling** = combining part files using geometric *mates* (Concentric, Coincident, Distance, Angle, Tangent).
- ✅ Always start assembly by **fixing one component** as ground (usually the flywheel in a clutch).
- ✅ Recommended mate order: **Concentric → Coincident → Distance Offset**.
- ✅ A **Single Plate Clutch** transmits torque via **kinetic friction** between the flywheel and the friction disc.
- ✅ Two governing theories:
  - **Uniform Pressure (new clutch):** $T = \dfrac{2}{3} \mu W \cdot \dfrac{r_2^{\,3} - r_1^{\,3}}{r_2^{\,2} - r_1^{\,2}}$
  - **Uniform Wear (worn clutch):** $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu W (r_1 + r_2)$
- ✅ Maximum pressure location: Uniform Pressure → **everywhere**; Uniform Wear → **at inner radius $r_1$**.
- ✅ For uniform wear to be valid, the design must satisfy **$r_2 \leq 2 r_1$** (aspect ratio rule).
- ✅ Standard coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.25$ to $0.40$ depending on lining material.
- ✅ Standard safe unit pressure: $p = 0.10$ to $0.35 \,\text{N/mm}^2$.
- ✅ **Double-faced clutches** double the torque capacity: $T_{double} = 2 \times T_{single}$.
- ✅ Always **verify DOF = 0** before submitting the assembly.
- ✅ Generate an **exploded view** + **BOM** + **2D orthographic draft** with proper title block.
- ✅ Most-used CAD commands: *Extrude Boss/Base*, *Revolve*, *Circular Pattern*, *Fillet*, *Chamfer*, *Mate*, *Move Component*, *Section View*.
- ✅ Power-Torque relationship: $P(\text{kW}) = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60000}$ with $T$ in N·m.
- ✅ Examiner's golden rule: **Always state the theory assumed and the design constraint used (e.g., $r_2 \leq 2 r_1$).** No assumption = no full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Cone Friction Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Cone Friction Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cone Friction Clutch** is a positive-engagement friction device in which the driving (male) and driven (female) members are truncated cones that transmit rotational torque through frictional contact on their inclined mating surfaces. The frictional engagement force is generated by an axial thrust (provided by a spring, lever, or hydraulic actuator) that presses the cones together along the axis of the shaft.

In the context of CAD/CAE laboratory practice (PCMUL507), the term encompasses two engineering deliverables:
- **Assembly Model**: A hierarchical, constraint-based digital reconstruction of the clutch, in which each individual part (male cone, female cone, spring, operating lever, bearings, housing) is modeled parametrically and assembled with geometric mates.
- **Drafting (Engineering Drawing)**: The generation of orthographic views, sectional views, and an exploded pictorial view with dimensions, tolerances, surface finish symbols, and a Bill of Materials (BOM), produced from the 3D assembly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Braked Bicycle Wheel

Imagine pressing the side of a spinning bicycle wheel with a tilted cup-shaped object (a coffee mug). The harder you push the mug axially into the rim, the more torque the rim can transmit before slipping. The slant of the mug converts your small push into a huge normal force and hence huge friction. This is precisely how a **cone clutch** works — the **semi-cone angle (α)** is the geometric amplifier that converts a modest axial force into a large frictional torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMUL507 — Module 2):**
> Students are expected to (a) model individual components of a friction clutch, (b) assemble them using bottom-up or top-down methodology in any standard 3D CAD package (SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo Parametric, Siemens NX, Fusion 360), and (c) generate a 2D engineering drawing sheet with proper sectional views, dimensions, and BOM.

### 1.3 Key Components of a Cone Friction Clutch Assembly

| # | Part Name | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Male Cone (Driving Member)** | Tapered cone rigidly fixed to the driving shaft; carries the friction lining. |
| 2 | **Female Cone (Driven Member)** | Conical socket rigidly fixed to the driven shaft/pulley. |
| 3 | **Friction Lining** | Wear-resistant material (asbestos, sintered bronze, ceramic) bonded to the male cone. |
| 4 | **Thrust Spring (Belleville / Helical)** | Provides the engaging axial force. |
| 5 | **Operating Lever / Fork** | Mechanically compresses the spring to disengage. |
| 6 | **Clutch Shaft** | Transmits torque from prime-mover. |
| 7 | **Driven Shaft / Pulley Hub** | Carries the driven member. |
| 8 | **Bearing (Ball/Roller)** | Supports radial loads and allows free rotation when disengaged. |
| 9 | **Housing / Yoke** | Encloses the assembly and provides reaction surface. |

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU 2024 Scheme)

- **Semi-cone angle (α)**: typically **12° – 15°** for self-energizing action.
- **Coefficient of friction (μ)**: **0.25 – 0.4** for asbestos/sintered lining on steel.
- **Mean cone radius (R_m)**: governed by torque requirement and space envelope.
- **Standard SI Units**: Force in **N (Newton)**, Torque in **N·m**, Pressure in **Pa (N/m²)** or **N/mm² (MPa)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Energization Phenomenon:** When the cone's semi-cone angle is small, the friction force adds to the applied axial force, amplifying the contact pressure. This is the principle of "self-energization" unique to cone (and disc) clutches and is a favourite viva question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design Theories for Cone Clutches

The pressure distribution between the contacting conical surfaces is unknown a priori. Two classical hypotheses are used:

**(a) Uniform Pressure Theory**
Assumes the normal pressure `p` is constant over the entire contact area. Used for **new clutches** with rigid, freshly ground surfaces.

**(b) Uniform Wear Theory**
Assumes the rate of wear is uniform, so `p × r = constant (C)`. Used for **engaged clutches after running-in** and is the more conservative, recommended design basis.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear | Unit |
|---|-----------|------------------|--------------|------|
| 1 | Torque `T` | $$\frac{2\pi\mu p}{3\sin\alpha}\left(R_2^3 - R_1^3\right)$$ | $$\frac{\pi\mu C}{\sin\alpha}\left(R_2^2 - R_1^2\right)$$ | N·m |
| 2 | Torque `T` (in terms of P_a) | $$\frac{2\mu P_a}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{R_2^3 - R_1^3}{R_2^2 - R_1^2}$$ | $$\frac{\mu P_a\, R_m}{\sin\alpha}$$ | N·m |
| 3 | Axial Force `P_a` | $$\pi p\left(R_2^2 - R_1^2\right)$$ | $$2\pi C\left(R_2 - R_1\right)$$ | N |
| 4 | Max Pressure `p_max` | $$p$$ (uniform) | $$\dfrac{C}{R_1}$$ | N/m² |
| 5 | Mean Radius `R_m` | $$\dfrac{2\left(R_2^3 - R_1^3\right)}{3\left(R_2^2 - R_1^2\right)}$$ | $$\dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2}$$ | m |
| 6 | Self-energizing Condition | $$\tan\alpha < \mu$$ (friction holds the cone in) | Same | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols Used (no vertical pipe inside):** `R₁` = inner cone radius, `R₂` = outer cone radius, `α` = semi-cone angle, `μ` = coefficient of friction, `p` = normal pressure, `P_a` = axial engaging force, `C` = wear constant = `p_max · R₁`, `R_m` = mean radius.

### 2.3 Why a Cone Geometry is used (Engineering Utility)

- **Torque Amplification:** Because `T = μP_a R / sinα`, smaller `α` produces larger torque for the same axial force — a geometric "lever" effect.
- **Compact Design:** A cone clutch of a given torque capacity occupies less axial space than a single-plate clutch.
- **Self-Locking Capability:** If `α < arctan(μ)`, the clutch is **self-energizing** — once engaged, friction alone prevents slippage even if the axial force is removed.
- **Smooth Engagement:** The gradual surface contact reduces shock loading.
- **Real-World Applications:** Machine-tool spindle drives, automobile differential locks, helicopter rotor drives, heavy-duty conveyor drives.

### 2.4 CAD Modeling Rationale

In a parametric CAD environment, the cone is created as a **revolved feature** of a right triangle about the shaft axis. The hypotenuse of the triangle becomes the friction surface. The semi-cone angle is therefore a directly editable design parameter, making the clutch a textbook case-study for demonstrating **parametric, feature-based modeling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Always design using **Uniform Wear Theory** because (i) running-in always occurs, (ii) it gives lower predicted pressure (safer), and (iii) it matches the empirical behaviour of operational clutches.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Torque Capacity — Uniform Pressure Theory

**Step 1 — Geometry setup**
Consider a small annular ring element of the conical friction surface at radius `r`, thickness `dr`, and semi-cone angle `α`.

**Step 2 — Projected horizontal area of the ring**
The ring lies on the inclined surface. Its projection onto the plane perpendicular to the axis is:
$$dA_{\text{projected}} = 2\pi r\, dr$$

**Step 3 — Actual area on the inclined cone surface**
The slant of the cone enlarges the surface area:
$$dA_{\text{cone}} = \frac{2\pi r\, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Normal force on the ring element**
With uniform pressure `p`:
$$dN = p \cdot dA_{\text{cone}} = \frac{2\pi p\, r\, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 5 — Friction force on the ring element**
$$dF = \mu \cdot dN = \frac{2\pi \mu p\, r\, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 6 — Frictional torque contribution of the ring**
The friction force acts tangentially at radius `r`, so:
$$dT = dF \cdot r = \frac{2\pi \mu p\, r^{2}\, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 7 — Total torque (integrate from R₁ to R₂)**
$$T = \int_{R_1}^{R_2} \frac{2\pi \mu p\, r^{2}}{\sin\alpha}\, dr$$

**Step 8 — Carry out the integration**
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2\pi \mu p}{\sin\alpha}\int_{R_1}^{R_2} r^{2}\, dr \\
  &= \frac{2\pi \mu p}{\sin\alpha}\left[\frac{r^{3}}{3}\right]_{R_1}^{R_2} \\
  &= \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3\sin\alpha}\left(R_2^{3} - R_1^{3}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9 — Express in terms of axial force P_a**
Axial force = integral of the horizontal component of `dN`:
$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \int_{R_1}^{R_2} dN \sin\alpha = \int_{R_1}^{R_2} 2\pi p\, r\, dr \\
    &= 2\pi p \left[\frac{r^{2}}{2}\right]_{R_1}^{R_2} \\
    &= \pi p\left(R_2^{2} - R_1^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore `p = P_a / [\pi(R_2^{2} - R_1^{2})]`. Substituting into the torque expression:
$$T = \frac{2\mu P_a}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{R_2^{3} - R_1^{3}}{R_2^{2} - R_1^{2}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Torque Capacity — Uniform Wear Theory

**Step 1 — Wear condition**
Uniform wear ⇒ `p · r = C` (constant), hence `p = C/r`.

**Step 2 — Normal force on the ring element**
$$dN = \frac{p \cdot 2\pi r\, dr}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{2\pi C\, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Friction force**
$$dF = \mu\, dN = \frac{2\pi \mu C\, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Torque contribution**
$$dT = dF \cdot r = \frac{2\pi \mu C\, r\, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from R₁ to R₂**
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\int_{R_1}^{R_2} r\, dr \\
  &= \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\left[\frac{r^{2}}{2}\right]_{R_1}^{R_2} \\
  &= \frac{\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\left(R_2^{2} - R_1^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum pressure at the inner radius**
Maximum pressure occurs at the smallest radius:
$$p_{\max} = \frac{C}{R_1} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C = p_{\max} R_1$$

**Step 7 — Express torque in terms of p_max**
$$T = \frac{\pi \mu\, p_{\max} R_1}{\sin\alpha}\left(R_2^{2} - R_1^{2}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Axial force**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \int_{R_1}^{R_2} 2\pi p\, r\, dr = 2\pi \int_{R_1}^{R_2} C\, dr \\
    &= 2\pi C\left(R_2 - R_1\right) = 2\pi p_{\max} R_1\left(R_2 - R_1\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9 — Substitute back**
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{\pi \mu}{\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{P_a}{2\pi(R_2 - R_1)}\cdot(R_2^{2} - R_1^{2}) \\
  &= \frac{\mu P_a}{2\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{(R_2 - R_1)(R_2 + R_1)}{R_2 - R_1} \\
  &= \frac{\mu P_a\,(R_1 + R_2)}{2\sin\alpha} \\
  &= \frac{\mu P_a\, R_m}{\sin\alpha}
\end{aligned}$$

This compact form is the most important KTU formula for cone clutches.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- Semi-cone angle `α = 12°`, `μ = 0.3`
- Outer diameter `D_2 = 300 mm` ⇒ `R_2 = 150 mm = 0.15 m`
- Inner diameter `D_1 = 200 mm` ⇒ `R_1 = 100 mm = 0.10 m`
- Axial engaging force `P_a = 1000 N`
- Use **Uniform Wear** theory

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin 12° &= 0.2079 \\
R_m &= \frac{R_1 + R_2}{2} = \frac{0.10 + 0.15}{2} = 0.125 \text{ m} \\
T &= \frac{\mu P_a R_m}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{0.3 \times 1000 \times 0.125}{0.2079} \\
  &= \frac{37.5}{0.2079} \approx 180.4 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum normal pressure:**
$$\begin{aligned}
p_{\max} R_1 &= \frac{P_a}{2\pi(R_2 - R_1)} = \frac{1000}{2\pi \times 0.05} = 3183.1 \\
p_{\max} &= \frac{3183.1}{0.10} = 31831 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.0318 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Since `p_max` < allowable pressure of the lining (≈ 1 MPa for asbestos), the design is safe.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Cone Clutch Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Prime Mover Electric Motor / Engine] --> B[Driving Shaft Input]
    B --> C[Male Cone Driver Member with Friction Lining]
    C --> D[Frictional Contact Cone-Cone Interface Semi-Angle Alpha]
    D --> E[Female Cone Driven Member Pulley Hub]
    E --> F[Driven Shaft Output to Load]
    G[Belleville or Helical Thrust Spring] -->|Axial Force P_a| C
    H[Operating Lever or Hydraulic Actuator] -->|Releases Engagement| G
    I[Ball or Tapered Roller Bearing] -->|Radial Support| B
    J[Cast Iron Housing Yoke] -->|Reaction Surface| G
    K[FEM Analysis in ANSYS or SolidWorks Simulation] -.->|Contact Pressure Check| D
    L[Drafting Ortho and Sectional Views with BOM] -.->|Manufacturing Output| C
```

### 4.2 CAD Assembly Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify Components from Drawing] --> S2[Step 2 Parametric Modeling of Each Part]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Apply Material e.g. Cast Iron, Bronze]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Bottom-Up Assembly Insert Parts]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Apply Mates Coincident Concentric Tangent]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Verify Degrees of Freedom Zero DOF]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Run Interference Detection]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Create Exploded View]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Generate Drafting Sheet]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Add Ortho Sectional and Auxiliary Views]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Insert Dimensions Tolerances and Surface Finish]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Generate Bill of Materials BOM and Title Block]
```

### 4.3 Geometric Construction of the Cone (Revolve Profile)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Start a New Part File and Select a Front Plane] --> P2[Sketch the Right Triangular Profile with Vertex at Axis]
    P2 --> P3[Define Vertical Leg = Half Cone Width R2 minus R1]
    P3 --> P4[Define Horizontal Leg = Cone Half Length = R2 minus R1 over tan alpha]
    P4 --> P5[Hypotenuse automatically inclined at semi-cone angle alpha]
    P5 --> P6[Revolve Feature Boss about the shaft axis 360 degrees]
    P6 --> P7[Apply Fillet to the cone tip for stress relief]
    P7 --> P8[Female Cone created similarly as a Revolved Cut or Shell]
    P8 --> P9[Save Part and Add to Assembly Library]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Sectional View Description (for mental visualization)

```
        +-------------------------+   <-- Driven Shaft (Output)
        |     [FEMALE CONE]       |
        |       \               / |
        |        \             /  |
        |         \  CONTACT /    |   <-- Friction Lining (hatched)
        |          \   ZONE  /    |
        |           \      /      |
        |     [MALE  \    /  CONE]
        |     CONE]   \  /        |
        |             \/  <-α->   |
        |             /\          |
        |        SPRING||         |   <-- Thrust Spring (helical)
        |             ||          |
        +--------|HOUSING|--------+
                 (REACTION)
                  ↑ LEVER
```
*(ASCII schematic — actual drafting is generated in CAD with proper ANSI/ISO hatching on the cut surfaces, GD&T, and a centre line marked "CL".)*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a cone friction clutch. Why is its semi-cone angle kept small (typically 12°–15°)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A cone friction clutch is a friction device in which the driving and driven members have matching truncated conical surfaces that transmit torque by friction. The torque capacity is given by `T = μP_a R / sinα`. Since `T` is inversely proportional to `sinα`, a smaller semi-cone angle produces a much larger torque for the same axial force `P_a`. This makes the clutch compact and self-energizing when `α < arctan(μ)`, allowing it to remain engaged even with reduced spring force. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the difference between uniform pressure and uniform wear theories as applied to cone clutches.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Uniform Pressure Theory** assumes the normal pressure `p` is constant over the entire contact area. It is applicable to a *new* clutch with rigid, freshly ground surfaces.
- **Uniform Wear Theory** assumes the rate of wear is uniform, giving `p·r = C` (constant). The pressure is therefore maximum at the inner radius `R_1` and decreases hyperbolically outward. It is applicable to a *run-in* clutch and is used as the design basis because it predicts lower, safer pressures. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**A 14-Mark Question on cone clutch design and CAD modeling.**

**(a)** A single-cone clutch has a semi-cone angle of 12° and operates with a coefficient of friction of 0.28. The clutch is to transmit 1.2 kW at 900 rpm. The mean cone radius is 80 mm and the width of the friction surface is 40 mm. Determine:
  (i) The axial force required to engage the clutch, and
  (ii) The maximum intensity of pressure on the contact surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **bottom-up assembly** methodology used in any standard 3D CAD package (SolidWorks/Creo/CATIA) to assemble a cone friction clutch. List the geometric mates used between (i) shaft and male cone, (ii) male cone and female cone, and (iii) spring and housing. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical solution — Uniform Wear Theory**

Power `P = 1.2 kW = 1200 W`, speed `N = 900 rpm`.

Torque required:
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{60 P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 1200}{2\pi \times 900} \\
  &= \frac{72000}{5654.87} \approx 12.73 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

Given: `R_m = 80 mm = 0.08 m`, `α = 12°`, `μ = 0.28`, width `b = 40 mm = 0.04 m`.

For a cone, `b = (R_2 - R_1)/\sinα` ⇒ `R_2 - R_1 = b \sinα = 0.04 \times 0.2079 = 8.316 \times 10^{-3}` m.

Also, `R_m = (R_1 + R_2)/2 = 0.08 m`.

From the two equations:
$$R_1 = 0.08 - \frac{8.316 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.07584 \text{ m}$$
$$R_2 = 0.08 + \frac{8.316 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 0.08416 \text{ m}$$

**(i) Axial force** using `T = μP_a R_m / \sinα`:
$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \frac{T \sin\alpha}{\mu R_m} = \frac{12.73 \times 0.2079}{0.28 \times 0.08} \\
    &= \frac{2.647}{0.0224} \approx 118.2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Maximum pressure** using `p_max = P_a / [2πR_1(R_2 - R_1)]`:
$$\begin{aligned}
p_{\max} &= \frac{118.2}{2\pi \times 0.07584 \times 8.316 \times 10^{-3}} \\
         &= \frac{118.2}{3.962 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 29834 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.0298 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Both results are within practical limits. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Bottom-up Assembly Methodology — Step-by-Step Description (7 marks):**

1. **Part Modeling Stage** — Each component is modeled separately as a `.sldprt` (SolidWorks) or `.prt` (Creo) file.
   - Male cone: revolve of a triangular profile.
   - Female cone: revolve + shell or cut-extrude.
   - Spring: helix sweep.
   - Shaft: revolve of a stepped profile.
   - Lever: sweep or lofted feature.
2. **Open a New Assembly File** and insert the first fixed component (typically the **housing or driver shaft**) and set it as the origin-fixed (grounded) component.
3. **Insert Subsequent Parts** using `Insert Component`.
4. **Apply Geometric Mates** to constrain degrees of freedom (DOF):
   - *(i) Shaft ↔ Male Cone:* **Concentric** + **Coincident** of the cone's back face with a shaft shoulder.
   - *(ii) Male Cone ↔ Female Cone:* **Concentric** (axial alignment) + **Coincident** at the cone tip OR a specified axial distance mate.
   - *(iii) Spring ↔ Housing:* **Concentric** of the spring's mean diameter with the housing boss + **Coincident** of the spring's end coils with the housing seat.
5. **Validate** the assembly has **zero DOF**; the model is now kinematically locked.
6. **Interference Detection** is run to ensure no overlap of the cones in the *disengaged* state.
7. **Exploded View** is created using the `Explode` tool along the assembly axis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**An alternative 14-Mark Question covering theory + drafting.**

**(a)** A cone clutch transmits 25 kW at 1500 rpm. The semi-cone angle is 13° and the coefficient of friction is 0.3. The maximum axial pressure is 0.1 MPa. If the cone face width is 50 mm, calculate:
  (i) The required mean radius,
  (ii) The axial force needed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with the aid of a properly labelled sketch, the **drafting procedure** to convert a 3D assembly model of a cone clutch into a 2D manufacturing drawing. What views, sectioning conventions, and annotations are mandatory as per BIS/ISO standards? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical solution — Uniform Wear Theory**

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{60 P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 25000}{2\pi \times 1500} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{6}}{9424.78} \\
  &\approx 159.15 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

Given: `α = 13°`, `μ = 0.3`, `p_max = 0.1 \text{ MPa} = 10^{5} \text{ N/m}^{2}`, `b = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}`.

From `b = (R_2 - R_1)/\sinα`:
$$R_2 - R_1 = b \sin\alpha = 0.05 \times \sin 13° = 0.05 \times 0.2250 = 0.01125 \text{ m}$$

Uniform wear: `p_max · R_1 = C` and `P_a = 2πC(R_2 - R_1)`.
Torque: `T = (πμC/\sinα)(R_2^{2} - R_1^{2}) = (πμC/\sinα)(R_2 - R_1)(R_1 + R_2)`.

Substituting `C = p_max R_1`:
$$T = \frac{\pi \mu\, p_{\max} R_1 (R_2 - R_1)(R_1 + R_2)}{\sin\alpha}$$

Let `R_1 + R_2 = 2R_m`. Then:
$$T = \frac{2\pi \mu\, p_{\max} R_1 R_m (R_2 - R_1)}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Strategy:** iterate over `R_1` and `R_2` satisfying `R_2 - R_1 = 0.01125` and the above torque equation.

Try `R_m = 0.10 m` (i.e., `R_1 = 0.0944`, `R_2 = 0.1056` m):
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2\pi \times 0.3 \times 10^{5} \times 0.0944 \times 0.10 \times 0.01125}{\sin 13°} \\
  &= \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.3 \times 10^{5} \times 1.062 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2250} \\
  &= \frac{20.01}{0.2250} \approx 88.9 \text{ N·m} \quad (\text{too low})
\end{aligned}$$

Try `R_m = 0.13 m` (i.e., `R_1 = 0.1244`, `R_2 = 0.1356` m):
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2\pi \times 0.3 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1244 \times 0.13 \times 0.01125}{0.2250} \\
  &= \frac{34.5}{0.2250} \approx 153.4 \text{ N·m} \quad (\text{close, slightly low})
\end{aligned}$$

Try `R_m = 0.133 m` ⇒ `R_1 = 0.1274`, `R_2 = 0.1386` m:
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2\pi \times 0.3 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1274 \times 0.133 \times 0.01125}{0.2250} \\
  &\approx 159.2 \text{ N·m} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**(i) Mean radius:** `R_m ≈ 133 mm` **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Axial force:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= 2\pi p_{\max} R_1 (R_2 - R_1) = 2\pi \times 10^{5} \times 0.1274 \times 0.01125 \\
    &= 2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{5} \times 1.433 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &\approx 900.3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) `P_a ≈ 900 N`** **(4 marks)** — Total = **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Drafting Procedure from 3D to 2D Drawing (7 marks):**

1. **Open the Assembly File** in the CAD package. Launch the `Drawing` environment and select an **ISO A2 or A3 sheet size** with a pre-defined title block and border.

2. **Insert Standard Views** by projecting from the assembly model:
   - **Front view** (along X-axis) — primary view, shows the cone profiles in section.
   - **Top view** (along Z-axis) — shows the circular cross-section of the female cone.
   - **Side / Left view** (along Y-axis) — auxiliary or right-side elevation.
   - **Isometric / Pictorial view** — usually placed in the upper-right corner to show the 3D assembly.
   - **Exploded view** — to illustrate part separation and assembly sequence.

3. **Apply Sectioning (BIS 6963 / ISO 128-50):**
   - Use a **full section (clipped section)** through the axis of the clutch so the **friction cone engagement** is clearly visible.
   - Hatching: cast iron at **45°** with closely-spaced lines, bronze/bronze-alloy at **different angle/spacing** to differentiate materials.
   - **Centre line** must pass through the shaft axis.

4. **Add Dimensions** (BIS 919 / ISO 129):
   - **Linear dimensions** for cone face width `b`, outer diameter `D_2`, inner diameter `D_1`, total axial length.
   - **Angular dimension** for the semi-cone angle `α`.
   - **Leader-line callouts** for the lining material, semi-cone angle, and surface finish.

5. **Add Annotations:**
   - **Surface finish symbols** (√, √Ra) on the friction surface, e.g., `Ra 0.8`.
   - **GD&T** (geometric dimensioning & tolerancing) per ISO 1101 — concentricity, cylindricity, run-out.
   - **Datum references** (A, B, C) on the shaft and the housing face.

6. **Insert the BOM (Bill of Materials)** in the upper-right corner or as a table on the sheet:
   - Item No., Part Name, Material, Qty., Part Number, Remarks.
   - **Balloon numbers** linking each item in the exploded view to the BOM.

7. **Title Block** in the lower-right:
   - Project Title, Drawn By, Checked By, Approved By, Scale (e.g., 1:2), Sheet No., Date, Drawing No.

8. **Final check:** run `Audit` and `Standard Check` to ensure all views are on the sheet, the section line is correctly drawn, and the drawing passes the company's drawing-office checklist. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong formula applied** — Mixing up uniform pressure vs uniform wear formulas. The question will explicitly state which theory to use; re-read carefully.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — `R_1`, `R_2` in mm while pressure is in MPa. Convert everything to SI (m, Pa) BEFORE substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s** when computing torque from power: `T = P × 60 / (2πN)`.
> 4. **In drafting, missing the section view** of the cone engagement — this is *mandatory* and carries 2 marks alone.
> 5. **BOM without balloon numbers** — KTU strict deducts 1 mark for a BOM that is not properly referenced to the assembly.
> 6. **Not stating the self-energizing condition** (`α < arctan μ`) — a 1-mark bonus usually given to students who mention it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **cone friction clutch** uses inclined conical surfaces to amplify axial engaging force into a large frictional torque.
- [x] The governing equation under **Uniform Wear** is `T = μP_a R_m / sinα` — memorize this in SI units.
- [x] Under **Uniform Pressure**, torque is `T = (2πμp / 3sinα)(R_2^{3} - R_1^{3})`.
- [x] **Mean radius** under uniform wear is the arithmetic mean `(R_1 + R_2)/2`; under uniform pressure it is the ratio of cubed to squared terms.
- [x] **Self-energizing condition:** `tanα < μ`; a small semi-cone angle (12°–15°) is standard.
- [x] Always convert RPM to rad/s and use `P (W) = 2πNT/60` when back-calculating torque.
- [x] For **CAD assembly**, prefer **bottom-up** modelling: model each part, insert into assembly, apply geometric mates.
- [x] Validate the assembly has **zero degrees of freedom** before exporting a drawing.
- [x] The **drafting sheet** must contain: ortho views (3), one **sectional view through the axis**, an **isometric or exploded view**, complete **dimensions**, **surface finish** and **GD&T** symbols, a **BOM with balloon callouts**, and a fully filled **title block**.
- [x] In the lab record, **screenshots of the model tree, mate list, and the final sheet** are mandatory as per KTU 2024 Scheme viva requirements.
- [x] Friction lining materials commonly assumed: asbestos (μ ≈ 0.3), sintered bronze (μ ≈ 0.2), ceramic (μ ≈ 0.4).
- [x] Allowable contact pressure on the lining: typically **0.35–1.0 MPa** depending on material.
- [x] Always check **interference clearance** in the disengaged state using the CAD's interference-detection tool.
- [x] For FEA/CAE cross-check (next experiment in Module 3), the contact pressure `p_max` calculated here should be used as the boundary condition in ANSYS/ABAQUS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Diesel Engine Piston
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Diesel Engine Piston

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Assembly Modeling — Formal Definition

**Assembly Modeling** is the process in Computer Aided Design (CAD) where multiple independently created **part models** are brought together into a single parent file, and their relative positions, orientations, and physical relationships are locked using a set of geometric constraints called **mates** (in SolidWorks), **constraints** (in CATIA), or **assembly relations** (in Creo/NX).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Assembly modeling is the CAD operation that combines two or more part files using geometric relationships (coincident, concentric, distance, angle, parallel, tangent) to simulate the kinematic and static behavior of a real product sub-system, producing a single multi-body model that can be used for interference checking, animation, mass-property evaluation, and drawing generation.

## 1.2 Drafting — Formal Definition

**Drafting (Engineering Drawing)** in a CAD environment is the generation of **2D orthographic, sectional, auxiliary, and detail views** from a 3D part or assembly model, complete with dimensions, tolerances, surface-finish symbols, datums, GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing), title block, parts list (BOM), and revision history, conforming to **ASME Y14.5-2018** or **ISO 128 / ISO 1101** standards.

## 1.3 Diesel Engine Piston — Component Overview

The **Diesel Engine Piston** is a reciprocating component that converts the pressure energy of combustion gases into linear mechanical motion, transmitted through a **gudgeon pin (wrist pin)** to the connecting rod.

| Sub-Component | Functional Role | Typical Feature in CAD |
|---|---|---|
| **Crown** | Top face exposed to combustion; often has a bowl in DI diesels | Conical / re-entrant cavity |
| **Ring Grooves** | House compression and oil-control rings | 3 to 4 rectangular slots |
| **Lands** | Thin walls between ring grooves | Cylindrical bands |
| **Skirt** | Guides the piston in the cylinder bore | Open / full barrel shape |
| **Pin Boss** | Supports the gudgeon pin load | Two internal cylindrical hubs |
| **Pin Bore** | Bearing surface for the gudgeon pin | Through hole in the boss |
| **Internal Ribs** | Provide rigidity to the crown | Web-like internal webs |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab record for this experiment typically includes a minimum of **6 to 10 sub-components** in the final assembly: Piston Body, Piston Ring-1, Piston Ring-2, Oil Scraper Ring, Gudgeon Pin, Circlip × 2, and sometimes a partial connecting rod and cylinder liner segment for assembly validation.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"Lego Blocks with Glue" Analogy:**
> Think of assembly modeling as **building with Lego blocks**, but the blocks don't just snap together — you must also *declare* the rule of their connection: *"this face of block A must always sit flat on the top face of block B"* (Coincident), or *"the central axis of the cylindrical block must pass through the center of the hole in the other block"* (Concentric).
>
> **Drafting** is what you draw on a **2D blueprint sheet** so that a machinist in a workshop — who has never seen your 3D model — can manufacture the part to the exact size and tolerance. A 3D model is for engineers; a 2D draft is for the shop floor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Assembly Design Strategies

| Strategy | Direction | When Used | CAD Command Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bottom-Up Assembly** | Parts → Sub-assembly → Assembly | When all parts are fully designed | SolidWorks: Insert Components |
| **Top-Down Assembly** | Assembly skeleton → Parts derived from layout | When the spatial envelope drives part design | SolidWorks: Layout Sketch |
| **Middle-Out (Hybrid)** | Mix of both | Most industrial practice | Combination |

## 2.2 Standard Assembly Mates / Constraints

1. **Coincident Mate** — Two faces lie in the same infinite plane.
2. **Concentric Mate** — Two cylindrical / circular edges share a common axis.
3. **Distance Mate** — Faces / edges are separated by an exact **offset value**.
4. **Angle Mate** — Non-default angular relationship between two planes.
5. **Tangent Mate** — A curved face touches a plane or another curve at one point.
6. **Parallel / Perpendicular Mate** — Plane-to-plane orientation.
7. **Symmetric Mate** — Two faces are mirror images about a reference plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A fully defined assembly has **zero degrees of freedom** between all its components. If any component can still be dragged freely, the assembly is **under-defined** and will be marked down.

## 2.3 Drafting Standards — What to Apply

| Element | ASME Y14.5-2018 | ISO 128 / 1101 |
|---|---|---|
| Line types | Thick (visible), thin (hidden), chain (center) | Same |
| Dimensioning | Decimal inches / mm | mm only |
| Tolerance | Plus-minus or GD&T symbols | ISO tolerances (IT grades) |
| Section view | Hatching at **45°**, 2 mm spacing | Same |
| Title block | Bottom-right corner, multi-cell | Right or bottom |
| BOM | Above title block, ballooned callouts | Parts list with item numbers |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Limits, Fits & Tolerance Stack-Up

> [!NOTE]
> The diesel engine piston has **precision running fits** with the cylinder bore (H7/g6) and with the gudgeon pin (H6/h5 or H7/g6). The following formulas are the core analytical tools for the viva.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fundamental Deviation** (shaft, lower case) | $ei = \text{lower deviation}$ | mm | Fit calculation |
| 2 | **Fundamental Deviation** (hole, upper case) | $ES = \text{upper deviation}$ | mm | Fit calculation |
| 3 | **Maximum Clearance** | $C_{max} = ES - ei$ | mm | Worst-case loose |
| 4 | **Minimum Clearance** | $C_{min} = EI - es$ | mm | Worst-case tight |
| 5 | **Tolerance of Hole** | $T_H = ES - EI$ | mm | Hole tolerance band |
| 6 | **Tolerance of Shaft** | $T_s = es - ei$ | mm | Shaft tolerance band |
| 7 | **Type of Fit** | $C_{min} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Clearance Fit}$ | — | Selection check |
| 8 | **Worst-Case Stack-Up** | $T_{wc} = \sum T_i$ | mm | Linear tolerancing |
| 9 | **Statistical (RSS) Stack-Up** | $T_{rss} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ | mm | Probabilistic |
| 10 | **Piston-Cylinder Clearance** | $C = D_b - D_p$ | mm | Design check |
| 11 | **Piston Pin Bearing Pressure** | $p = \dfrac{F_b}{L_p \times d_p}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Pin boss check |
| 12 | **Piston Pin Bending Stress** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{F_b \cdot L_p}{0.785 \cdot d_p^3}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Failure check |
| 13 | **Section Line Spacing** | $s = 2\text{ to } 3 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Drafting practice |
| 14 | **Sheet Scale Ratio** | $S = L_{\text{drawing}} / L_{\text{actual}}$ | unitless | Drawing scale |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Interference Detection:** Assembly modeling in SolidWorks/CATIA performs clash-detection in milliseconds, which is impossible on a physical prototype.
- **Mass Properties:** The total mass, center of gravity, and moment of inertia of the piston assembly are automatically extracted for **crank-slider dynamics simulation**.
- **2D Drafts as Legal Documents:** A drafted piston drawing is a **contractual artifact** — the manufacturer is legally bound to deliver the part to the dimensions and GD&T specified in the drawing.
- **Tolerance Loop Closure:** In the piston-cylinder-pin-rod assembly, tolerance stack-up determines whether the engine can be assembled at all; a small stack-up error here is what causes **engine seizure** in production.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tolerance Stack-Up Along the Piston Pin Axis

> **Problem:** The piston pin passes through (i) the piston pin boss on the left, (ii) the connecting rod small-end, and (iii) the piston pin boss on the right. Each interface has a clearance gap. If the connecting rod small-end width is $W_r = 40.00 \pm 0.05$ mm, and the piston pin boss widths are $W_1 = 18.00 \pm 0.03$ mm and $W_2 = 18.00 \pm 0.03$ mm, compute the **worst-case axial play** of the gudgeon pin.

**Step 1 — Define the assembly loop.** The axial play (free space) along the pin axis is the space not occupied by the three components.

$$\Delta = W_1 + W_r + W_2$$

**Step 2 — Identify the tolerances.** Each dimension has an independent tolerance:
- $W_1 = 18.00 \pm 0.03$ mm
- $W_r = 40.00 \pm 0.05$ mm
- $W_2 = 18.00 \pm 0.03$ mm

**Step 3 — Compute the nominal (mean) play.**

$$\Delta_{nom} = 18.00 + 40.00 + 18.00 = 76.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the worst-case (arithmetic) stack-up rule.**

$$T_{wc} = \sum T_i = 0.03 + 0.05 + 0.03 = 0.11 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta_{max} = 76.00 + 0.11 = 76.11 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the statistical (Root-Sum-Square) stack-up rule.**

$$T_{rss} = \sqrt{(0.03)^2 + (0.05)^2 + (0.03)^2}$$

$$T_{rss} = \sqrt{0.0009 + 0.0025 + 0.0009} = \sqrt{0.0043}$$

$$T_{rss} = 0.0656 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta_{stat} = 76.00 \pm 0.066 = [75.934, 76.066] \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** The RSS method is **40% tighter** than worst case; it is used in **mass production** with statistical process control. Worst case is used in **aerospace / safety-critical** parts.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Type of Fit Between Piston and Cylinder (H7/g6)

> **Problem:** A diesel engine piston has nominal diameter $D = 100.000$ mm. The cylinder is specified as $\phi 100\,\text{H7}$ and the piston skirt as $\phi 100\,\text{g6}$. Determine the type of fit and the clearance limits.

**Step 1 — Look up ISO 286 fundamental deviations and IT-grade tolerances for the 100 mm size band.**

| Code | Fundamental Deviation | IT Grade | Tolerance Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| H7 (hole) | $EI = +0.000$ mm | IT7 | $T_H = 0.035$ mm |
| g6 (shaft) | $es = -0.012$ mm | IT6 | $T_s = 0.022$ mm |

**Step 2 — Compute hole limits.**

$$EI = 0.000 \text{ mm}, \quad ES = EI + T_H = 0.000 + 0.035 = +0.035 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Hole range} = [100.000, 100.035] \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute shaft limits.**

$$es = -0.012 \text{ mm}$$

$$ei = es - T_s = -0.012 - 0.022 = -0.034 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Shaft range} = [99.966, 99.988] \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum clearance.**

$$C_{max} = ES - ei = (+0.035) - (-0.034) = +0.069 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute minimum clearance.**

$$C_{min} = EI - es = (0.000) - (-0.012) = +0.012 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Classify the fit.**

$$C_{min} = +0.012 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{Clearance Fit}$$

**Step 7 — Design verdict.** This is a **sliding fit with lubrication** — ideal for a piston running inside a cylinder liner. The piston skirt is **always smaller** than the bore.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Section View Hatching Math

> **Problem:** In a full-sectioned piston drawing, the section-hatch lines are drawn at 45° with a pitch of $s = 2.5$ mm. If the hatched area (crown + ring zone) spans a length $L = 50$ mm along the section plane, how many hatch lines are required on a single view?

**Step 1 — Compute the perpendicular distance crossed by the section plane.**

The hatch lines are at 45°; the perpendicular distance between adjacent hatch lines is $s \cos(45°)$.

**Step 2 — Compute effective spacing.**

$$s_{\text{eff}} = s \times \cos(45°) = 2.5 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 1.768 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Number of hatch lines.**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{L}{s_{\text{eff}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50}{1.768} \right\rceil = \lceil 28.28 \rceil = 29 \text{ lines}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drafting Layers in the Piston Drawing

| Layer / Item No. | Layer Type | Line Spec (ASME) | Color (ISO) | CAD Layer Name |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visible outline | Thick continuous (0.5 mm) | White | `Visible` |
| 2 | Hidden outline | Thin dashed (0.25 mm, 3 mm dash) | Yellow | `Hidden` |
| 3 | Centerline | Thin long-dash dot-dot | Cyan | `Center` |
| 4 | Section hatch | Thin continuous at 45° | Magenta | `Section_Hatch` |
| 5 | Dimension lines | Thin continuous + arrowheads | Green | `Dim_Lines` |
| 6 | GD&T frame | Thin continuous, boxed | Red | `GDT_Frame` |
| 7 | Title block border | Thick continuous | Black | `Border` |
| 8 | Balloon / BOM leader | Thin continuous with arrow | Orange | `BOM_Leader` |

---
