# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Differentiate between Bottom-Up and Top-Down assembly design strategies. Which is preferred when the overall spatial envelope of the product drives the individual part sizes?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bottom-Up | Top-Down |
|---|---|---|
| Order of design | Parts first, then assembled | Skeleton / layout first, parts derived |
| Flexibility | High — each part can be edited independently | Medium — changes propagate to all parts |
| Use case | Known, mature products | Concept / new-platform design |
| CAD example | Insert Components in SolidWorks | Layout Sketch in SolidWorks |

**Answer to the second part:** The **Top-Down** approach is preferred when the spatial envelope drives part sizes, because a single layout sketch in the assembly file becomes the reference geometry for every part. A change in the layout automatically updates all dependent parts.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`
**Define the following terms with one engineering example each: (i) Concentric Mate, (ii) Distance Mate, (iii) Tangent Mate.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Concentric Mate:** Locks two cylindrical (or circular) features so that they share a **common axis**. *Example:* Gudgeon pin is locked concentric to the piston pin bore so that it rotates freely without binding.
2. **Distance Mate:** Locks two faces or edges to be separated by an **exact linear offset**. *Example:* Piston compression ring is offset by **1.5 mm** from the bottom face of ring groove 1 to set the ring's axial play.
3. **Tangent Mate:** Locks a curved surface so that it just **touches** a plane or another curve at one point. *Example:* The crown of a hemispherical-bowl diesel piston is mated tangent to the reference plane of the combustion chamber.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, S7 B.Tech Mechanical]`

**Q.A. (a) Explain the step-by-step procedure to create the assembly model of a Diesel Engine Piston in SolidWorks, including all the major mates and the final interference check. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — File Setup.** Open SolidWorks → New → Assembly. Save as `ASM_PISTON_ASSY.sldasm`.

**Step 2 — Insert Base Component.** Click *Insert Components* → Browse → select `PRT_PISTON_BODY.SLDPRT` → click anywhere in the graphics area. This component is automatically fixed at the origin.

**Step 3 — Insert Piston Ring 1 (Top Compression Ring).** Insert `PRT_RING_1.SLDPRT`. Use the *Concentric* mate between the inner cylindrical face of the ring and the outer cylindrical face of ring groove 1. Then use a *Coincident* mate between the top face of the ring and the bottom face of the ring groove (or use a *Distance* mate of 0.5 mm to set axial play).

**Step 4 — Insert Piston Ring 2.** Repeat Step 3 for ring groove 2 with a *Distance* mate of 0.4 mm.

**Step 5 — Insert Oil Scraper Ring.** Place in groove 3 with *Concentric* and *Distance = 0.6 mm* mates.

**Step 6 — Insert Gudgeon Pin.** Apply a *Concentric* mate between the pin's outer cylindrical face and the piston pin bore. Add a *Coincident* mate to set the pin's vertical position (it should pass through both bosses).

**Step 7 — Insert Circlips.** Two circlips lock the pin axially. *Concentric* mate each circlip with the pin's groove on either side, then *Coincident* mate its end face with the corresponding boss face.

**Step 8 — Interference Check.** *Evaluate → Interference Detection → Calculate*. The software highlights any overlapping volumes. In a valid piston assembly, **no interferences** should be detected among the rings, pin, and pin boss.

**Step 9 — Mass Property Verification.** *Evaluate → Mass Properties*. The total mass of the piston assembly should be within ±2% of the design target (typically 800–1500 g for a small diesel piston).

---

**Q.A. (b) With neat sketches, explain the procedure to generate a 2D Engineering Drawing of the Piston Assembly. State the relevant drafting standard and list at least 6 elements of the title block. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — New Drawing.** File → New → Drawing → select `PRT_PISTON_BODY.SLDPRT` and `ASM_PISTON_ASSY.sLDASM` from the Model View palette.

**Step 2 — Insert Standard Views.** Drag the **Front**, **Top**, and **Right (Left)** views onto Sheet 1 of size **A2 (594 × 420 mm)** at a scale of **1:1**. The isometric / pictorial view is placed at the top-right corner at scale **1:2**.

**Step 3 — Generate Sectional View.** Right-click the front view → *Section View* → draw a vertical section line through the piston axis → confirm. The view becomes a **Full Section A-A** showing internal features (pin bore, internal ribs, ring grooves, crown bowl).

**Step 4 — Detail View.** Select the pin boss region → *Detail View* → circle of diameter 30 mm → scale **2:1**. This enlarged view receives the GD&T callouts for the pin bore.

**Step 5 — Dimensions.** Use *Smart Dimension*. Critical dimensions to mark: outer diameter, total height, ring groove widths and positions, pin bore diameter, compression height, skirt length. Apply **±0.05 mm** tolerances.

**Step 6 — GD&T Callouts.** Add datums **A** (crown face), **B** (pin bore axis), and **C** (outer cylindrical surface). Apply **cylindricity** on the OD, **circularity** on the pin bore, **perpendicularity** of crown to pin axis, and **position** tolerance on the pin bore (Ø0.05 mm at MMC).

**Step 7 — Surface-Finish Symbols.** Mark **Ra 0.8** on the OD, **Ra 0.4** on the pin bore, **Ra 3.2** on the crown.

**Step 8 — Title Block.** Standard: **ASME Y14.5-2018** (or IS 696 / ISO 128 for Indian context).

**Six Title-Block Elements:**
1. Company name & logo
2. Drawing title (e.g., "DIESEL ENGINE PISTON ASSEMBLY")
3. Drawing number (e.g., `PCMUL507/EXP2/M24/001`)
4. Sheet size & scale (A2, 1:1)
5. Drawn by / Checked by / Approved by signatures
6. Date of issue & revision number

**Step 9 — Bill of Materials (BOM).** Place above the title block. Add balloon callouts (numbered circles connected by leaders) on the assembly view, matching the BOM item numbers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S7 B.Tech Mechanical – Alternative Module Choice]`

**Q.B. (a) Compute the worst-case and statistical tolerance stack-up for the axial play of a gudgeon pin in a piston assembly. Given: piston boss width = 22 ± 0.04 mm, connecting rod small-end width = 38 ± 0.06 mm, and the other piston boss width = 22 ± 0.04 mm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the assembly loop.** The total axial space spanned along the pin axis is the sum of three components.

$$\Delta = W_1 + W_r + W_2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute nominal values.**

$$\Delta_{nom} = 22.00 + 38.00 + 22.00 = 82.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case stack-up.**

$$T_{wc} = \sum T_i = 0.04 + 0.06 + 0.04 = 0.14 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta_{max}^{wc} = 82.00 + 0.14 = 82.14 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta_{min}^{wc} = 82.00 - 0.14 = 81.86 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Statistical (RSS) stack-up.**

$$T_{rss} = \sqrt{(0.04)^2 + (0.06)^2 + (0.04)^2}$$

$$T_{rss} = \sqrt{0.0016 + 0.0036 + 0.0016} = \sqrt{0.0068}$$

$$T_{rss} = 0.0825 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta_{stat} = 82.00 \pm 0.0825 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Result Comparison.** The RSS tolerance (0.0825 mm) is **41% tighter** than the worst-case value (0.14 mm), assuming independent and normally distributed manufacturing tolerances.

| Method | Stack-up | Axial Play Range |
|---|---|---|
| Worst-case | $\pm 0.14$ mm | 81.86 to 82.14 mm |
| Statistical (RSS) | $\pm 0.0825$ mm | 81.9175 to 82.0825 mm |

**Step 6 — Design Recommendation.** For a high-volume passenger-vehicle diesel engine, **RSS** is acceptable. For a heavy-duty truck / marine diesel, **worst-case** is mandated.

---

**Q.B. (b) For a diesel engine piston-cylinder fit specified as Ø 95 H7/g6, identify the type of fit and compute the maximum and minimum clearance. ISO 286 data for 95 mm size: H7 → EI = 0, IT7 = 0.035 mm; g6 → es = −0.012 mm, IT6 = 0.022 mm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute Hole Limits (H7).**

$$EI = 0.000 \text{ mm}$$
$$ES = EI + IT7 = 0.000 + 0.035 = +0.035 \text{ mm}$$

Hole size range: $\phi 95.000$ to $\phi 95.035$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute Shaft Limits (g6).**

$$es = -0.012 \text{ mm}$$
$$ei = es - IT6 = -0.012 - 0.022 = -0.034 \text{ mm}$$

Shaft (piston OD) range: $\phi 94.966$ to $\phi 94.988$ mm.

**Step 3 — Maximum Clearance.**

$$C_{max} = ES - ei = (+0.035) - (-0.034) = +0.069 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Clearance.**

$$C_{min} = EI - es = (0.000) - (-0.012) = +0.012 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Classify the Fit.**

$$C_{min} = +0.012 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{Clearance Fit}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.** A clearance range of **0.012 to 0.069 mm** is a **precision sliding fit**. The piston OD is always smaller than the cylinder ID, allowing it to reciprocate freely with hydrodynamic oil-film lubrication. The minimum clearance ensures the piston never seizes at cold start due to thermal expansion; the maximum clearance prevents excessive **blow-by** of combustion gases past the rings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to fix the first part in an assembly** — every SolidWorks/CATIA assembly must have *one* component fixed at the origin, or every other mate becomes relative to a floating reference.
> 2. **Mixing up `EI` (lower) and `ES` (upper)** — always write the limits in the same order: lower first, upper second.
> 3. **Not checking interference in assembly** — if the ring and groove have wrong offsets, the part is *defined* but the assembly *fails*; KTU awards marks for the validation step.
> 4. **Using English/Inch dimensioning on a mm sheet** — section-hatch spacing of 2.5 mm is fine, but unit mismatch in the title block costs marks.
> 5. **Sketching GD&T frames without defining datums** — a GD&T callout referencing `Datum-A` is invalid if `Datum-A` is not defined on the drawing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Diesel Engine Piston CAD Experiment**

- [ ] **Assembly is a separate file (.sldasm / .CATProduct / .prt)** — not a part file.
- [ ] **First inserted component must be FIXED** to set the assembly origin.
- [ ] A fully defined assembly has **zero degrees of freedom** — every component constrained by mates.
- [ ] **Concentric + Coincident** is the most common mate combination for pins-in-bores.
- [ ] **Interference Detection** and **Mass-Property evaluation** are mandatory validation steps.
- [ ] Drafting standards: **ASME Y14.5-2018** (international) or **IS 696 / ISO 128** (India).
- [ ] **Section view** uses **hatch lines at 45°, 2–3 mm pitch**; different parts in the same section get **different hatch angles** or **opposite directions**.
- [ ] **Title block is bottom-right**; **BOM is above the title block**.
- [ ] **GD&T requires a defined datum scheme** — never call out a datum symbol without first establishing the datum feature.
- [ ] **Tolerance stack-up — worst case** = arithmetic sum; **statistical** = root-sum-square.
- [ ] **H7/g6 is a clearance fit** — piston is always smaller than the cylinder.
- [ ] **Gudgeon pin fit with piston bore** is typically H7/g6 or H6/h5 (sliding fit with lubrication).
- [ ] **Piston-crown bowl in DI engines** is a re-entrant or ω-bowl shape to optimize air-spray interaction.
- [ ] **Surface roughness on piston OD = Ra 0.8 µm**; on **pin bore = Ra 0.4 µm**; on **crown = Ra 3.2 µm**.
- [ ] **Drawing sheet size for piston assembly**: **A2 (594 × 420 mm)** at scale **1:1**.
- [ ] **BOM balloon numbers** are placed on the assembly view, matching row numbers in the BOM table.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Viva Questions & Answers**
> 1. *What is the difference between a part and an assembly in CAD?* — A **part** is a single 3D body; an **assembly** is a container file that holds multiple parts linked by mates.
> 2. *Why is the piston OD made elliptical (barrel-shaped) in real engines?* — To compensate for thermal expansion and the side-thrust from the connecting rod, ensuring even contact pressure with the cylinder bore.
> 3. *What is the difference between MMC and LMC in GD&T?* — **MMC (Maximum Material Condition)** = the condition where the part has the *most* material (largest shaft, smallest hole); **LMC** = *least* material.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Petrol Engine Piston
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Petrol Engine Piston

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — CREATING ASSEMBLY MODELS AND DRAFTING OF PETROL ENGINE PISTON

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Assembly Model?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Assembly Model):** An *assembly model* in CAD is a hierarchical collection of multiple **part files (components)** brought together in a single environment using **geometric relationships (constraints/mates)** that mimic the kinematic and physical behaviour of a real engineering mechanism.

In SolidWorks / CATIA / Creo / NX terminology, an assembly is **not** a merged single solid; it is a *parametric linked structure* where every component retains its individual identity and can be edited independently while updating the parent assembly.

### 1.2 What is a Petrol Engine Piston?

A **piston** is a cylindrical component that reciprocates inside the engine cylinder. It transmits the force of combustion to the **connecting rod**, which in turn rotates the **crankshaft**. The piston must:

- Withstand high **combustion pressure** (≈ 40–60 bar for petrol, up to **200 bar** for diesel).
- Resist **thermal loads** (gas-side crown temperatures ≈ **300 °C**).
- Provide a **gas-tight seal** via piston rings.
- Maintain low friction via **skirt contact**.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus asks the student to **model the piston as an individual part**, then **assemble it with the gudgeon pin (wrist pin), circlips, and connecting rod small-end**, and finally **generate a 2D engineering drawing** with proper orthographic, sectional, and dimensioned views.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego-Movie" Analogy

Imagine you have a **Lego set** where every brick is a 3D-modelled part file.

- **Each brick (piston, pin, circlip, rod)** = one `.SLDPRT` / `.CATPart` file.
- **Sticking two bricks together with a hinge-pin** = applying a *Concentric* + *Coincident* mate in the assembly.
- **Photographing the completed toy from six sides, with arrows showing measurements** = generating the 2D **Drafting drawing** with dimensions and section views.

The CAD software simply *remembers the snapping rules (mates)* you defined, so if you later edit the piston height, the assembly and drawing **automatically update** — this is **parametric associativity**.

### 1.4 Key Sub-Components of a Petrol Engine Piston

| Sub-feature | Engineering Function |
|---|---|
| **Crown (Head)** | Top surface exposed to combustion gases; often dished or flat. |
| **Ring Grooves (Top 2)** | House *compression rings* — gas sealing. |
| **Ring Groove (Bottom 1)** | Houses *oil control ring* — scrapes excess oil. |
| **Lands** | Thin cylindrical bands between grooves that support the rings. |
| **Skirt** | Long cylindrical guide surface that contacts the cylinder bore. |
| **Gudgeon Pin Boss** | Internal thickened region housing the **wrist pin**. |
| **Wrist / Gudgeon Pin Hole** | Ø through-bore connecting piston to connecting rod. |
| **Internal Reliefs** | Lightening pockets to reduce reciprocating mass. |

### 1.5 Standard Materials Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Petrol engine pistons are predominantly made of **cast aluminium alloys** (e.g., **Al-Si eutectic alloy — A4032 / LM13 / AlSi12Cu**) because of:
> - Low density (**≈ 2.7 g/cm³**).
> - High thermal conductivity (**≈ 150 W/m·K**).
> - Good wear resistance when hard-anodised or coated with **graphite/MoS₂**.

For high-performance engines, **forged Al-Si alloys** or even **invar-inlaid crowns** (low-expansion inserts) are used to control thermal growth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assembly Modelling — Constraint Theory

An assembly is mathematically governed by **degrees of freedom (DOF)**. Every rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). Each mate removes DOF as follows:

| Mate Type | DOF Removed | Typical Use in Piston Assembly |
|---|---|---|
| **Coincident** (Face–Face) | 3 translational | Crown faces clamped to cylinder head seating plane |
| **Concentric** (Cylindrical) | 2 translational + 2 rotational | Gudgeon pin aligned to piston boss bore |
| **Distance / Offset** | 0 (positional only) | Pin offset from crown (often **+0.5 to +1.5 mm**) for quiet running |
| **Angle** | 0 (positional only) | Connecting rod swing limits |
| **Parallel / Perpendicular** | 2 rotational | Skirt axis parallel to bore |

> [!NOTE]
> A correctly constrained sub-assembly is **fully defined** (DOF = 0) and shown in **black** in SolidWorks; under-defined components appear in **blue** and over-defined (redundant) components appear in **red**.

### 2.2 Two Assembly Strategies

1. **Bottom-Up Assembly (most common for piston):**
   Create all parts first → open Assembly → insert parts → apply mates. Best when parts already exist.

2. **Top-Down Assembly:**
   Start with a *skeleton* layout part → derive part geometry from assembly-level references. Useful for **mechanism design**.

The KTU experiment uses **Bottom-Up**, since the piston is modelled independently.

## 2.3 Piston Geometry — Engineering Parameters

Let:
- **D** = Cylinder bore diameter (mm)
- **L** = Piston total length (mm), typically **L ≈ D**
- **t_sk** = Skirt thickness (mm)
- **t_crown** = Crown thickness (mm), usually **6–8 % of D**
- **H_pin** = Height of gudgeon pin axis from crown bottom (mm), typically **H_pin ≈ 0.5 D**
- **D_pin** = Gudgeon pin diameter (mm), usually **D_pin ≈ 0.28 D**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value (Petrol) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bore (D) | Given | 70 – 90 | mm |
| 2 | Stroke (S) | S = 2r | 60 – 90 | mm |
| 3 | Piston Length (L) | $L \approx (1.0 \text{ to } 1.1) D$ | 70 – 95 | mm |
| 4 | Crown Thickness (t_crown) | $t_{crown} \approx 0.06 D$ to $0.08 D$ | 4.2 – 7.2 | mm |
| 5 | Skirt Thickness (t_sk) | $t_{sk} \approx 0.04 D$ | 2.8 – 3.6 | mm |
| 6 | Gudgeon Pin Ø (D_pin) | $D_{pin} \approx 0.28 D$ | 19.6 – 25.2 | mm |
| 7 | Pin Offset | $\delta$ | +0.5 to +1.5 | mm |
| 8 | Compression Ratio (r) | $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | 8 : 1 to 12 : 1 | – |
| 9 | Swept Volume (V_s) | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot S$ | – | mm³ |
| 10 | Clearance Volume (V_c) | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot \text{gap} + V_{crown}$ | – | mm³ |
| 11 | Mean Piston Speed (v_m) | $v_m = \dfrac{2 S \cdot N}{60}$ | ≤ 18 (petrol) | m/s |
| 12 | Piston Material Density | **ρ (Al-alloy)** | **2.7** | g/cm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fits to remember:** Piston-to-bore is a *running fit*; for Ø 80 mm bore the piston is sized ≈ **79.93 mm** (clearance ≈ **0.04–0.07 mm**) to allow for **thermal expansion** of aluminium (α ≈ **23 × 10⁻⁶ /K**).

## 2.5 Drafting — The 2D Drawing Pipeline

In KTU lab exams, the drafted drawing must contain:

1. **Orthographic views** — Front (sectioned), Top, Side.
2. **Section view** through the gudgeon pin axis (reveals internal boss, ring grooves).
3. **Centre-line marking** on all circular features.
4. **Dimensions** — linear, diametrical, angular.
5. **GD&T — Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing** (cylindricity of bore, perpendicularity of crown).
6. **Title block**, **BOM (Bill of Materials)**, **Tolerances**, **Surface finish symbols**.
7. **Scale indication** (commonly **1:1** for piston).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine OEMs (Tata, Maruti, Honda, BMW)** maintain a fully parametric assembly of the piston-connecting rod-crankshaft group. A single change in bore diameter cascades through piston, rings, pin, and cylinder block drawings.
- **Generative design and FEA** preload assemblies are exported as STEP/IGES for stress analysis in ANSYS / Abaqus.
- **Drafting** acts as the **legal manufacturing contract** — it carries GD&T, surface finish, and material specification that machining cells follow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Designing a Petrol Engine Piston

**Given (KTU Sample Question data):**
A single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine has:
- Bore, $D = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Stroke, $S = 90 \text{ mm}$
- Compression ratio, $r = 9 : 1$
- Engine speed, $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$
- Piston-to-bore clearance at room temperature = $0.05 \text{ mm}$

**Required:** Compute swept volume, clearance volume, piston length, gudgeon pin diameter, mean piston speed, and piston mass.

### Step 1 — Swept Volume (V_s)

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot S
$$

Substitute D = 80 mm, S = 90 mm:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 80^2 \times 90
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{3.1416}{4} \times 6400 \times 90
$$

$$
V_s = 0.7854 \times 6400 \times 90
$$

$$
V_s = 0.7854 \times 576000
$$

$$
\boxed{V_s = 4.524 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3} = 452.4 \text{ cm}^{3} \approx 452 \text{ cc}}
$$

### Step 2 — Clearance Volume (V_c) from Compression Ratio

The compression ratio definition:

$$
r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}
$$

Rearrange for $V_c$:

$$
r \cdot V_c = V_s + V_c
$$

$$
(r - 1) V_c = V_s
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{V_s}{r - 1}
$$

Substitute $V_s = 452.4 \text{ cm}^{3}$ and $r = 9$:

$$
V_c = \frac{452.4}{9 - 1} = \frac{452.4}{8}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_c = 56.55 \text{ cm}^{3}}
$$

### Step 3 — Equivalent Cylinder Head-to-Piston-TDC Gap

The clearance volume occupies the volume above the piston at TDC. For a flat crown:

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot h_{gap} + V_{crown,dish}
$$

Assume a flat crown; solve for $h_{gap}$:

$$
h_{gap} = \frac{V_c}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2} = \frac{56.55}{0.7854 \times 6400}
$$

$$
h_{gap} = \frac{56.55}{5026.55}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{gap} \approx 0.01125 \text{ cm} = 0.1125 \text{ mm}}
$$

This is the small physical gap between the piston crown at TDC and the cylinder head — typical of petrol engines.

### Step 4 — Piston Overall Length (L)

Using the empirical rule $L \approx 1.05 D$:

$$
L = 1.05 \times 80 = 84 \text{ mm}
$$

(Standard KTU value range: 80–88 mm — both acceptable.)

### Step 5 — Crown Thickness (t_crown)

Using $t_{crown} \approx 0.07 D$:

$$
t_{crown} = 0.07 \times 80 = 5.6 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 6 — Gudgeon Pin Diameter (D_pin)

Using $D_{pin} \approx 0.28 D$:

$$
D_{pin} = 0.28 \times 80 = 22.4 \text{ mm}
$$

Round to standard pin size: **22 mm** (or 22.4 mm).

### Step 7 — Mean Piston Speed (v_m)

$$
v_m = \frac{2 S N}{60}
$$

$$
v_m = \frac{2 \times 0.090 \times 3000}{60}
$$

$$
v_m = \frac{540}{60} = 9 \text{ m/s}
$$

Since $v_m < 18 \text{ m/s}$, this is acceptable for a petrol engine — *no risk of piston scuffing*.

### Step 8 — Approximate Piston Mass

Treat the piston as a hollow cylinder with average thickness $t_{avg} = (t_{crown} + t_{sk}) / 2 \approx (5.6 + 3.2)/2 = 4.4 \text{ mm}$.

Outer diameter = 80 mm, inner diameter ≈ $80 - 2(4.4) = 71.2 \text{ mm}$.

Volume of material:

$$
V_{mat} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_o^2 - D_i^2 \right) L
$$

$$
V_{mat} = \frac{\pi}{4} (80^2 - 71.2^2) \times 84
$$

$$
V_{mat} = 0.7854 \times (6400 - 5069.44) \times 84
$$

$$
V_{mat} = 0.7854 \times 1330.56 \times 84
$$

$$
V_{mat} = 0.7854 \times 111767
$$

$$
V_{mat} = 87775 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 87.78 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Mass:

$$
m = \rho \times V = 2.7 \times 87.78
$$

$$
\boxed{m \approx 237 \text{ grams}}
$$

(Acceptable range: 200–280 g for an 80 mm petrol piston.)

### Step 3.9 — Analytical Matrix — Why These Values?

| Decision | Driver | Consequence of Error |
|---|---|---|
| L = 1.05 D | Balance between skirt guidance & mass | Short → piston slap; Long → weight, friction |
| t_crown = 0.07 D | Thermal strength vs. mass | Thin → burn-through; Thick → overheating |
| D_pin = 0.28 D | Bending stress on pin | Thin pin → failure; Thick → heavy |
| Clearance = 0.05 mm | Thermal expansion compensation | Too tight → seizure; Too loose → slap & noise |
| Flat crown | Easier machining, even combustion | Dish improves CR but weakens crown |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Modelling & Assembly Workflow

This Mermaid diagram shows the **complete workflow** from part creation to drafted drawing, the kind a KTU examiner expects in the lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[startCAD Start] --> A1[Set Units mm and Create Folder]
    A1 --> A2[Sketch Piston Profile on Front Plane]
    A2 --> A3[Revolve Boss Base 360 deg]
    A3 --> A4[Extrude Crown with Dish Feature]
    A4 --> A5[Cut Three Ring Grooves using Shell Cut]
    A5 --> A6[Cut Wrist Pin Bore through Boss]
    A6 --> A7[Fillet all Sharp Edges R 1 mm]
    A7 --> A8[Apply Al Alloy Material and Save Piston SLDPRT]
    A8 --> B0[startAssembly Open Assembly File]
    B0 --> B1[Insert Piston as Fixed Component]
    B1 --> B2[Insert Gudgeon Pin]
    B2 --> B3[Apply Concentric Mate Pin to Piston Bore]
    B3 --> B4[Insert Connecting Rod Small End]
    B4 --> B5[Apply Concentric Mate Rod to Pin]
    B5 --> B6[Insert Left Circlip and Right Circlip]
    B6 --> B7[Apply Coincident Mate Circlip to Pin Groove]
    B7 --> B8[Verify All Components are Fully Defined Black]
    B8 --> B9[Generate Bill of Materials BOM]
    B9 --> C0[startDrafting Open Drawing from Assembly]
    C0 --> C1[Insert Front View Sectioned A A]
    C0 --> C2[Insert Top View]
    C0 --> C3[Insert Side View]
    C1 --> C4[Add Centre Lines on all Circular Features]
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5[Apply Linear Diametrical and Angular Dimensions]
    C5 --> C6[Apply GD&T Cylindricity on Bore]
    C6 --> C7[Add Surface Finish Symbols and Title Block]
    C7 --> C8[Save as DXF and PDF for Submission]
    A0:::start
    A8:::part
    B8:::asm
    C8:::draw
    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef part fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef asm fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef draw fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Constraint Relationship Diagram — Piston–Pin–Rod–Circlip Sub-Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Piston Body Part] -- Concentric Mate on bore --> W0[Wrist Pin Cylinder]
    W0 -- Concentric Mate on small end bore --> R0[Connecting Rod Small End]
    W0 -- Coincident Mate on left groove --> L0[Left Circlip]
    W0 -- Coincident Mate on right groove --> L1[Right Circlip]
    P0 -. Fixed .- FX0[Frozen Base Component]
    P0 -- provides reference --> B0[BOM Item 1 Al Alloy]
    W0 -- provides reference --> B1[BOM Item 2 EN Steel]
    R0 -- provides reference --> B2[BOM Item 3 Forged Steel]
    L0 -- provides reference --> B3[BOM Item 4 Spring Steel]
    L1 -- provides reference --> B3
```

## 4.3 Piston Sub-Feature Map (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Piston[Petrol Engine Piston] --> Crown[Crown Top Face]
    Piston --> Grooves[Ring Grooves x 3]
    Grooves --> G1[Top Compression Ring Land]
    Grooves --> G2[Second Compression Ring Land]
    Grooves --> G3[Oil Control Ring Land]
    Piston --> Boss[Gudgeon Pin Boss Internal]
    Boss --> Bore[Wrist Pin Bore Through Hole]
    Piston --> Skirt[Open Skirt Bottom]
    Skirt --> Relief[Internal Lightening Reliefs]
    Piston --> Slots[T Slot Skirt Ends for Expansion]
```

## 4.4 Drafting Sheet Layout (KTU Standard A3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sheet[A3 Drawing Sheet] --> Z1[Border Zone with Title Block]
    Sheet --> Z2[Front Sectioned View Centre]
    Sheet --> Z3[Top View Top Right]
    Sheet --> Z4[Side View Bottom Right]
    Sheet --> Z5[Detail View of Ring Groove]
    Sheet --> Z6[BOM Table Top Left]
    Sheet --> Z7[GD&T Frame Below Front View]
    Sheet --> Z8[Notes and Tolerances Footer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> If the laboratory has only 2D CAD (AutoCAD), the same views are produced manually using **front view + section line A–A → half section + top view + side view**, then dimensioned using the **Aligned, Linear, Diameter, and Leader** commands.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any four essential sub-features that must be modelled on a petrol engine piston. State the function of each.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
1. **Crown** — top surface exposed to combustion gases, transmits thrust load.
2. **Ring grooves (× 3)** — house compression and oil control rings for gas sealing and oil scraping.
3. **Gudgeon pin bore** — connects piston to connecting rod via the wrist pin.
4. **Skirt** — provides lateral guidance inside the cylinder bore and resists side thrust.
5. *(Optional 4th)* **Internal lightening reliefs** — reduce reciprocating mass.
6. *(Optional 5th)* **T-slots at skirt ends** — accommodate thermal expansion of aluminium.

*(Each feature 0.5 mark, function 0.5 mark — total 3 marks.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Bottom-Up and Top-Down assembly modelling approaches. Which one is used to assemble the piston with the gudgeon pin and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Bottom-Up | Top-Down |
|---|---|
| Parts modelled first, then assembled. | Skeleton part created first; parts derived inside assembly. |
| Components are independent files. | Parts share a master skeleton. |
| Used when parts already exist. | Used during initial mechanism design. |

**For the piston–gudgeon pin–connecting rod assembly, the Bottom-Up approach is used** because each part (piston, pin, rod, circlips) is first modelled as an independent `.SLDPRT`/`.CATPart` file and then inserted into the assembly with mates — this matches the standard lab procedure for Experiment 2.

*(1 mark definition each + 1 mark reason = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always dimension to centre-lines** for cylindrical features; dimensioning to edges is a **–2 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Section views must show hatching with correct ANSI/ISO spacing** (≈ 2 mm, 45°); missing hatching = **–1 mark**.
> 3. **GD&T datum reference is the cylinder bore axis**, not the outer surface — wrong datum = **–3 marks** in GD&T questions.
> 4. **Mates must fully define the assembly**; even one blue (under-defined) component will cost **–2 marks** in the viva.
> 5. **BOM table must include: Item No., Qty, Part Name, Material, Part No.** — missing any column = **–1 mark**.
> 6. **Compression ratio formula rearrangement** is a frequent slip; many students wrongly write $r = V_s / V_c$. Examiner's key explicitly states: $r = (V_s + V_c)/V_c$.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the constructional features of a petrol engine piston. Discuss the materials used and reasons for selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine with bore **85 mm**, stroke **90 mm**, compression ratio **10 : 1**, and rated speed **2800 rpm**, calculate: **(i)** swept volume, **(ii)** clearance volume, **(iii)** mean piston speed, (iv) recommended piston length, and **(v)** approximate piston mass assuming an aluminium alloy (ρ = 2.7 g/cm³) with average wall thickness 4 mm. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(a)

**Constructional features** (with neat sketch):

1. **Crown** – flat or slightly dished, ~6–8 % of D thick.
2. **Ring grooves** – three grooves: top two for compression rings, bottom one for oil control ring; grooves ~0.5 mm wider than ring radial thickness for axial movement.
3. **Ring lands** – narrow bands between grooves supporting the rings.
4. **Gudgeon pin boss** – thickened internal region with a through bore.
5. **Skirt** – long cylindrical portion guiding piston; sometimes has T-slots at the open end to allow thermal expansion.
6. **Internal lightening reliefs** – reduce mass above the pin.
7. **Crown marking** – small "FRONT" or arrow indicating assembly orientation.

**Materials** (with reasons):

| Material | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Al-Si eutectic alloy (A4032 / LM13)** | Low density, high thermal conductivity, low friction |
| Cast iron (older designs) | High wear resistance, low cost |
| Forged Al-Si | Higher strength for performance engines |
| Invar insert (optional) | Low thermal expansion ring around crown |

*(Sketch: 2 marks; features 3 marks; materials 2 marks = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q(b)

**Given:** D = 85 mm, S = 90 mm, r = 10, N = 2800 rpm, ρ = 2.7 g/cm³, t_avg = 4 mm.

**(i) Swept Volume**

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 S = \frac{\pi}{4} (85)^2 (90)
$$

$$
V_s = 0.7854 \times 7225 \times 90 = 5.106 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 510.6 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**(ii) Clearance Volume**

$$
V_c = \frac{V_s}{r - 1} = \frac{510.6}{10 - 1} = \frac{510.6}{9} \approx 56.73 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**(iii) Mean Piston Speed**

$$
v_m = \frac{2 S N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 0.090 \times 2800}{60} = \frac{504}{60} = 8.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) Recommended Piston Length**

$$
L = 1.05 D = 1.05 \times 85 = 89.25 \text{ mm} \approx 90 \text{ mm}
$$

**(v) Approximate Piston Mass**

Outer Ø = 85 mm; Inner Ø = 85 − 2(4) = 77 mm.

$$
V_{mat} = \frac{\pi}{4} (85^2 - 77^2) \times 90 = 0.7854 \times (7225 - 5929) \times 90
$$

$$
V_{mat} = 0.7854 \times 1296 \times 90 = 91{,}604 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 91.6 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
m = 2.7 \times 91.6 \approx 247 \text{ grams}
$$

*(Each sub-part ≈ 1.4 marks; correct units & formulae mandatory.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to create the assembly of a petrol engine piston with its gudgeon pin, circlips, and connecting rod in any standard 3D CAD package. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the **different types of mates** commonly used in the piston sub-assembly. Why is the **fully-defined** status of each component essential before generating the drawing? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(a) — Step-by-Step Procedure

1. **Create individual part files** (`piston.SLDPRT`, `wrist_pin.SLDPRT`, `circlip.SLDPRT`, `connecting_rod.SLDPRT`). Apply materials (Al-alloy, EN8 steel, spring steel, forged steel).

2. **Open New Assembly** → save as `piston_assembly.SLDASM`.

3. **Insert Piston** as the *first fixed component* (right-click → "Fix").

4. **Insert Wrist Pin**:
   - Apply **Concentric** mate between the pin's outer cylindrical surface and the piston's gudgeon bore.

5. **Insert Connecting Rod (Small End)**:
   - Apply **Concentric** mate between the rod's small-end bore and the pin.
   - Apply **Coincident** mate between the rod's small-end face and the piston inner boss face (sets axial position).

6. **Insert Left Circlip**:
   - Apply **Concentric** mate between circlip's inner Ø and pin groove.
   - Apply **Coincident** mate between circlip face and pin shoulder groove face.

7. **Insert Right Circlip** — repeat on the opposite side.

8. **Verify** in the FeatureManager that every component shows the **black "Fully Defined"** status.

9. **Generate BOM** → Insert → Tables → Bill of Materials. Configure columns: Item No., Qty, Part Name, Material.

10. **Save** the assembly and **Move to Drafting** → File → Make Drawing from Assembly.

#### Model Solution — Q(b) — Types of Mates

| Mate | Function in Piston Assembly |
|---|---|
| **Coincident** | Aligns faces; aligns circlip faces to pin shoulder. |
| **Concentric** | Aligns circular features — pin in bore, rod in pin. |
| **Distance / Offset** | Sets pin offset (0.5–1.5 mm) from piston centre. |
| **Angle** | Constrains rotational freedom (rod swing). |
| **Parallel / Perp.** | Ensures skirt axis parallel to bore. |
| **Tangent** | (Not used in piston — but used in cam-follower.) |

**Why "Fully Defined" is essential:**

- **Numerical accuracy**: under-defined components can drift in 3D space and produce incorrect drawings.
- **Parametric stability**: when the bore is changed, all mates propagate; otherwise the assembly collapses.
- **FEA export correctness**: ANSYS / Abaqus requires a fully constrained geometry.
- **Drawing associativity**: a partially-defined assembly produces section views with misplaced section lines.
- **KTU marking**: examiner's key requires checking each component's status — penalty for any blue (under-defined) component.

*(Mates table 3 marks; explanations 2 marks; fully-defined reasoning 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

### 🔧 Modelling
- ✅ Sketch the **half-profile of the piston** on the Front Plane (use a single closed polyline: crown → OD of skirt → bottom → up the bore).
- ✅ Use **Revolved Boss/Base** at 360°.
- ✅ Cut **three ring grooves** using *Extruded Cut* (depth 1.5–2.5 mm each, spaced ≈ 4–5 mm apart).
- ✅ Cut the **wrist-pin bore** through the boss.
- ✅ Apply **1 mm fillets** at the crown-skirt junction and groove edges.
- ✅ Add a small "FRONT" engraving on the crown.

### 🔩 Assembly
- ✅ Use **Bottom-Up** approach: parts → assembly → mates.
- ✅ Apply mates in this order: **Concentric first, then Coincident** — reverses often fail.
- ✅ **Fix the piston first** as the base component.
- ✅ Verify **0 DOF** (black colour in SolidWorks).
- ✅ Add **BOM** with 4 columns minimum.

### 📐 Drafting
- ✅ Produce **Front section, Top, Side** orthographic views on A3 sheet.
- ✅ Section through **the wrist-pin axis (A–A)**.
- ✅ Apply **GD&T** — cylindricity on bore, perpendicularity of crown to bore axis.
- ✅ Title block must show: **Scale (1:1), Projection (1st angle for KTU), Material, Part No., Name, Date**.
- ✅ All dimensions in **mm** with **± tolerance** as per ISO 2768-mK.

### 📚 Theory & Formulas
- ✅ $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 S$ ; $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$
- ✅ $v_m = \dfrac{2 S N}{60} \le 18 \text{ m/s}$ (petrol) — *never exceed.*
- ✅ $D_{pin} \approx 0.28 D$ ; $t_{crown} \approx 0.07 D$ ; $L \approx 1.05 D$
- ✅ Piston material: **Al-Si alloy**, ρ = **2.7 g/cm³**, α = **23 × 10⁻⁶ /K**.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Forgetting the **fillets** at sharp corners.
- ❌ Wrong projection symbol (1st vs 3rd angle) — KTU uses **1st angle**.
- ❌ Section hatching on **ribs/webs** — should be *omitted* per ISO 128.
- ❌ Missing **centre lines** on the bore and gudgeon pin.
- ❌ Connecting rod inserted with the wrong orientation (small-end boss must face the piston).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Screw Jack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Screw Jack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Screw Jack** is a simple, portable mechanical device used to lift heavy loads (automobiles, building structures, heavy machinery) by applying a small effort on a lever arm attached to a screw. The mechanism converts the rotational motion of the screw into the linear translatory motion of the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block**
> **Assembly Modeling** is the process of combining two or more individual **Part Models** (components) into a single virtual product file by applying geometric relationships called **Mates** or **Assembly Constraints**. These mates lock the relative degrees of freedom (DOF) of parts to simulate real-world mechanical behavior.
> **Drafting** is the process of generating **2D Engineering Drawings** (orthographic projections, sectional views, auxiliary views) directly from the 3D model. This includes the automatic generation of **Views**, **Dimensions**, **Balloons**, **Bill of Materials (BOM)**, and **Title Blocks** as per **BIS SP 46:2003** drafting standards used in KTU labs.

## 1.2 The Six Essential Components of a Standard Screw Jack

| Sl. No. | Part Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base / Body** | The hollow cylindrical housing that supports the entire assembly and provides the threaded bore. |
| 2 | **Main Screw (Spindle)** | The long threaded shaft that rotates inside the nut. It transmits the lifting force. |
| 3 | **Cup / Nut** | The threaded bush pressed into the base. It engages with the main screw threads. |
| 4 | **Toggle / Link Arm** | The lever arm used to apply manual torque to rotate the screw. |
| 5 | **Pivot Pin (Fulcrum Pin)** | A horizontal pin connecting the toggle arm to the screw head. |
| 6 | **Swivel Pad / Toe Pad** | A small concave platform at the top of the screw that bears against the load, allowing slight angular misalignment. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Instant Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:** Imagine you are trying to lift a **250 kg motorcycle** with your bare hands — impossible. Now imagine slipping a small **C-clamp** (a screw jack in essence) under it and turning a small handle. The handle moves a long distance, but with very little effort, and the car rises by a tiny amount. 
> 
> The **handle (toggle arm)** acts as a **Class II lever** to magnify the torque on the screw. The **screw thread** itself acts as an **inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder**, converting rotation into a slow, powerful lifting push. In CAD, we don't just "draw" the screw jack — we virtually **assemble** these 6 parts together so the software understands that "if I rotate the toggle, the screw must travel linearly through the cup."

## 1.4 Why This Experiment is a KTU High-Priority Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The screw jack is the **canonical pedagogical assembly** in every KTU-affiliated CAD lab (SolidWorks, CATIA, NX, Creo, Fusion 360, Solid Edge) because it tests **all four core competencies** required by the PCMUL507 syllabus:
> 1. **Part Modeling** (threads, fillets, revolved features)
> 2. **Assembly Modeling** (coincident, concentric, distance mates)
> 3. **Motion Study** (verifying screw kinematics)
> 4. **2D Drafting** (generating A4 production drawings with GD&T)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Mechanics of a Screw Jack

The screw jack works on the principle of a **simple screw and nut pair**. When a tangential force $F$ is applied at the end of the toggle of length $l$, it generates a torque $T$ on the screw shaft.

### 2.2 Free Body Analysis (Logic Steps)

1. **Effort Application:** The operator applies an effort $F$ tangentially at the tip of the toggle arm. 
2. **Torque Generation:** This produces a torque about the screw axis: $T = F \times l$.
3. **Thread Engagement:** The threads convert this torque into an axial thrust force $W$ (the load).
4. **Frictional Loss:** Because of sliding friction in the threads ($\mu = \tan\phi$) and at the swivel pad ($\mu_2$), part of the input torque is lost as heat.
5. **Load Lifting:** The net available torque lifts the load vertically by one **pitch (p)** for every full revolution of the screw.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The screw jack is a **self-locking** device. The condition for self-locking is that the **helix angle ($\alpha$) must be less than the friction angle ($\phi$)**. If $\alpha < \phi$, the load will not descend on its own when the handle is released — a critical safety property for vehicle jacks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols and units are KTU-board standard. Memorize this table verbatim.**

| Sl. | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Torque required to **lift** load | $T_l = W \tan(\alpha + \phi) \times \dfrac{d_m}{2} + \mu_2 W \times \dfrac{d_c}{2}$ | $W$ = Load, $d_m$ = Mean thread dia, $d_c$ = Collar dia |
| 2 | Torque required to **lower** load | $T_{low} = W \tan(\phi - \alpha) \times \dfrac{d_m}{2} + \mu_2 W \times \dfrac{d_c}{2}$ | Valid only if $\alpha < \phi$ |
| 3 | Mean Thread Diameter | $d_m = d - 0.6495 \times p$ | $d$ = nominal (major) dia, $p$ = pitch |
| 4 | Helix Angle | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{p}{\pi \cdot d_m}$ | Angle of thread with horizontal plane |
| 5 | Friction Angle | $\tan\phi = \mu$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of thread friction |
| 6 | Mechanical Advantage (Ideal) | $MA = \dfrac{2\pi l}{p}$ | $l$ = toggle arm length |
| 7 | Velocity Ratio (VR) | $VR = \dfrac{2\pi l}{p}$ | Same as ideal MA (work in = work out) |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)}$ | Thread efficiency (ignoring collar friction) |
| 9 | Effort at Handle | $P = \dfrac{T_l}{l}$ | Tangential effort at the toggle tip |
| 10 | Self-locking condition | $\alpha < \phi$ | **Mandatory** for a safe screw jack |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Every car comes with a scissor/screw jack for roadside tire changes.
- **Heavy Engineering:** Used to lift railway coaches, bridges, and building foundations during repair.
- **Manufacturing:** The same screw-and-nut principle is used in **CNC lead screws, ball screws, and linear actuators**.
- **Tolerance Engineering:** In KTU labs, the drafting exercise teaches you to apply **GD&T symbols** (perpendicularity, concentricity) on the screw-cup interface — exactly as done in industry production drawings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Modeling, Assembly & Drafting Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodology Note:** The procedure below is **software-agnostic** and works identically in SolidWorks, CATIA, NX, Creo, and Fusion 360. Specific mouse-clicks are given for **SolidWorks** (the most common KTU lab software) in parentheses.

## 3.1 Phase 1 — Part Modeling of Individual Components

### Step 1: Model the Base (Body)
1. Start a new **Part** document. Select the **Front Plane** and sketch a rectangle.
2. Apply **Extrude Boss** feature with a height equal to the **length of the cup + 5 mm** (e.g., 40 mm).
3. Sketch a circle concentric to the rectangle on the top face.
4. Apply **Extruded Cut** to create the through-hole for the cup.

### Step 2: Model the Cup (Nut)
1. Start a new Part. Sketch the **thread profile** (a V-thread of 60° included angle) on the Front Plane.
2. Use the **Helix Curve** tool (Insert → Curve → Helix) with **Pitch** $p$ and **Height** $H$ matching the screw.
3. Sweep the thread profile along the helix curve. *(This is the KTU-standard thread modeling method.)*
4. Add a chamfer at the top edge for the load-bearing face.

### Step 3: Model the Main Screw
1. Sketch a circle of diameter $d$ on the Front Plane.
2. Apply **Revolve Boss** about the centerline to create the cylindrical blank.
3. Apply the **Cut-Extrude** with the same helix curve used for the cup to create matching internal threads on the screw.
4. Create the **head** of the screw (a rectangular pad or spherical cap) at the top.
5. Drill a **transverse hole** to receive the pivot pin.
6. Add the **swivel pad** as a small concave cylindrical cap at the very top.

### Step 4: Model the Toggle Arm
1. Sketch a long rectangular bar of length $l$ (typically 300–450 mm) and width 25 mm.
2. Extrude to 12 mm thickness.
3. Add a **through-hole** at one end (for the pivot pin) and a **knurled grip** at the other end (for ergonomics).

### Step 5: Model the Pivot Pin
1. Create a simple cylinder of standard pin diameter (e.g., 8 mm) with a small chamfer on both ends.

## 3.2 Phase 2 — Assembly Modeling (Bottom-Up Approach)

### Step 6: Create the Assembly File
1. **File → New → Assembly**. The first component inserted becomes the **fixed (grounded) part** — this is the **Base**.

### Step 7: Apply the Mates (Assembly Constraints)

| Mate Type | Applied Between | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Concentric** | Base inner hole ↔ Cup outer cylinder | Aligns the cup axis with the base bore |
| **Coincident** | Cup top face ↔ Base top face | Locks the cup height inside the base |
| **Concentric** | Cup inner thread ↔ Screw outer thread | Aligns the screw with the nut |
| **Distance** | Screw head bottom face ↔ Base top face | Defines the initial lift position (e.g., 20 mm) |
| **Concentric** | Toggle hole ↔ Pivot pin outer surface | Attaches toggle to pin |
| **Coincident** | Toggle bottom face ↔ Screw head side face | Locates the toggle beside the screw head |
| **Concentric** | Pivot pin ↔ Screw head transverse hole | Completes the toggle-pivot-screw linkage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Check:** A typical screw jack has 6 main components. Total DOF without mates = $6 \times 6 = 36$. Each mate removes 1 to 3 DOF. After applying the mates above, the **screw should retain exactly 1 rotational DOF** about its axis (for the screw motion). If you over-constrain, the software will throw a **"Redundant Mate"** error.

### Step 8: Verify with Motion Study
1. Switch to the **Motion Study** tab at the bottom.
2. Drag the **Motor** tool onto the toggle arm face. Set angular velocity (e.g., 30 rpm).
3. Play the animation. The screw should **rise and lower smoothly** with no interpenetration.

## 3.3 Phase 3 — 2D Drafting of the Assembly

### Step 9: Create the Drawing File
1. **File → New → Drawing**. Select **A4 Landscape** sheet and **BIS / ISO** drafting standard.
2. Insert the assembled screw jack as the **Standard 3 View** (Front, Top, Side).

### Step 10: Generate Required Views
- **Full Sectional Front View (AA):** Click **Section View** tool, draw a cutting plane line through the centerline, and tick **"Show hidden lines as visible in section"**. This exposes the internal thread engagement — the **most important view in a KTU viva**.
- **Detail View:** Zoom in on the thread engagement area and use the **Detail View** tool (circle of diameter ~25 mm) at a scale of 2:1.
- **Isometric View:** Right-click the view → **Orientation → Isometric**.

### Step 11: Apply Dimensions & Annotations
- Add **overall dimensions** (length, height, base width).
- Add the **thread callout** (e.g., M20 × 2.5 — 6H / 6g as per **IS 4218**).
- Insert **GD&T symbols** (perpendicularity of cup face, concentricity of thread).
- Add **part balloons** (numbered circles) with leader lines pointing to each part.

### Step 12: Generate the Bill of Materials (BOM)
1. Click **Tables → Bill of Materials**.
2. Choose **Top of Front View** placement. The software auto-populates the part names and quantities (e.g., Base-1, Cup-1, Screw-1, Toggle-1, Pin-1, Swivel Pad-1).
3. Fill in the **Title Block** with your Name, Roll No., Date, Scale (1:2), and Drawing Number (KTU/PCMUL507/EXP-02).

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Calculation (For Lab Record)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A screw jack has a screw of nominal diameter **$d = 30$ mm**, pitch **$p = 5$ mm**, mean collar diameter **$d_c = 40$ mm**, toggle arm length **$l = 350$ mm**, and must lift a load **$W = 10$ kN**. The coefficient of thread friction **$\mu = 0.15$** and collar friction **$\mu_2 = 0.18$**. Calculate the torque required, effort at handle, and efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate the mean thread diameter.**

$$d_m = d - 0.6495 \times p$$

$$d_m = 30 - 0.6495 \times 5 = 30 - 3.2475 = 26.7525 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the helix angle $\alpha$.**

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{p}{\pi \cdot d_m} = \dfrac{5}{\pi \times 26.7525} = \dfrac{5}{84.05} = 0.05949$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.05949) = 3.405^\circ$$

**Step 3: Calculate the thread friction angle $\phi$.**

$$\tan\phi = \mu = 0.15 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.15) = 8.531^\circ$$

**Step 4: Check the self-locking condition.**

Since $\alpha = 3.405^\circ < \phi = 8.531^\circ$, the screw jack is **self-locking** (safe to use).

**Step 5: Calculate the lifting torque $T_l$.**

$$T_l = W \tan(\alpha + \phi) \times \dfrac{d_m}{2} + \mu_2 W \times \dfrac{d_c}{2}$$

$$T_l = 10\,000 \times \tan(3.405^\circ + 8.531^\circ) \times \dfrac{26.7525}{2} + 0.18 \times 10\,000 \times \dfrac{40}{2}$$

$$T_l = 10\,000 \times \tan(11.936^\circ) \times 13.376 + 0.18 \times 10\,000 \times 20$$

$$T_l = 10\,000 \times 0.21145 \times 13.376 + 36\,000$$

$$T_l = 28\,280.3 + 36\,000 = 64\,280.3 \text{ N-mm} = 64.28 \text{ N-m}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the effort at the handle.**

$$P = \dfrac{T_l}{l} = \dfrac{64.28}{0.35} = 183.66 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the efficiency $\eta$.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)} = \dfrac{0.05949}{0.21145} = 0.2813 = 28.13\%$$

**Final Results:** $T_l = 64.28$ N-m, $P = 183.66$ N, $\eta = 28.13\%$, Self-locking is **confirmed**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose marks on screw jack questions due to these **3 high-frequency errors**:
> 1. **Forgetting the self-locking check** — Examiners award 2 marks specifically for verifying $\alpha < \phi$. Skipping this costs you 2/14.
> 2. **Confusing $d$ and $d_m$** — Using the nominal diameter (30 mm) in the torque formula instead of the mean diameter (26.75 mm) gives a wrong answer. Always derive $d_m$ first.
> 3. **In the drafting viva**, students often forget to mention the **BIS SP 46:2003** line conventions (e.g., hidden lines as short dashed lines, centerlines as long-short-long). Mentioning the standard explicitly adds 1 bonus mark in the viva.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define assembly modeling. List any four types of mates used in CAD assembly of a screw jack.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Assembly modeling is the process of combining individual 3D part files into a single product file by applying geometric relationships called **mates** that simulate the physical connections between components. (1 mark)

Four types of mates used in screw jack assembly: (½ mark each)
1. **Concentric Mate** — Aligns the axes of two cylindrical faces (used for cup-base, screw-cup, pin-toggle).
2. **Coincident Mate** — Aligns two planar faces flush (used for cup top to base top, toggle to screw head).
3. **Distance Mate** — Maintains a specified gap between two faces (used for initial screw lift position).
4. **Parallel Mate** — Keeps two planar faces parallel at a defined offset (used for toggle arm orientation).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the self-locking condition of a screw jack. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The self-locking condition states that the **helix angle $\alpha$ of the screw thread must be less than the friction angle $\phi$** of the thread surface. (1 mark)

Mathematically: $\alpha < \phi$  $\Rightarrow$  $\tan\alpha < \mu$ (1 mark)

**Importance:** If this condition is satisfied, the screw jack will not unwind or descend on its own when the operator releases the toggle arm. The load remains stationary at any lifted position, ensuring **operator safety** and **load stability**. This is critical for automotive jacks where the load is a vehicle and the operator is a person. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**A screw jack has the following specifications:**
- Nominal thread diameter $d = 40$ mm, Pitch $p = 6$ mm
- Mean collar diameter $d_c = 55$ mm
- Toggle arm length $l = 400$ mm
- Load $W = 25$ kN
- Coefficient of thread friction $\mu = 0.18$
- Coefficient of collar friction $\mu_2 = 0.20$

**(a)** Calculate the mean thread diameter, helix angle, and verify the self-locking condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the torque required to lift the load, the effort at the toggle tip, and the overall efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Mean thread diameter.
$$d_m = d - 0.6495 \times p = 40 - 0.6495 \times 6 = 40 - 3.897 = 36.103 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Helix angle.
$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{p}{\pi \cdot d_m} = \dfrac{6}{\pi \times 36.103} = \dfrac{6}{113.42} = 0.05290$$
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.05290) = 3.028^\circ$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Friction angle.
$$\tan\phi = \mu = 0.18 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.18) = 10.20^\circ$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Self-locking check.
Since $\alpha = 3.028^\circ < \phi = 10.20^\circ$, the screw jack is **self-locking** ✓. The load will not descend on its own.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Lifting torque from thread.
$$T_1 = W \tan(\alpha+\phi) \times \dfrac{d_m}{2} = 25\,000 \times \tan(13.228^\circ) \times \dfrac{36.103}{2}$$
$$T_1 = 25\,000 \times 0.23488 \times 18.0515 = 106\,000.4 \text{ N-mm}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Lifting torque from collar friction.
$$T_2 = \mu_2 W \times \dfrac{d_c}{2} = 0.20 \times 25\,000 \times 27.5 = 137\,500 \text{ N-mm}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Total lifting torque.
$$T_l = T_1 + T_2 = 106\,000.4 + 137\,500 = 243\,500.4 \text{ N-mm} = 243.5 \text{ N-m}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Effort at the handle.
$$P = \dfrac{T_l}{l} = \dfrac{243.5}{0.4} = 608.75 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Efficiency.
$$\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)} = \dfrac{0.05290}{0.23488} = 0.2252 = 22.52\%$$

**Final Answer:** $T_l = 243.5$ N-m, $P = 608.75$ N, $\eta = 22.52\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, list the **six main parts of a screw jack** and explain the function of any four. Describe the **drafting procedure** to generate a full sectional front view of the screw jack assembly using any CAD software. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for **bottom-up assembly modeling** of a screw jack in SolidWorks (or equivalent CAD software). Clearly state the mate types applied between the Base, Cup, Screw, Toggle, and Pivot Pin. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (3 Marks):** Six parts of a screw jack (with diagram):
1. **Base / Body** — Hollow cylindrical housing.
2. **Cup (Nut)** — Threaded bush for the spindle.
3. **Main Screw / Spindle** — Long threaded shaft.
4. **Toggle / Link Arm** — Lever for applying torque.
5. **Pivot Pin / Fulcrum Pin** — Connects toggle to screw head.
6. **Swivel Pad** — Concave pad bearing against the load.

**Functions (any 4, 1 mark each, total 4 marks — combined with the 3 marks above to give 7):**
- **Base:** Provides structural support and houses the cup.
- **Cup:** Engages with the screw threads to convert rotation into linear motion.
- **Main Screw:** Transmits the lifting thrust from the toggle to the load.
- **Toggle Arm:** Multiplies the operator's effort through lever action (Class II lever).
- **Swivel Pad:** Allows angular misalignment between the screw axis and the load surface.

**Step 2 (Extra / For full 7 marks — drafting procedure):**
1. Open **File → New → Drawing**.
2. Select **A4 Landscape** template.
3. Click **Model View** tool, browse to the screw jack assembly, and place **Standard 3 View** on the sheet.
4. Click **Section View** tool, draw a vertical cutting plane line through the centerline of the front view.
5. Tick **"Aligned Section"** and **"Show hidden lines"** options.
6. Place the sectioned view on the sheet. Apply **hatching (section lines)** at 45° to all cut surfaces.
7. Add **dimensions**, **part balloons**, and the **BOM table**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step-by-Step Bottom-Up Assembly Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Create individual part files for the 6 components and save them in a single folder.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Open **File → New → Assembly**. Insert the **Base** first. Right-click it and select **"Make Independent"** then **"Float"** or **"Ground"** it. The base is the **fixed component**.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Insert the **Cup**. Apply a **Concentric Mate** between the outer cylindrical face of the cup and the inner cylindrical hole of the base. Apply a **Coincident Mate** between the top face of the cup and the top face of the base.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Insert the **Main Screw**. Apply a **Concentric Mate** between the screw's outer thread and the cup's inner thread. Apply a **Distance Mate** between the bottom of the screw head and the top of the base, set to the initial lift position (e.g., 15 mm).

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Insert the **Pivot Pin** into the transverse hole of the screw head using a **Concentric Mate** between the pin and the hole.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Insert the **Toggle Arm**. Apply a **Concentric Mate** between the toggle's pivot hole and the pivot pin. Apply a **Coincident Mate** between the bottom face of the toggle and the side face of the screw head.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Insert the **Swivel Pad** on top of the screw using a **Concentric Mate** with the screw axis and a **Coincident Mate** with the top face. Open the **Motion Study** tab, add a **Rotational Motor** to the toggle, and verify smooth screw travel.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this to your wall the night before the lab exam.**

- [ ] A screw jack has **6 standard parts**: Base, Cup, Screw, Toggle, Pivot Pin, Swivel Pad.
- [ ] Assembly modeling uses **Mates** to combine parts — remember the **4 main mate types**: Concentric, Coincident, Distance, Parallel.
- [ ] The **first part inserted** in an assembly is the **fixed (grounded) part**.
- [ ] Use the **Helix + Sweep** method to model V-threads in CAD (do **not** use the cosmetic thread option for sectional views — it won't show in section).
- [ ] Mean diameter formula: $d_m = d - 0.6495p$ (this is **NOT** the nominal diameter).
- [ ] Helix angle: $\tan\alpha = p / (\pi d_m)$ — a very small angle, typically $2^\circ$ to $5^\circ$.
- [ ] **Self-locking is mandatory**: $\alpha < \phi$. Verify this **before** computing the torque.
- [ ] Lifting torque has **two components**: thread torque + collar friction torque. Forgetting the collar term is a common error.
- [ ] Efficiency of a screw jack is **always less than 50%** in real life due to friction — typical range is **20% to 35%**.
- [ ] Mechanical advantage is high (typically 100 to 500), but the trade-off is **very slow lifting speed**.
- [ ] For drafting, always include the **full sectional front view** in your A4 sheet — it is the **single most important view** for a screw jack.
- [ ] Use **BIS SP 46:2003** line conventions; reference **IS 4218** for thread callouts (e.g., M20 × 2.5).
- [ ] The **BOM table** should list all 6 parts with sequential balloon numbers (1 to 6) and quantities.
- [ ] In the **viva**, be ready to explain the **Motion Study** and why the screw self-locks when the handle is released.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Tail Stock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Assembly Models and Drafting of Tail Stock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Assembly Modeling — The Formal KTU Definition

**Assembly Modeling** is a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) process in which multiple individual part models (created in part files) are geometrically constrained and positioned together in a single virtual environment to form a complete functional mechanism or product. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats this as the second stage of the design cycle, following individual part creation (Module Experiment 1).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim syllabus intent):**
> *"An assembly is a collection of two or more parts that are positioned together to allow the visualization and validation of the design intent, the simulation of the mechanism, and the generation of assembly drawings for manufacturing."*

The assembly file (often with extension `.iam` in Autodesk Inventor, `.asm` in SolidWorks, or `.prt` in Creo/Pro-E) does not contain geometry itself; it contains **references** to the part files through spatial relationships called **assembly constraints** (or **mates**).

## 1.2 Drafting — The Formal KTU Definition

**Drafting** in CAD refers to the process of generating two-dimensional orthographic engineering drawings from a 3D model (part or assembly). The drafting file (`.idw` in Inventor, `.slddrw` in SolidWorks, or `.drw` in Creo) embeds views of the 3D model, applies engineering annotations (dimensions, tolerances, surface finish symbols), and follows national/international drawing standards (IS 696, ISO 128, ASME Y14.5).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module Experiment 2, the drafting stage requires producing a fully dimensioned **2D assembly drawing of the Tail Stock** along with a **parts list (Bill of Materials)** and a **detailed drawing of any one sub-component** (commonly the Body or the Quill).

## 1.3 What is a Tail Stock? — Machine Context

The **Tail Stock** (also spelled *Tailstock*) is the movable support unit mounted on the **bed ways** of a **lathe machine** at the opposite end of the headstock. Its primary function is to **support the free (unsupported) end of long workpieces** during turning, drilling, reaming, and grinding operations.

### 1.3.1 Real-World Analogy — "The Hand That Holds the Other End"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine you are trying to rotate a long pencil between your two palms to sharpen it. One hand (the headstock) rotates the pencil, while the other hand (the tail stock) **just supports** the other end so it does not wobble or bend. The tail stock is that second, non-rotating hand — it carries a sharp point (the **dead center**) that fits into a pre-drilled hole at the workpiece end. This hand can slide along the bed (to accommodate different workpiece lengths) and can advance or retract a small shaft (the **quill**) to push the center firmly into the workpiece.

### 1.3.2 Standard Constituent Parts of a Tail Stock

A typical lathe tail stock consists of the following sub-assemblies and parts (the exact number may vary from **8 to 14 parts** depending on the design — KTU typically expects a 7–10 part assembly):

| Sl. No. | Part Name | Function |
|:-------:|:-----------|:---------|
| 1 | **Base (Bottom Slide)** | Slides on the lathe bed ways; clamped via a locking lever |
| 2 | **Body (Top Slide)** | Upper casting; houses the quill mechanism; slides on the base |
| 3 | **Quill (Spindle)** | Hollow cylindrical shaft that slides axially inside the body |
| 4 | **Dead Center** | Hardened 60° (or Morse Taper) point held in the quill bore |
| 5 | **Quill Handle (Feed Lever)** | Rotating lever with a ball/handle used to advance/retract the quill |
| 6 | **Handle Hub / Handle Disc** | The disc mounted on the quill screw to which the handle is attached |
| 7 | **Locking Bolts (Handle Lock & Quill Lock)** | Two socket-head screws — one to lock the body-to-base, one to lock the quill |
| 8 | **Graduated Dial (Thimble)** | Calibrated ring on the quill screw for measuring quill travel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Dimensions (typical S-II lab exercise):**
> Overall tail stock length ≈ **300–360 mm**; body height ≈ **120–140 mm**; quill diameter ≈ **40–50 mm**; quill travel ≈ **100–130 mm**; base footprint ≈ **120 × 220 mm**. Center height must match headstock center height (typically **100 mm** or **150 mm** for the lab models).

### 1.3.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Morse Taper sizes** for tail stock centers: MT-2, MT-3, MT-4 (must match headstock spindle taper).
- **Center point angle**: **60°** (metric standard, dead center).
- **Bed way width**: standardized to lathe size — KTU lab lathe = **180 mm** or **200 mm**.
- **Material of Body/Base**: **Cast Iron (Grade FG 200 / IS 210)** — high damping, vibration-absorbing.
- **Material of Quill**: **Medium Carbon Steel (EN8 / AISI 1045)**, case-hardened to 50–55 HRC.
- **Material of Dead Center**: **High Speed Steel (HSS, M2)** or **Carbide tipped**, hardened to **60–62 HRC**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bottom-Up vs Top-Down Assembly Strategy

KTU 2024 expects students to understand the assembly modeling philosophy. There are two principal strategies:

### 2.1.1 Bottom-Up Assembly (Industry & KTU Standard)
1. Create each **part** as an independent `.ipt` / `.SLDPRT` / `.PRT` file.
2. Place the first part as the **fixed (grounded)** component.
3. Insert the next part and apply **constraints (mates)** to position it.
4. Repeat for all parts until the assembly is complete.
5. Add **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and **interference check**.

> **Why this approach is preferred for KTU labs:** Easier grading — examiner can open each part file and check the individual geometry.

### 2.1.2 Top-Down Assembly (Skeleton-Driven)
1. Create a **skeleton/layout** model defining key positions, axes, and planes.
2. Create parts **in-context** of the assembly using references to the skeleton.
3. Geometry is automatically updated if the skeleton changes.

> **Why used in industry:** Supports concurrent engineering, design intent, and parametric family variants.

## 2.2 Assembly Constraints (Mates) — The Logical Hierarchy

A **mate** is a geometric relationship that removes degrees of freedom (DOF) between two components. Each rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translational + 3 rotational). A fully constrained assembly reduces the relative DOF to **zero** for static parts.

| Mate Type | Removes DOF | Use Case in Tail Stock |
|:----------|:------------|:-----------------------|
| **Coincident (Mate)** | 3 translational (aligns faces) | Quill end face ↔ Body bore bottom |
| **Concentric (Mate)** | 2 translational (aligns axes) | Quill OD ↔ Body bore ID |
| **Insert (Mate)** | 2 translational (combined) | Dead center Morse taper ↔ Quill bore |
| **Distance (Offset Mate)** | 1 translational | Quill-to-handle-hub gap |
| **Angle (Mate)** | 1 rotational | Handle lever ↔ Quill axis (typically 90°) |
| **Tangent (Mate)** | 1 translational | — (Not common in tail stock) |
| **Planar (Align)** | 3 translational | Base bottom ↔ Bed way top |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often leave the quill **under-constrained**. The quill has 1 DOF (axial sliding) in the real machine, but in a CAD assembly you should **fully constrain** it to a *specific position* for the assembly drawing, while still leaving the **component file** unconstrained internally (so it can still slide during the kinematic study — KTU advanced experiment).

## 2.3 Drafting Standards (IS 696 / SP 46)

KTU 2024 evaluates drafting output against the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** conventions:

- **First Angle Projection** is the standard used in India (and KTU question papers).
- **Line types**: Continuous thick (visible edges), continuous thin (dimension/leader), dashed (hidden), chain thin (centerline).
- **Dimensioning**: Aligned dimensions for shafts, linear dimensions for lengths, angular dimensions for the 60° center.
- **Sectional views**: Use **full section** or **half section** for symmetrical parts.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Tail Stock Engineering Geometry

The following formulas are essential for dimensioning the tail stock assembly drawing and the individual part drawings. They cover taper geometry, fit tolerances, and force transmission during lathe operation.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Application in Tail Stock |
|:-:|:------------------|:------------|:---------------------------|
| 1 | Morse Taper per unit length (taper ratio) | $$T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$$ | Standard MT-2: $T = 0.0496$ (≈ 1:20) over 2.030 in length |
| 2 | Self-holding condition (Morse) | $$\tan \alpha < \mu$$ where $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{2} \arctan T$ | Ensures dead center does not slip — half-angle must be < friction angle |
| 3 | Quill axial travel distance | $$L_{travel} = n \cdot p_{thread}$$ | $n$ = number of handle rotations, $p$ = quill screw pitch (typical 2.5 mm) |
| 4 | Center height tolerance | $$H_{center} = H_{nom} \pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$$ | Must match headstock center height within **0.02 mm** |
| 5 | Bending stress in long workpiece (relevant context) | $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$$ | Tail stock reduces deflection $\delta$ from $\dfrac{F L^3}{3EI}$ (cantilever) to $\dfrac{F L^3}{48 EI}$ (simply supported) |
| 6 | Quill cross-section (hollow) | $$I_{quill} = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right)$$ | Required rigidity of the quill against bending under cutting force |
| 7 | Morse Taper standard size (MT-2 example) | $$D = 17.780 \text{ mm}, \; d = 14.060 \text{ mm}, \; L = 51.99 \text{ mm}$$ | Reference for designing the quill bore taper |
| 8 | Clamping force on bed way | $$F_{clamp} \geq \mu_s \cdot W_{tailstock}$$ | Prevents tail stock from sliding under cutting force |
| 9 | Handle mechanical advantage | $$MA = \dfrac{L_{handle}}{r_{quill-screw}}$$ | Typical $MA = 4$ to $6$ for hand-lever tail stocks |
| 10 | Quill feed (axial displacement per revolution) | $$f = p \cdot \eta_{screw}$$ | $\eta_{screw} \approx 0.40$ for square thread, $0.30$ for ACME |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** For the drafting question, you will likely be asked to dimension the **60° angle** of the dead center and the **Morse taper angle** ($\approx 1.489°$ half-angle, total taper $\approx 2.98°$). Memorize: MT taper = **5/8 inch per foot (≈ 0.0496)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Tail Stock Design Knowledge

- **CNC lathe programming**: Knowing the quill travel is essential for the operator to know the maximum drillable depth without re-positioning the tail stock.
- **Process planning**: Tail stock must be retracted before parting-off operations to avoid collision.
- **Vibration analysis**: Quill rigidity directly affects surface finish on slender workpieces.
