# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Design Analysis (Phase 1)

Before opening the CAD software, perform the following analytical steps. KTU lab viva questions are frequently drawn from this section.

**Step 3.1.1** — Identify the design specifications from the KTU problem statement:
- Center height ($H_c$) = **100 mm** (typical for lab lathe)
- Quill bore = **Morse Taper No. 2 (MT-2)**
- Quill travel ($L_{travel}$) ≥ **100 mm**
- Base footprint to match bed width = **180 mm**
- Number of parts in assembly = **8 to 10**

**Step 3.1.2** — List the parts and assign file names:

| Part No. | File Name | Total Parts |
|:--------:|:----------|:-----------:|
| 1 | `Base.ipt` | 1 |
| 2 | `Body.ipt` | 1 |
| 3 | `Quill.ipt` | 1 |
| 4 | `DeadCenter.ipt` | 1 |
| 5 | `HandleHub.ipt` | 1 |
| 6 | `Handle.ipt` | 1 |
| 7 | `HandleBolt.ipt` | 1 |
| 8 | `QuillLockBolt.ipt` | 1 |
| 9 | `GraduatedDial.ipt` | 1 (optional) |
| **Total** | | **9 parts** |

## 3.2 Part Modeling Phase (Phase 2)

> [!NOTE]
> Module Experiment 1 covers the detailed part creation. For Module Experiment 2 (assembly), the parts are **assumed to be pre-created** and are imported into the assembly file.

### 3.2.1 Critical Part Dimensions for the Assembly

**(a) Base (`Base.ipt`)**
- Overall: 220 mm (length) × 120 mm (width) × 50 mm (height)
- Bottom: Two V-grooves (angle **90°**) for the lathe bed ways.
- Top: A T-slot or guide for the body to slide; a clamping slot at the rear.

**(b) Body (`Body.ipt`)**
- Lower flange: matches base width (120 mm) for sliding contact.
- Main cylinder: outer diameter ≈ **100 mm**, internal bore = Morse Taper MT-2 (entry Ø 17.78 mm at top, exit Ø 14.06 mm at quill contact).
- Quill exit hole: Ø **40 mm** at the front face (where the dead center protrudes).
- Quill lock threaded hole: M10 × 1.5 tapped hole on the side.
- Handle hub mounting: through-hole Ø **12 mm** perpendicular to the quill axis at the rear.

**(c) Quill (`Quill.ipt`)**
- External cylindrical body: Ø 40 mm × 180 mm long.
- Internal Morse Taper MT-2: matches the body bore.
- External ACME / square thread at the rear: Ø 30 mm, pitch = 2.5 mm, length = 60 mm.
- A circumferential groove at the front for the graduated dial (optional).

**(d) Dead Center (`DeadCenter.ipt`)**
- Morse Taper shank: MT-2 (matches quill bore).
- Working tip: 60° included angle, tip radius ≈ 0.4 mm.
- Total length ≈ **90 mm**.

**(e) Handle Hub (`HandleHub.ipt`)**
- Disc: Ø 60 mm × 12 mm thick.
- Internal thread: M30 × 1.5 (or ACME internal, matches quill thread).
- Center hole: Ø 12 mm for the handle bolt.
- 6 × Ø 6 mm peripheral holes for counterweight balance.

**(f) Handle (`Handle.ipt`)**
- Lever: 150 mm long × 20 mm diameter.
- Ball grip: Ø 30 mm at the free end.
- Mounting shank: Ø 12 mm with M10 internal thread.

**(g) Handle Bolt (`HandleBolt.ipt`)**
- M10 socket head cap screw, length 40 mm.

**(h) Quill Lock Bolt (`QuillLockBolt.ipt`)**
- M10 socket head cap screw with a dog-point (to press the quill).

## 3.3 Assembly Modeling Phase (Phase 3) — Exhaustive Procedure

### Step 3.3.1 — Create the Assembly File
- Open the CAD software (Autodesk Inventor / SolidWorks / Creo).
- **File → New → Assembly** (`.iam` / `.SLDASM` / `.ASM`).
- Save as `TailStock_Assembly.iam`.

### Step 3.3.2 — Place the First Part (Base — Grounded)
- **Assemble → Component → Place** → select `Base.ipt`.
- The first part is **automatically grounded** (fixed in space). This is critical — KTU students often forget to ground the base, causing the whole assembly to float.

### Step 3.3.3 — Insert the Body
- **Assemble → Component → Place** → select `Body.ipt`.
- It appears in the graphics window at an arbitrary position.

### Step 3.3.4 — Apply the First Mate: Body to Base
- **Assemble → Constraint → Mate** (or `Place Component` dialog → `Constrain`).
- Select the **top face of the Base** and the **bottom face of the Body** → apply **Mate (Coincident)**.
- Select the **left side face of the Base** and the **left side face of the Body** → apply **Mate (Coincident)**.
- Select the **back face of the Base** and the **back face of the Body** → apply **Mate (Coincident)**.
- **Result:** Body is fully constrained on the base. DOF = 0.

### Step 3.3.5 — Insert the Quill
- **Assemble → Component → Place** → select `Quill.ipt`.

### Step 3.3.6 — Apply Mates: Quill to Body
- Select the **outer cylindrical face of the Quill** and the **inner cylindrical face of the Body bore** → apply **Concentric (Insert) Mate**.
- Select the **rear face of the Quill flange** and the **rear inner face of the Body** → apply **Coincident Mate** (pushes the quill fully into the body).
- Select the **top face of the Quill ACME thread** and the **top inner face of the Body** → apply **Coincident Mate**.
- **Result:** Quill is fully positioned inside the body.

### Step 3.3.7 — Insert and Mate the Handle Hub
- Insert `HandleHub.ipt`.
- Select the **outer cylindrical face of the Hub** and the **outer cylindrical face of the Quill threaded section** → **Concentric Mate**.
- Select the **front face of the Hub** and the **rear face of the Quill thread end** → **Coincident Mate**.
- The hub should be **screwed onto the quill thread** in real life — for a static assembly, we lock them together.

### Step 3.3.8 — Insert and Mate the Handle
- Insert `Handle.ipt`.
- Select the **cylindrical mounting shank of the Handle** and a **Ø 12 mm hole on the Hub** → **Concentric Mate**.
- Select a face on the handle and the **side face of the Hub** → **Coincident Mate**.
- Set the handle lever direction to be at **90° to the lathe axis** (for operator clearance).

### Step 3.3.9 — Insert and Mate the Handle Bolt
- Insert `HandleBolt.ipt`.
- **Concentric Mate** the bolt shank with the handle hole.
- **Coincident Mate** the bolt head with the handle face.

### Step 3.3.10 — Insert the Dead Center
- Insert `DeadCenter.ipt`.
- **Concentric Mate** the Morse Taper shank with the Quill internal bore (front side).
- This applies the **Insert (Taper) Mate** in some CAD systems (e.g., SolidWorks).

### Step 3.3.11 — Insert the Quill Lock Bolt
- Insert `QuillLockBolt.ipt`.
- **Concentric Mate** the bolt shank with the **M10 threaded hole on the Body side**.
- The bolt tip should press against the Quill OD — for static assembly, the bolt tip is positioned at a clearance of **0.5 mm** to show the locking function.

### Step 3.3.12 — Insert the Graduated Dial (Optional)
- Insert `GraduatedDial.ipt`.
- **Concentric Mate** with the Quill OD.
- Position at the front of the Quill, behind the dead center.

### Step 3.3.13 — Validate the Assembly
- **Tools → Analyze → Interference Detection** → Run analysis. Expected output: **No interferences** (except possibly the bolt-to-quill, which should be cleared).
- **Tools → Analyze → Measure** → Verify center height = 100 mm from the base bottom to the dead center tip.
- **File → Save** the assembly.

## 3.4 Drafting Phase (Phase 4) — Detailed Procedure

### Step 3.4.1 — Create the Drafting File
- **File → New → Drawing** (`.idw` / `.SLDDRW` / `.DRW`).
- Select a **standard sheet size — A2 (594 × 420 mm)** for the assembly drawing; **A3 (420 × 297 mm)** for the part drawing.
- Select the **border and title block** template (institute standard).
- Select the **projection angle** — **First Angle** (as per IS 696).

### Step 3.4.2 — Place the Standard Views of the Assembly
- **Place View → Base** (parent view) → select the assembly file → select **Front View** orientation.
- **Projected View** → Top View (above front, in first angle).
- **Projected View** → Left Side View (right of front, in first angle).
- **Isometric View** → place in the lower-right corner for pictorial reference.

### Step 3.4.3 — Apply Sectional View (Recommended for Assembly)
- **Section View** → choose the cutting plane to pass through the lathe axis (longitudinal full section).
- This reveals the **Morse Taper joint** between the dead center and the quill, and the **ACME thread** of the quill-hub joint.
- Hatch the cut surfaces: **Cast Iron = 45° cross-hatch at 1.5 mm spacing**; **Steel parts = 45° parallel hatch at 1.5 mm spacing**.

### Step 3.4.4 — Add Dimensions to the Assembly Drawing
- **Overall dimensions**: 360 × 120 × 200 mm (L × W × H).
- **Key interface dimensions**: Center height = 100 mm; Quill travel = 100 mm; Base width = 120 mm.
- **Angular dimension**: 60° (dead center tip).
- Use **aligned dimensioning** for the quill diameter.
- Use **linear dimensioning** for the base length.

### Step 3.4.5 — Add Balloons and Item Numbers
- **Annotate → Balloon** → number each part: 1, 2, 3, … 9.
- Place the balloons on the **isometric view** with leader lines to the corresponding parts.

### Step 3.4.6 — Insert the Parts List (Bill of Materials)
- **Table → Parts List** → select the assembly → place below the title block.
- Columns: **Item No. | Part Name | Material | Qty**.
- Example row: `3 | Quill | EN8 Steel | 1`.

### Step 3.4.7 — Add Title Block Information
- Drawing Title: **TAIL STOCK — ASSEMBLY**
- Drawing No.: **TS-ASM-001**
- Scale: **1:2** (typical)
- Drawn By / Checked By / Approved By / Date.

### Step 3.4.8 — Create the Part Drawing (Detailed)
- For the **Quill** (or any other part), create a new drawing sheet.
- Place **Front view, Top view, Side view, and Sectional front view**.
- Add **all dimensions** with tolerances (e.g., Ø 40 ± 0.05, thread Ø 30 ACME).
- Add the **Morse Taper dimension** with a callout: "MT-2, 1:20 Taper".
- Specify **surface finish**, **GD&T** (e.g., concentricity of the Morse bore to the OD = Ø 0.02), and **material specification**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DOF Reduction Table for the Tail Stock

| Step | Part Placed | Mates Applied | DOF Remaining | Comments |
|:----:|:------------|:---------------|:--------------|:---------|
| 1 | Base | Grounded | 0 | Fixed reference |
| 2 | Body | 3 Coincident | 0 | Sliding in real life; fixed in assembly drawing |
| 3 | Quill | 2 Concentric + 1 Coincident | 0 | Axial sliding not modeled (kinematic study separate) |
| 4 | Handle Hub | 1 Concentric + 1 Coincident | 0 | Threaded engagement modeled statically |
| 5 | Handle | 1 Concentric + 1 Coincident | 0 | Handle orientation set |
| 6 | Handle Bolt | 1 Concentric + 1 Coincident | 0 | Fastener |
| 7 | Dead Center | 1 Concentric (Taper) | 0 | Taper mate |
| 8 | Quill Lock Bolt | 1 Concentric | 0 | Fastener |
| 9 | Graduated Dial | 1 Concentric | 0 | Optional |

**Final DOF of the entire assembly = 0.** Assembly is fully constrained and ready for drafting.

## 3.6 Mathematical Validation — Quill Rigidity Check

To validate the design intent (a viva question), compute the maximum deflection of the quill under a radial cutting force.

Given:
- Cutting force $F_c = 500$ N (typical for mild steel turning on a lab lathe).
- Quill overhang length $L = 180$ mm = 0.180 m.
- Quill outer diameter $D_o = 40$ mm = 0.040 m.
- Quill inner diameter $D_i = 32$ mm = 0.032 m.
- Young's modulus for EN8 steel: $E = 210$ GPa = $210 \times 10^9$ N/m².

**Step 1 — Second moment of area:**

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left((0.040)^4 - (0.032)^4\right)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(2.560 \times 10^{-7} - 1.04858 \times 10^{-7}\right)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(1.5114 \times 10^{-7}\right)$$

$$I = 7.42 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Maximum deflection (cantilever):**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{F_c L^3}{3 E I}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{500 \times (0.180)^3}{3 \times 210 \times 10^9 \times 7.42 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{500 \times 5.832 \times 10^{-3}}{4.676}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2.916}{4.676} = 0.623 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.62 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** The quill deflects by **0.62 mm** under maximum cutting force. This is within the acceptable range for general-purpose turning (typically ≤ 1 mm). Hence, the quill diameter of 40 mm is **adequate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Question:** "If you want to halve the deflection, by what factor must you increase the OD?" Answer: $\delta \propto 1/D^4$, so you need $D^{4}_{new} = 2 D^{4}_{old}$, giving $D_{new} = 2^{1/4} D_{old} \approx 1.19 D_{old}$, i.e., increase the OD by 19% (to about 47.5 mm).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Assembly Constraint" in CAD. List any four commonly used assembly constraints with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **assembly constraint** (also called a *mate* in SolidWorks/Inventor terminology) is a geometric relationship applied between two or more components in a CAD assembly to control their relative position and orientation, thereby reducing the relative degrees of freedom (DOF) between them. Each constraint removes a specific number of DOF from the 6 possible (3 translational + 3 rotational).

**Four commonly used assembly constraints:**

1. **Coincident (Mate):** Aligns two planar faces so they lie in the same plane. Removes **3 translational DOF**.
2. **Concentric (Insert):** Aligns two cylindrical faces (or circular edges) so they share a common axis. Removes **2 translational DOF**.
3. **Distance (Offset):** Maintains a specified gap or offset between two faces. Removes **1 translational DOF**.
4. **Angle:** Maintains a specified angle between two planar faces or edges. Removes **1 rotational DOF**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Morse Taper? Why is it used in the tail stock quill assembly?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Morse Taper (MT)** is a standardized self-holding taper system invented by Stephen A. Morse, used extensively in machine tools for mounting tools and accessories. The taper ratio is approximately **5/8 inch per foot (≈ 0.0496, or 1:20)**, giving a total included angle of about **2° 58'**.

**Reasons for using Morse Taper in the tail stock quill assembly:**

1. **Self-holding:** The small taper angle ($\alpha \approx 1.49°$ half-angle) is less than the friction angle ($\tan^{-1}\mu$ for steel-on-steel ≈ 5–8°), so the dead center locks in place purely by friction — no clamp needed.
2. **Easy tool change:** Dead center can be removed and re-inserted quickly.
3. **Self-centering:** The taper automatically aligns the center axis with the quill axis.
4. **Standardization:** MT-2, MT-3, MT-4 are interchangeable with headstock spindles, drill chucks, and reamer holders.
5. **High torsional rigidity:** Transmits turning torque without slippage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Bottom-Up** and **Top-Down** approaches of assembly modeling. State the advantages and disadvantages of each. Which approach is preferred for the KTU Tail Stock assembly exercise, and why?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled **sectional front view**, describe the construction and working of a lathe **Tail Stock**. Label at least **6 parts** in the diagram.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Bottom-Up Assembly Approach:**

In the **bottom-up** approach, each individual part is first modeled as a separate part file, fully dimensioned and constrained internally. The parts are then inserted into an assembly file, where assembly-level constraints (mates) are applied to position them relative to one another. The first part placed is typically **grounded** (fixed in space) to act as a reference.

- **Advantages:** (i) Simple to implement and verify; (ii) Each part file is independent and can be modified without affecting the assembly hierarchy; (iii) Suitable for parts with established designs; (iv) Easier to manage in large teams where different engineers design different parts.
- **Disadvantages:** (i) In-context relationships are difficult to capture; (ii) Design changes in one part may require manual updates in the assembly; (iii) Not suitable when the assembly layout drives the part design.

**Top-Down Assembly Approach:**

In the **top-down** approach, an **assembly skeleton** (or layout) model is first created that defines the key positions, axes, planes, and interface dimensions. The individual parts are then created **in-context** of the assembly — they reference the skeleton's geometry (e.g., a plane or axis) so that any change in the skeleton automatically propagates to all dependent parts.

- **Advantages:** (i) Design intent is captured centrally; (ii) Changes propagate automatically to all parts; (iii) Ideal for families of similar products; (iv) Supports concurrent engineering.
- **Disadvantages:** (i) More complex to set up; (ii) Requires careful management of external references; (iii) Slower initial setup; (iv) Risk of circular references if not managed properly.

**Preferred approach for the KTU Tail Stock Exercise:**

The **bottom-up** approach is preferred for the KTU Tail Stock lab exercise because:
1. The parts are **pre-designed** with fixed dimensions, and the design intent is already established by the syllabus.
2. It is **easier for examiners to grade** — each part file can be opened and verified independently.
3. Students can **modularly complete** the parts (Module Experiment 1) and the assembly (Module Experiment 2) in separate sessions.
4. The assembly is a one-off mechanism with no family variants, so the benefits of top-down (parametric families) are not utilized.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Construction of the Lathe Tail Stock:**

The tail stock consists of a **Base (1)** that slides on the V-ways of the lathe bed. The base has a clamping lever at the rear (not shown) to lock the tail stock at any desired position. Mounted on top of the base is the **Body (2)**, which is a hollow casting that houses the **Quill (3)** and the Morse Taper bore.

The **Quill (3)** is a hollow cylindrical shaft that slides axially inside the body bore. Its rear end carries an **ACME (or square) thread** that engages with the internal thread of the **Handle Hub (4)**. The **Handle (5)** is attached to the hub via a **Handle Bolt (6)**. Rotating the handle causes the quill to advance or retract, moving the **Dead Center (7)** in or out.

The dead center has a **60° point** and a Morse Taper shank that fits into the front bore of the quill. A **Graduated Dial (8)** is fitted on the quill to indicate the amount of quill travel. Two lock bolts are provided: the **Body Lock (9)** clamps the body to the base, and the **Quill Lock (10)** clamps the quill to the body after positioning.

**Working:**

1. The tail stock is **slid along the bed** until the dead center is aligned with the workpiece end.
2. The **body lock** is tightened to fix the tail stock position.
3. The **handle** is rotated, advancing the quill and pressing the dead center firmly into the pre-drilled center hole at the workpiece end.
4. The **quill lock** is tightened to maintain the quill position.
5. The **cutting tool** can now operate along the length of the workpiece, with the dead center providing support that prevents deflection and vibration.

**Labeled Sectional View (textual representation):**

> **Reference:** See the Assembly drawing generated in Step 3.4.3. The **section plane** passes through the vertical centerline of the tail stock. The section reveals:
> - The **Morse Taper joint** between the dead center shank and the quill bore.
> - The **ACME thread** engagement between the quill and the handle hub.
> - The **bed-way contact** between the base and the lathe bed.
> - Hatching: **45° cross-hatch for cast iron parts** (base, body) and **45° parallel hatch for steel parts** (quill, dead center, fasteners).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** List the **standard parts** (BOM) of the lathe tail stock assembly with material specifications. State the function of each part in 1–2 lines.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **Morse Taper** system. Derive the relationship between the taper ratio, the half-angle, and the self-holding condition.

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Item | Part Name | Qty | Material | Function |
|:----:|:----------|:----:|:---------|:---------|
| 1 | Base | 1 | Cast Iron (FG 200) | Slides on the lathe bed ways; carries the body. |
| 2 | Body | 1 | Cast Iron (FG 200) | Houses the quill bore and the Morse Taper socket. |
| 3 | Quill | 1 | EN8 Steel (case hardened) | Sliding shaft that carries the dead center. |
| 4 | Dead Center | 1 | HSS (M2) | Supports the workpiece free end. |
| 5 | Handle Hub | 1 | Mild Steel | Disc that engages the quill ACME thread. |
| 6 | Handle | 1 | Mild Steel with ball grip | Lever to rotate the quill feed mechanism. |
| 7 | Handle Bolt | 1 | High Tensile Steel (Gr 8.8) | Clamps the handle to the hub. |
| 8 | Quill Lock Bolt | 1 | High Tensile Steel (Gr 8.8) | Locks the quill at a desired position. |
| 9 | Graduated Dial | 1 | Brass | Indicates the quill axial displacement. |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Morse Taper System:**

The Morse Taper is a self-holding taper system used to mount tools (drills, reamers, dead centers) in machine tool spindles. The system was standardized by Stephen A. Morse in the 1860s. The taper ratio is defined as:

$$T = \frac{D - d}{L}$$

where:
- $D$ = large-end diameter (mm)
- $d$ = small-end diameter (mm)
- $L$ = axial length of the taper (mm)

For a standard **MT-2** taper:
- $D = 17.780$ mm
- $d = 14.060$ mm
- $L = 51.994$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute the taper ratio:**

$$T = \frac{17.780 - 14.060}{51.994} = \frac{3.720}{51.994} = 0.07155$$

This corresponds to **5/8 inch per foot** (≈ 0.0496 metric), but the actual MT-2 standard is slightly different. (Note: KTU expects the value **T ≈ 0.05 to 0.10**, depending on the standard cited.)

**Step 2 — Compute the half-angle:**

The taper ratio is related to the half-angle $\alpha$ by:

$$T = 2 \tan \alpha$$

Therefore:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{T}{2}$$

$$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{T}{2}\right) = \arctan(0.0358) \approx 2.05°$$

**Step 3 — Self-holding condition:**

The Morse taper is self-holding (i.e., the male taper does not slip out under axial load) if the half-angle $\alpha$ is **less than the friction angle** $\phi$, where:

$$\tan \phi = \mu_s$$

For steel-on-steel sliding contact (with cutting fluid), the static friction coefficient is $\mu_s \approx 0.10$ to $0.15$. Therefore:

$$\phi = \arctan(0.12) \approx 6.84°$$

Since $\alpha = 2.05° < \phi = 6.84°$, the **self-holding condition is satisfied** for MT-2. This is why the dead center stays in place under axial load without any external clamping.

**Step 4 — Tangential holding force:**

The axial holding force $F_a$ that the taper can transmit is:

$$F_a = F_f \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)$$

where $F_f$ is the frictional force at the taper interface. For typical cutting loads, the taper provides ample holding force.

**Sketch (textual representation):**

> A Morse Taper joint is drawn as two concentric truncated cones. The **male taper** (on the tool) is inserted into the **female taper** (in the quill bore). The **60° working point** protrudes from the front. The **taper angle 2α ≈ 4°** is shown on the section view. The contact length is $L$ along the axis. Standard callout in the drawing: "**MT-2, 1:20 Taper**".

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Compiled from KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 
> 1. **Missing Bill of Materials (BOM):** A parts list is **MANDATORY** on the assembly drawing. Drawing without BOM = lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Third Angle Projection used instead of First Angle:** KTU follows **First Angle Projection** (Indian Standard IS 696). Using third angle = lose **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong hatching pattern:** Hatching the wrong material (e.g., hatching steel with the cast-iron cross-hatch pattern) = lose **0.5–1 mark** per error.
> 4. **No section view in the assembly drawing:** A full section through the quill is **highly recommended** to show the Morse Taper and ACME thread. Missing it = lose **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting the title block:** All sheets must have a **fully filled title block** with name, roll no., date, scale, and drawing number.
> 6. **Dimensioning the assembly drawing as a part drawing:** Assembly drawings should have **only overall and key interface dimensions** (5–8 dimensions max). Microscopic dimensions of individual parts belong in the part drawings.
> 7. **Leaving parts un-mated (floating):** All parts in the assembly file must be **fully constrained** (0 DOF). An under-constrained assembly causes warnings and = lose **1 mark**.
> 8. **Not using standard tolerances:** Dimensions like "100 mm" must be qualified as "100 ± 0.05" or "100 H7/g6" for mating parts.
> 9. **Confusing Morse Taper and Self-Opening Taper:** The Morse Taper is **self-holding**; some students mistakenly draw it as a self-extracting taper.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition of Assembly Modeling:** Collection of parts positioned via constraints to validate design intent.
- [ ] **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a rigid body in 3D space (3T + 3R); each mate removes a specific number of DOF.
- [ ] **Bottom-Up vs Top-Down:** KTU lab = Bottom-Up.
- [ ] **First part placed is GROUNDED** (fixed). Forgetting this is a critical error.
- [ ] **Common Mates:** Coincident, Concentric, Distance, Angle, Tangent.
- [ ] **Morse Taper = 1:20 ratio (≈ 5/8 inch per foot)**, half-angle ≈ 1.5° to 2°.
- [ ] **Self-holding condition:** Half-angle < friction angle.
- [ ] **Dead center angle = 60°** (included angle).
- [ ] **Standard MT sizes:** MT-2, MT-3, MT-4. KTU lab typically uses **MT-2** (D = 17.780 mm, d = 14.060 mm, L = 51.994 mm).
- [ ] **Number of parts in the KTU tail stock assembly: 8–10** (base, body, quill, dead center, handle hub, handle, two bolts, optional dial).
- [ ] **Materials:** Base/Body = Cast Iron FG 200; Quill = EN8 Steel; Dead Center = HSS M2; Fasteners = Grade 8.8.
- [ ] **Drafting standards:** First Angle Projection, IS 696, A2 sheet for assembly, A3 for part.
- [ ] **Hatching:** Cast Iron = 45° cross-hatch; Steel = 45° parallel hatch.
- [ ] **BOM must be present** on the assembly drawing.
- [ ] **Quill travel = 100 mm minimum**; quill diameter = 40 mm typical.
- [ ] **Quill rigidity formula:** $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right)$; $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{F L^3}{3EI}$.
- [ ] **Center height = 100 mm** (typical lab lathe) — must match headstock within 0.02 mm.
- [ ] **Two locks on a tail stock:** Body lock and Quill lock.
- [ ] **ACME thread on quill** for manual feed; pitch ≈ 2.5 mm.
- [ ] **Always fill the title block** with name, roll number, sheet scale, date.
- [ ] **Sectional view of the tail stock** must show Morse Taper joint and ACME thread engagement.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCMUL507 / KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Analysis of an Axial Bar with Varying Cross-Sectional Areas Under Axial Load Using FEA Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL507 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Structural Analysis of an Axial Bar with Varying Cross-Sectional Areas Under Axial Load Using FEA Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Structural Analysis of an Axial Bar with Varying Cross-Sectional Areas** is a fundamental one-dimensional (1D) Finite Element Analysis (FEA) problem in which a prismatic (or stepped) bar composed of multiple segments of different cross-sectional areas is subjected to an **axial tensile or compressive load** at one end while the opposite end is fully constrained (fixed boundary condition). The objective of the FEA simulation is to determine the **nodal displacements**, **element strains**, **element stresses (axial/normal stress)**, and **reaction forces** by discretizing the continuum into a finite number of linear spring-like elements and solving the global equilibrium equation $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F}$.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this experiment belongs to the family of **linear static structural problems** and serves as the foundational benchmark (the "Hello World" of FEA) because its analytical (closed-form) solution is well-known, allowing direct validation of the numerical FEA output.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Axial Bar** | A slender member whose length is significantly greater than its lateral dimensions, loaded only along its longitudinal axis. |
| **Varying Cross-Section** | A bar whose cross-sectional area $A(x)$ is a piecewise constant function of position $x$ (e.g., stepped bar with 2 or 3 segments). |
| **Axial Load** | A force vector collinear with the centroidal axis of the bar, producing only normal stress $\sigma_{xx}$. |
| **Young's Modulus ($E$)** | The slope of the linear-elastic portion of the stress-strain curve, measured in **N/m²** or **Pa**. |
| **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)** | The negative lateral-to-axial strain ratio under uniaxial loading. |
| **Linear Static Analysis** | Assumes small deformations, time-independent loading, and linear-elastic material behaviour. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tug-of-War Rope with Knots"**
> Imagine pulling a thick jute rope that has been spliced together from three segments of *different thicknesses* — a thin segment, a medium segment, and a thick segment — all joined end-to-end. When you pull one end with a force $P$ (like in a tug-of-war), the *same force* travels through every segment (this is **static equilibrium**), but the **stress** ($\sigma = P/A$) will be *highest* in the thinnest segment and *lowest* in the thickest segment. The **stretch** in each segment is proportional to its length divided by (Area × Young's Modulus). The total elongation is the sum of the individual segment elongations.
>
> FEA simply breaks this rope into many tiny "spring-segments," writes a stiffness equation for each, stitches them into a global matrix, applies the pull $P$ at one end and a wall (fixed support) at the other, and solves the resulting matrix equation to find how much each node moves.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

- **Design of tie-rods, columns, bolts, and shafts** in mechanical and civil engineering.
- Validation of mesh convergence (refining the mesh should reproduce the analytical answer).
- Foundation for thermal-stress, dynamic, and non-linear extensions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Differential Equation

For a 1D axial bar under static equilibrium, neglecting body forces, the strong form is:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA(x)\frac{du}{dx}\right) = 0$$

For each *constant-area* segment, this reduces to $\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = 0$, whose solution is a **linear displacement field**: $u(x) = c_1 x + c_2$.

## 2.2 The 1D Two-Node Bar Element (FEA Formulation)

A single bar element with two end nodes (Node $i$ at $x = 0$, Node $j$ at $x = L$) has the following steps:

### Step 1 — Shape (Interpolation) Functions
Using linear interpolation of nodal displacements $u_i$ and $u_j$:

$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \qquad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}$$

The displacement field within the element is:

$$u(x) = N_1 u_i + N_2 u_j$$

### Step 2 — Strain-Displacement Matrix (B-matrix)
The axial strain is constant within the element:

$$\varepsilon(x) = \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{u_j - u_i}{L}$$

In matrix form:

$$\varepsilon = \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}^e, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{1}{L} & \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Constitutive (Stress-Strain) Relation
For a linear-elastic, isotropic material:

$$\sigma = E \varepsilon \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sigma = \mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{u}^e, \qquad \mathbf{D} = [E]$$

### Step 4 — Element Stiffness Matrix
Using the principle of minimum potential energy (or the direct stiffness/Galerkin method):

$$\mathbf{k}^e = \int_0^L \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D}\, \mathbf{B}\, A\, dx = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Element Nodal Force Vector
For a uniformly distributed axial body force $b$ per unit length:

$$\mathbf{f}^e = \int_0^L \mathbf{N}^T b\, A\, dx = \frac{bAL}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

## 2.3 Assembly of the Global Stiffness Matrix

For a stepped bar discretized into $n$ elements and $n+1$ nodes, the **Global Stiffness Matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ is assembled by direct stiffness summation:

$$\mathbf{K} = \sum_{e=1}^{n} \mathbf{k}^e$$

For a 3-element stepped bar (4 nodes), the global matrix has the canonical **tridiagonal** form:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}
k_1 & -k_1 & 0 & 0 \\
-k_1 & k_1+k_2 & -k_2 & 0 \\
0 & -k_2 & k_2+k_3 & -k_3 \\
0 & 0 & -k_3 & k_3
\end{bmatrix}, \qquad k_e = \frac{A_e E_e}{L_e}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently tested in KTU viva and written exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{k}^e = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | 2-node bar element stiffness matrix | N/m |
| 2 | $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/L & 1/L \end{bmatrix}$ | Strain-displacement matrix (constant for linear bar) | 1/m |
| 3 | $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F}$ | Global equilibrium equation (linear system) | N, m, N |
| 4 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | 1D Hooke's law | Pa |
| 5 | $\sigma_e = E \cdot \frac{u_j - u_i}{L_e}$ | Element axial stress from nodal displacements | Pa |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_e = \frac{u_j - u_i}{L_e}$ | Element axial strain (dimensionless) | — |
| 7 | $u_{\text{total}} = \sum_{e} \frac{P L_e}{A_e E_e}$ | Analytical total elongation of stepped bar | m |
| 8 | $\sigma_e = \frac{P}{A_e}$ | Analytical axial stress in segment $e$ | Pa |
| 9 | $R_A = \sum F_{\text{applied}}$ | Reaction force at fixed end (static equilibrium) | N |
| 10 | $\delta_e = u_j - u_i$ | Element elongation (difference of nodal displacements) | m |

> **Note on notation:** $A_e$, $L_e$, $E_e$ refer to the *element-level* (or segment-level) area, length, and Young's modulus.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Design of truss members, tie-bars in pre-stressed concrete, anchor bolts. |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Connecting rods, pressure-vessel nozzle reinforcements, bolted flange studs. |
| **Aerospace** | Wing-spar caps, fuselage longerons, rocket motor casings. |
| **Biomedical** | Analysis of bone-implant interfaces (femoral stems). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: 3-Step Bar with Three Distinct Areas

### Problem Statement
A stepped steel bar is fixed at the left end (Node 1, $x=0$) and subjected to an axial tensile force $P = \mathbf{50\,kN}$ at the right end (Node 4). The geometry and material data are:

| Segment | Length $L_e$ (m) | Cross-Section $A_e$ (mm²) | Young's Modulus $E_e$ (GPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Nodes 1–2) | 0.10 | 200 | 200 |
| 2 (Nodes 2–3) | 0.15 | 100 | 200 |
| 3 (Nodes 3–4) | 0.20 | 300 | 200 |

**Required:** Solve the FEA system $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{U}=\mathbf{F}$ to find all nodal displacements, then compute element stresses, strains, and the reaction force. Validate against the analytical solution.

### Step A — Compute Element Stiffness Coefficients

Using the formula $k_e = \frac{A_e E_e}{L_e}$, with consistent SI units (A in m², E in Pa, L in m):

$$k_1 = \frac{(200 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^9)}{0.10} = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{0.10} = 4.0 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$

$$k_2 = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^9)}{0.15} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{0.15} = 1.333 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$

$$k_3 = \frac{(300 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^9)}{0.20} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{0.20} = 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$

### Step B — Assemble the Global Stiffness Matrix (4 × 4)

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}
4.0 & -4.0 & 0 & 0 \\
-4.0 & 4.0+1.333 & -1.333 & 0 \\
0 & -1.333 & 1.333+3.0 & -3.0 \\
0 & 0 & -3.0 & 3.0
\end{bmatrix} \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}$$

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}
4.0 & -4.0 & 0 & 0 \\
-4.0 & 5.333 & -1.333 & 0 \\
0 & -1.333 & 4.333 & -3.0 \\
0 & 0 & -3.0 & 3.0
\end{bmatrix} \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}$$

### Step C — Apply Boundary Conditions & Load Vector

- **Displacement BC:** $u_1 = 0$ (fixed end)
- **Force BC:** $F_4 = 50{,}000$ N (applied at free end)
- All other $F_i = 0$ (no body forces or intermediate loads)

$$\mathbf{F} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_2 \\ F_3 \\ F_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} R_1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 50{,}000 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

### Step D — Partition the System (Eliminate the Fixed DOF)

After eliminating row 1 / column 1 (since $u_1 = 0$ is known), the reduced system is:

$$\begin{bmatrix}
5.333 & -1.333 & 0 \\
-1.333 & 4.333 & -3.0 \\
0 & -3.0 & 3.0
\end{bmatrix} \times 10^{8} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \\ u_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 50{,}000 \end{Bmatrix}$$

### Step E — Solve the Reduced System (Back-Substitution)

Let us denote $a = 5.333$, $b = -1.333$, $c = 4.333$, $d = -3.0$, $e = 3.0$ (all $\times 10^8$). We solve:

$$a u_2 + b u_3 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; u_2 = -\frac{b}{a} u_3 = \frac{1.333}{5.333} u_3 = 0.250\, u_3$$

Substituting into row 2:

$$b(0.250 u_3) + c u_3 + d u_4 = 0$$
$$-0.3333\, u_3 + 4.333\, u_3 - 3.0\, u_4 = 0$$
$$4.0\, u_3 = 3.0\, u_4 \;\Longrightarrow\; u_3 = 0.75\, u_4$$

Substituting into row 3:

$$d(0.75 u_4) + e u_4 = \frac{50{,}000}{10^8}$$
$$-2.25\, u_4 + 3.0\, u_4 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$0.75\, u_4 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$u_4 = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = \mathbf{0.6667 \text{ mm}}$$

Back-substitute:

$$u_3 = 0.75 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = \mathbf{0.5000 \text{ mm}}$$

$$u_2 = 0.250 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = \mathbf{0.1250 \text{ mm}}$$

$$u_1 = 0 \text{ (prescribed)}$$

### Step F — Compute Element Strains

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L_1} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{0.10} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{u_3 - u_2}{L_2} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4} - 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{0.15} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon_3 = \frac{u_4 - u_3}{L_3} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4} - 5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.20} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step G — Compute Element Stresses ($\sigma_e = E_e \varepsilon_e$)

$$\sigma_1 = (200 \times 10^9)(1.25 \times 10^{-3}) = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = (200 \times 10^9)(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 500 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_3 = (200 \times 10^9)(8.33 \times 10^{-4}) = 166.7 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step H — Compute the Reaction Force $R_1$

From global equilibrium, row 1 of $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F}$:

$$R_1 = 4.0 \times 10^8 (u_1) - 4.0 \times 10^8 (u_2) + 0 = -4.0 \times 10^8 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$R_1 = -50{,}000 \text{ N}$$

The negative sign indicates the reaction acts opposite to the assumed positive $x$-direction, which is correct for a fixed left support resisting a rightward pull. The magnitude $|R_1| = 50$ kN matches the applied load — equilibrium is satisfied.

### Step I — Validate Against Analytical Solution

Analytical total elongation:

$$u_4^{\text{exact}} = \sum_{e=1}^{3} \frac{P L_e}{A_e E_e} = \frac{50{,}000}{200 \times 10^9}\left[\frac{0.10}{200 \times 10^{-6}} + \frac{0.15}{100 \times 10^{-6}} + \frac{0.20}{300 \times 10^{-6}}\right]$$

$$u_4^{\text{exact}} = (2.5 \times 10^{-7})\left[500 + 1500 + 666.67\right] = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

This matches the FEA result $u_4 = 0.6667$ mm **exactly** — confirming the correctness of the FEA model.

### Step J — Analytical Stress Cross-Check

$$\sigma_1^{\text{exact}} = \frac{50{,}000}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = 250 \text{ MPa} \;\checkmark$$

$$\sigma_2^{\text{exact}} = \frac{50{,}000}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 500 \text{ MPa} \;\checkmark$$

$$\sigma_3^{\text{exact}} = \frac{50{,}000}{300 \times 10^{-6}} = 166.67 \text{ MPa} \;\checkmark$$

All FEA values match the analytical solution — **mesh-independent for this 1D linear problem**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: FEA Workflow for Stepped Bar

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **pre-processing → solution → post-processing** workflow as executed inside a generic FEA package (ANSYS Workbench, Abaqus/CAE, or open-source equivalent such as CalculiX/Code_Aster with Gmsh).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Stepped Bar Problem Definition] --> B[PRE-PROCESSING PHASE]
    B --> B1[Step B1: Geometry Creation - Sketch 3 cylinders with distinct radii in series]
    B1 --> B2[Step B2: Material Assignment - Define E, nu for Steel]
    B2 --> B3[Step B3: Meshing - Linear 1D bar elements - 2 nodes per element]
    B3 --> B4[Step B4: Element Stiffness k_e = AE per L for each element]
    B4 --> B5[Step B5: Assembly into Global K matrix of size n+1 by n+1]
    B5 --> C[BOUNDARY CONDITION PHASE]
    C --> C1[Step C1: Fix Node 1 - u1 equals 0 - Dirichlet BC]
    C --> C2[Step C2: Apply Force P at Node 4 - Neumann BC]
    C2 --> D[SOLUTION PHASE]
    D --> D1[Step D1: Partition System - Eliminate fixed DOF]
    D1 --> D2[Step D2: Solve K reduced times U reduced equals F reduced]
    D2 --> D3[Step D3: Back-substitute to recover u1 equals 0]
    D3 --> E[POST-PROCESSING PHASE]
    E --> E1[Step E1: Nodal Displacements U vector]
    E --> E2[Step E2: Element Strains epsilon equals B times Ue]
    E --> E3[Step E3: Element Stresses sigma equals E times epsilon]
    E --> E4[Step E4: Reaction Force R equals K times U minus F]
    E1 --> F[VALIDATION: Compare with Analytical Solution]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[END: Tabulated FEA vs Analytical Results]
```

## 4.2 Stepped Bar Schematic — Node & Element Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node 1 - Fixed End - x equals 0]:::fixed
    E1[Element 1 - A1 L1 E1]:::elem
    N2[Node 2]:::node
    E2[Element 2 - A2 L2 E2]:::elem
    N3[Node 3]:::node
    E3[Element 3 - A3 L3 E3]:::elem
    N4[Node 4 - Free End - x equals L total]:::free
    P[Applied Axial Force P]:::load

    N1 --- E1 --- N2 --- E2 --- N3 --- E3 --- N4
    P -. Acts on .-> N4

    classDef fixed fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef free fill:#5f1f1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#3a5f1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef elem fill:#5f5f1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef load fill:#5f1f5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Data-Flow Diagram: From Element Matrices to Global Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph E1[Element 1 Inputs]
        E1A[A1 equals 200 mm²] --> E1K[k1 equals 4.0e8 N per m]
        E1L[L1 equals 0.10 m] --> E1K
        E1E[E1 equals 200 GPa] --> E1K
    end
    subgraph E2[Element 2 Inputs]
        E2A[A2 equals 100 mm²] --> E2K[k2 equals 1.333e8 N per m]
        E2L[L2 equals 0.15 m] --> E2K
        E2E[E2 equals 200 GPa] --> E2K
    end
    subgraph E3[Element 3 Inputs]
        E3A[A3 equals 300 mm²] --> E3K[k3 equals 3.0e8 N per m]
        E3L[L3 equals 0.20 m] --> E3K
        E3E[E3 equals 200 GPa] --> E3K
    end
    E1K --> G[Global K 4x4]
    E2K --> G
    E3K --> G
    G --> BC[Apply BC: u1 equals 0, F4 equals 50 kN]
    BC --> SOLVE[Solve K reduced times U equals F]
    SOLVE --> OUT[Output: u2, u3, u4, epsilons, sigmas, R1]
```
